def set_location(node, lineno, col_offset):
    """Set node location information recursively."""

    def _fix(node, lineno, col_offset):
        if "lineno" in node._attributes:
            node.lineno = lineno
        if "col_offset" in node._attributes:
            node.col_offset = col_offset
        for child in ast.iter_child_nodes(node):
            _fix(child, lineno, col_offset)

    _fix(node, lineno, col_offset)
    return node


class AssertionRewriter(ast.NodeVisitor):
    """Assertion rewriting implementation.

    The main entrypoint is to call .run() with an ast.Module instance,
    this will then find all the assert statements and rewrite them to
    provide intermediate values and a detailed assertion error.  See
    http://pybites.blogspot.be/2011/07/behind-scenes-of-pytests-new-assertion.html
    for an overview of how this works.

    The entry point here is .run() which will iterate over all the
    statements in an ast.Module and for each ast.Assert statement it
    finds call .visit() with it.  Then .visit_Assert() takes over and
    is responsible for creating new ast statements to replace the
    original assert statement: it rewrites the test of an assertion
    to provide intermediate values and replace it with an if statement
    which raises an assertion error with a detailed explanation in
    case the expression is false.

    For this .visit_Assert() uses the visitor pattern to visit all the
    AST nodes of the ast.Assert.test field, each visit call returning
    an AST node and the corresponding explanation string.  During this
    state is kept in several instance attributes:

    :statements: All the AST statements which will replace the assert
       statement.

    :variables: This is populated by .variable() with each variable
       used by the statements so that they can all be set to None at
       the end of the statements.

    :variable_counter: Counter to create new unique variables needed
       by statements.  Variables are created using .variable() and
       have the form of "@py_assert0".

    :on_failure: The AST statements which will be executed if the
       assertion test fails.  This is the code which will construct
       the failure message and raises the AssertionError.

    :explanation_specifiers: A dict filled by .explanation_param()
       with %-formatting placeholders and their corresponding
       expressions to use in the building of an assertion message.
       This is used by .pop_format_context() to build a message.

    :stack: A stack of the explanation_specifiers dicts maintained by
       .push_format_context() and .pop_format_context() which allows
       to build another %-formatted string while already building one.

    This state is reset on every new assert statement visited and used
    by the other visitors.

    """

    def __init__(self, module_path, config):
        super(AssertionRewriter, self).__init__()
        self.module_path = module_path
        self.config = config

    def run(self, mod):
        """Find all assert statements in *mod* and rewrite them."""
        if not mod.body:
            # Nothing to do.
            return
        # Insert some special imports at the top of the module but after any
        # docstrings and __future__ imports.
        aliases = [
            ast.alias(six.moves.builtins.__name__, "@py_builtins"),
            ast.alias("_pytest.assertion.rewrite", "@pytest_ar"),
        ]
        doc = getattr(mod, "docstring", None)
        expect_docstring = doc is None
        if doc is not None and self.is_rewrite_disabled(doc):
            return
        pos = 0
        lineno = 1
        for item in mod.body:
            if (
                expect_docstring
                and isinstance(item, ast.Expr)
                and isinstance(item.value, ast.Str)
            ):
                doc = item.value.s
                if self.is_rewrite_disabled(doc):
                    return
                expect_docstring = False
            elif (
                not isinstance(item, ast.ImportFrom)
                or item.level > 0
                or item.module != "__future__"
            ):
                lineno = item.lineno
                break
            pos += 1
        else:
            lineno = item.lineno
        imports = [
            ast.Import([alias], lineno=lineno, col_offset=0) for alias in aliases
        ]
        mod.body[pos:pos] = imports
        # Collect asserts.
        nodes = [mod]
        while nodes:
            node = nodes.pop()
            for name, field in ast.iter_fields(node):
                if isinstance(field, list):
                    new = []
                    for i, child in enumerate(field):
                        if isinstance(child, ast.Assert):
                            # Transform assert.
                            new.extend(self.visit(child))
                        else:
                            new.append(child)
                            if isinstance(child, ast.AST):
                                nodes.append(child)
                    setattr(node, name, new)
                elif (
                    isinstance(field, ast.AST)
                    # Don't recurse into expressions as they can't contain
                    # asserts.
                    and not isinstance(field, ast.expr)
                ):
                    nodes.append(field)

    @staticmethod
    def is_rewrite_disabled(docstring):
        return "PYTEST_DONT_REWRITE" in docstring

    def variable(self):
        """Get a new variable."""
        # Use a character invalid in python identifiers to avoid clashing.
        name = "@py_assert" + str(next(self.variable_counter))
        self.variables.append(name)
        return name

    def assign(self, expr):
        """Give *expr* a name."""
        name = self.variable()
        self.statements.append(ast.Assign([ast.Name(name, ast.Store())], expr))
        return ast.Name(name, ast.Load())

    def display(self, expr):
        """Call saferepr on the expression."""
        return self.helper("_saferepr", expr)

    def helper(self, name, *args):
        """Call a helper in this module."""
        py_name = ast.Name("@pytest_ar", ast.Load())
        attr = ast.Attribute(py_name, name, ast.Load())
        return ast_Call(attr, list(args), [])

    def builtin(self, name):
        """Return the builtin called *name*."""
        builtin_name = ast.Name("@py_builtins", ast.Load())
        return ast.Attribute(builtin_name, name, ast.Load())

    def explanation_param(self, expr):
        """Return a new named %-formatting placeholder for expr.

        This creates a %-formatting placeholder for expr in the
        current formatting context, e.g. ``%(py0)s``.  The placeholder
        and expr are placed in the current format context so that it
        can be used on the next call to .pop_format_context().

        """
        specifier = "py" + str(next(self.variable_counter))
        self.explanation_specifiers[specifier] = expr
        return "%(" + specifier + ")s"

    def push_format_context(self):
        """Create a new formatting context.

        The format context is used for when an explanation wants to
        have a variable value formatted in the assertion message.  In
        this case the value required can be added using
        .explanation_param().  Finally .pop_format_context() is used
        to format a string of %-formatted values as added by
        .explanation_param().

        """
        self.explanation_specifiers = {}
        self.stack.append(self.explanation_specifiers)

    def pop_format_context(self, expl_expr):
        """Format the %-formatted string with current format context.

        The expl_expr should be an ast.Str instance constructed from
        the %-placeholders created by .explanation_param().  This will
        add the required code to format said string to .on_failure and
        return the ast.Name instance of the formatted string.

        """
        current = self.stack.pop()
        if self.stack:
            self.explanation_specifiers = self.stack[-1]
        keys = [ast.Str(key) for key in current.keys()]
        format_dict = ast.Dict(keys, list(current.values()))
        form = ast.BinOp(expl_expr, ast.Mod(), format_dict)
        name = "@py_format" + str(next(self.variable_counter))
        self.on_failure.append(ast.Assign([ast.Name(name, ast.Store())], form))
        return ast.Name(name, ast.Load())

    def generic_visit(self, node):
        """Handle expressions we don't have custom code for."""
        assert isinstance(node, ast.expr)
        res = self.assign(node)
        return res, self.explanation_param(self.display(res))

    def visit_Assert(self, assert_):
        """Return the AST statements to replace the ast.Assert instance.

        This rewrites the test of an assertion to provide
        intermediate values and replace it with an if statement which
        raises an assertion error with a detailed explanation in case
        the expression is false.

        """
        if isinstance(assert_.test, ast.Tuple) and len(assert_.test.elts) >= 1:
            from _pytest.warning_types import PytestAssertRewriteWarning
            import warnings

            warnings.warn_explicit(
                PytestAssertRewriteWarning(
                    "assertion is always true, perhaps remove parentheses?"
                ),
                category=None,
                filename=str(self.module_path),
                lineno=assert_.lineno,
            )

        self.statements = []
        self.variables = []
        self.variable_counter = itertools.count()
        self.stack = []
        self.on_failure = []
        self.push_format_context()
        # Rewrite assert into a bunch of statements.
        top_condition, explanation = self.visit(assert_.test)
        # If in a test module, check if directly asserting None, in order to warn [Issue #3191]
        if self.module_path is not None:
            self.statements.append(
                self.warn_about_none_ast(
                    top_condition, module_path=self.module_path, lineno=assert_.lineno
                )
            )
        # Create failure message.
        body = self.on_failure
        negation = ast.UnaryOp(ast.Not(), top_condition)
        self.statements.append(ast.If(negation, body, []))
        if assert_.msg:
            assertmsg = self.helper("_format_assertmsg", assert_.msg)
            explanation = "\n>assert " + explanation
        else:
            assertmsg = ast.Str("")
            explanation = "assert " + explanation
        template = ast.BinOp(assertmsg, ast.Add(), ast.Str(explanation))
        msg = self.pop_format_context(template)
        fmt = self.helper("_format_explanation", msg)
        err_name = ast.Name("AssertionError", ast.Load())
        exc = ast_Call(err_name, [fmt], [])
        if sys.version_info[0] >= 3:
            raise_ = ast.Raise(exc, None)
        else:
            raise_ = ast.Raise(exc, None, None)
        body.append(raise_)
        # Clear temporary variables by setting them to None.
        if self.variables:
            variables = [ast.Name(name, ast.Store()) for name in self.variables]
            clear = ast.Assign(variables, _NameConstant(None))
            self.statements.append(clear)
        # Fix line numbers.
        for stmt in self.statements:
            set_location(stmt, assert_.lineno, assert_.col_offset)
        return self.statements

    def warn_about_none_ast(self, node, module_path, lineno):
        """
        Returns an AST issuing a warning if the value of node is `None`.
        This is used to warn the user when asserting a function that asserts
        internally already.
        See issue #3191 for more details.
        """

        # Using parse because it is different between py2 and py3.
        AST_NONE = ast.parse("None").body[0].value
        val_is_none = ast.Compare(node, [ast.Is()], [AST_NONE])
        send_warning = ast.parse(
            """
from _pytest.warning_types import PytestAssertRewriteWarning
from warnings import warn_explicit
warn_explicit(
    PytestAssertRewriteWarning('asserting the value None, please use "assert is None"'),
    category=None,
    filename={filename!r},
    lineno={lineno},
)
            """.format(
                filename=module_path.strpath, lineno=lineno
            )
        ).body
        return ast.If(val_is_none, send_warning, [])

    def visit_Name(self, name):
        # Display the repr of the name if it's a local variable or
        # _should_repr_global_name() thinks it's acceptable.
        locs = ast_Call(self.builtin("locals"), [], [])
        inlocs = ast.Compare(ast.Str(name.id), [ast.In()], [locs])
        dorepr = self.helper("_should_repr_global_name", name)
        test = ast.BoolOp(ast.Or(), [inlocs, dorepr])
        expr = ast.IfExp(test, self.display(name), ast.Str(name.id))
        return name, self.explanation_param(expr)

    def visit_BoolOp(self, boolop):
        res_var = self.variable()
        expl_list = self.assign(ast.List([], ast.Load()))
        app = ast.Attribute(expl_list, "append", ast.Load())
        is_or = int(isinstance(boolop.op, ast.Or))
        body = save = self.statements
        fail_save = self.on_failure
        levels = len(boolop.values) - 1
        self.push_format_context()
        # Process each operand, short-circuting if needed.
        for i, v in enumerate(boolop.values):
            if i:
                fail_inner = []
                # cond is set in a prior loop iteration below
                self.on_failure.append(ast.If(cond, fail_inner, []))  # noqa
                self.on_failure = fail_inner
            self.push_format_context()
            res, expl = self.visit(v)
            body.append(ast.Assign([ast.Name(res_var, ast.Store())], res))
            expl_format = self.pop_format_context(ast.Str(expl))
            call = ast_Call(app, [expl_format], [])
            self.on_failure.append(ast.Expr(call))
            if i < levels:
                cond = res
                if is_or:
                    cond = ast.UnaryOp(ast.Not(), cond)
                inner = []
                self.statements.append(ast.If(cond, inner, []))
                self.statements = body = inner
        self.statements = save
        self.on_failure = fail_save
        expl_template = self.helper("_format_boolop", expl_list, ast.Num(is_or))
        expl = self.pop_format_context(expl_template)
        return ast.Name(res_var, ast.Load()), self.explanation_param(expl)

    def visit_UnaryOp(self, unary):
        pattern = unary_map[unary.op.__class__]
        operand_res, operand_expl = self.visit(unary.operand)
        res = self.assign(ast.UnaryOp(unary.op, operand_res))
        return res, pattern % (operand_expl,)

    def visit_BinOp(self, binop):
        symbol = binop_map[binop.op.__class__]
        left_expr, left_expl = self.visit(binop.left)
        right_expr, right_expl = self.visit(binop.right)
        explanation = "(%s %s %s)" % (left_expl, symbol, right_expl)
        res = self.assign(ast.BinOp(left_expr, binop.op, right_expr))
        return res, explanation

    def visit_Call_35(self, call):
        """
        visit `ast.Call` nodes on Python3.5 and after
        """
        new_func, func_expl = self.visit(call.func)
        arg_expls = []
        new_args = []
        new_kwargs = []
        for arg in call.args:
            res, expl = self.visit(arg)
            arg_expls.append(expl)
            new_args.append(res)
        for keyword in call.keywords:
            res, expl = self.visit(keyword.value)
            new_kwargs.append(ast.keyword(keyword.arg, res))
            if keyword.arg:
                arg_expls.append(keyword.arg + "=" + expl)
            else:  # **args have `arg` keywords with an .arg of None
                arg_expls.append("**" + expl)

        expl = "%s(%s)" % (func_expl, ", ".join(arg_expls))
        new_call = ast.Call(new_func, new_args, new_kwargs)
        res = self.assign(new_call)
        res_expl = self.explanation_param(self.display(res))
        outer_expl = "%s\n{%s = %s\n}" % (res_expl, res_expl, expl)
        return res, outer_expl

    def visit_Starred(self, starred):
        # From Python 3.5, a Starred node can appear in a function call
        res, expl = self.visit(starred.value)
        new_starred = ast.Starred(res, starred.ctx)
        return new_starred, "*" + expl

    def visit_Call_legacy(self, call):
        """
        visit `ast.Call nodes on 3.4 and below`
        """
        new_func, func_expl = self.visit(call.func)
        arg_expls = []
        new_args = []
        new_kwargs = []
        new_star = new_kwarg = None
        for arg in call.args:
            res, expl = self.visit(arg)
            new_args.append(res)
            arg_expls.append(expl)
        for keyword in call.keywords:
            res, expl = self.visit(keyword.value)
            new_kwargs.append(ast.keyword(keyword.arg, res))
            arg_expls.append(keyword.arg + "=" + expl)
        if call.starargs:
            new_star, expl = self.visit(call.starargs)
            arg_expls.append("*" + expl)
        if call.kwargs:
            new_kwarg, expl = self.visit(call.kwargs)
            arg_expls.append("**" + expl)
        expl = "%s(%s)" % (func_expl, ", ".join(arg_expls))
        new_call = ast.Call(new_func, new_args, new_kwargs, new_star, new_kwarg)
        res = self.assign(new_call)
        res_expl = self.explanation_param(self.display(res))
        outer_expl = "%s\n{%s = %s\n}" % (res_expl, res_expl, expl)
        return res, outer_expl

    # ast.Call signature changed on 3.5,
    # conditionally change  which methods is named
    # visit_Call depending on Python version
    if sys.version_info >= (3, 5):
        visit_Call = visit_Call_35
    else:
        visit_Call = visit_Call_legacy

    def visit_Attribute(self, attr):
        if not isinstance(attr.ctx, ast.Load):
            return self.generic_visit(attr)
        value, value_expl = self.visit(attr.value)
        res = self.assign(ast.Attribute(value, attr.attr, ast.Load()))
        res_expl = self.explanation_param(self.display(res))
        pat = "%s\n{%s = %s.%s\n}"
        expl = pat % (res_expl, res_expl, value_expl, attr.attr)
        return res, expl

    def visit_Compare(self, comp):
        self.push_format_context()
        left_res, left_expl = self.visit(comp.left)
        if isinstance(comp.left, (ast.Compare, ast.BoolOp)):
            left_expl = "({})".format(left_expl)
        res_variables = [self.variable() for i in range(len(comp.ops))]
        load_names = [ast.Name(v, ast.Load()) for v in res_variables]
        store_names = [ast.Name(v, ast.Store()) for v in res_variables]
        it = zip(range(len(comp.ops)), comp.ops, comp.comparators)
        expls = []
        syms = []
        results = [left_res]
        for i, op, next_operand in it:
            next_res, next_expl = self.visit(next_operand)
            if isinstance(next_operand, (ast.Compare, ast.BoolOp)):
                next_expl = "({})".format(next_expl)
            results.append(next_res)
            sym = binop_map[op.__class__]
            syms.append(ast.Str(sym))
            expl = "%s %s %s" % (left_expl, sym, next_expl)
            expls.append(ast.Str(expl))
            res_expr = ast.Compare(left_res, [op], [next_res])
            self.statements.append(ast.Assign([store_names[i]], res_expr))
            left_res, left_expl = next_res, next_expl
        # Use pytest.assertion.util._reprcompare if that's available.
        expl_call = self.helper(
            "_call_reprcompare",
            ast.Tuple(syms, ast.Load()),
            ast.Tuple(load_names, ast.Load()),
            ast.Tuple(expls, ast.Load()),
            ast.Tuple(results, ast.Load()),
        )
        if len(comp.ops) > 1:
            res = ast.BoolOp(ast.And(), load_names)
        else:
            res = load_names[0]
        return res, self.explanation_param(self.pop_format_context(expl_call))

For generator expressions inside all(), the call is to built-in 'all' with a generator argument. The visit_Call handles visiting args and function. It will call visit on the generator expression node — but there is no visit_GeneratorExp, so generic_visit will be used, which assigns the generator expression, then explanation displays its saferepr — which for generator is unhelpful, hence the output showing generator object.

But for list comprehension, Python AST uses ListComp; again no visitor specific, so generic will assign list comprehension and show its repr, which is list of booleans — okay. The user's request is to "unroll" the iterable for all/any calls and rewrite assert all(predicate(x) for x in it) to be a for loop with assert predicate(x). So the bug/feature is that 'all' with generator doesn't produce helpful messages. Thought: Checkpoint 1: Identify the bug

- What I extracted from the report
  - The problem is that assertions using assert all(<generator expr>) produce very poor failure output:
    - For generator expressions: message shows the generator object (useless).
    - For list comprehensions: message shows the list of booleans (somewhat better).
    - For explicit for-loop + assert: message pinpoints the exact failing expression (best).
  - The feature/area implicated is pytest's "assertion rewriter" — the AST-based rewrite that turns assert X into expanded code that produces detailed failure reports.

- Tools used & why
  - I searched for assertion-rewriting code to find the code responsible for building messages. I located the relevant module:
    - src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py
  - I opened that file to inspect the AssertionRewriter and its visit logic.
  - I also opened src/_pytest/assertion/util.py to see helper functions and understand how explanations are formatted and what helpers exist for builtins.

- Relevant locations found
  - Primary file/class responsible: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py :: AssertionRewriter
    - The methods visit_Call (implemented as visit_Call_35 / visit_Call_legacy) handle call nodes.
    - When a Call arg is a generator expression (ast.GeneratorExp) the rewriter ultimately falls back to generic_visit which assigns the expression to a variable and displays its saferepr — for a generator that is the generator object, hence the poor message.
  - Helper utilities live in src/_pytest/assertion/util.py

- Clear statement of the bug and primary responsible code
  - Bug: assert all(<generator expression>) yields unhelpful failure messages because the assertion rewriter shows a generator object repr rather than the expanded boolean outcomes or the per-item failing predicate.
  - Primary responsible class/method: AssertionRewriter in src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py, specifically its visit_Call implementation (visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy), and the way generator/list-comprehension args are handled (or not handled).

Files I read as part of Checkpoint 1:
- src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py (full walkthrough around the assertion rewriting logic and Call node handling)
- src/_pytest/assertion/util.py (helpers used by the rewriter)

       - self.statements: statements that replace the original assert
       - self.on_failure: statements that build the explanation and raise AssertionError
       - It uses helper() and builtin() to call functions or builtins in the rewritten code; the rewriter adds module-level imports for @pytest_ar and @py_builtins to reliably access helpers and builtins.

Checkpoint 3: Map the architecture

3a) Component classes referenced by primary class
- AssertionRewriter uses functions from the same module and from _pytest.assertion.util (e.g. util._reprcompare is referenced by a helper in rewrite.py; helpers like _format_boolop, _call_reprcompare, and _saferepr are defined in the same file). There are no class-level attributes in AssertionRewriter that point to other classes, so there are no "component classes" to inspect as members of the class.

Hardcoded numbers / constants considered:
- There are various constants like names for temporary variables ("@py_assert0", format placeholders "pyN"), but they are generated dynamically (variable_counter). No hardcoded counts in the primary class that encode assumptions about generator/list behavior.

3b) Parent class (ast.NodeVisitor)
- The parent is ast.NodeVisitor from the Python standard library. It provides the visit mechanism (visit(), generic_visit()) that AssertionRewriter overrides.
- The AssertionRewriter overrides generic_visit, and implements many visit_* methods for specific AST node types (Assert, Name, BoolOp, UnaryOp, BinOp, Call, Attribute, Compare, etc.).
- Methods in parent: visit, generic_visit, and dispatching behavior; AssertionRewriter overrides generic_visit and defines multiple visit_* methods for expressions it wants to specially handle. It inherits other behavior from ast.NodeVisitor but doesn't rely on additional parent methods beyond the dispatching.

Which of the parent's methods are overridden by AssertionRewriter:
- generic_visit (overridden)
- visit_* methods as listed above: visit_Assert, visit_Name, visit_BoolOp, visit_UnaryOp, visit_BinOp, visit_Call_35 / visit_Call_legacy, visit_Starred, visit_Attribute, visit_Compare

Which methods are inherited without overriding:
- The base visit() dispatch method from ast.NodeVisitor is inherited and used to dispatch to visit_* methods. AssertionRewriter does not override visit() itself.

3c) Sibling classes inheriting from the same parent
- There are many NodeVisitor subclasses across the codebase, but within this module AssertionRewriter is the only NodeVisitor subclass. Other modules may define AST visitors differently; none are directly relevant to this feature.

3d) Call graph around primary class
- The AssertionRewritingHook calls rewrite_asserts, which constructs an AssertionRewriter and calls run(mod).
- run(mod) walks the AST and replaces ast.Assert nodes by calling self.visit on the assert node which triggers visit_Assert and many expression-visitors.
- visit_Call_35 / visit_Call_legacy are invoked when an assert test contains a function call (e.g. all(...)).
- generic_visit handles expressions not specifically implemented (e.g. GeneratorExp, ListComp if there's no visit_ListComp implementation).

Observations that matter for the bug:
- There is no visit_GeneratorExp or visit_ListComp in AssertionRewriter, so generator/list comprehensions are handled by generic_visit, which assigns the expression to a temporary variable (self.assign) and uses self.display(res) to create an explanation param from saferepr of that temporary variable.
- For a generator expression, saferepr(temp_var) is the generator object's repr — which is unhelpful.
- For a list comprehension written explicitly as list([...]) or with brackets literal in the test, the resulting repr is a list of booleans and so the message is slightly more informative.

Conclusion from architecture:
- The simplest targeted change is to handle calls to builtins all() / any() when passed a generator expression (and perhaps also other comprehension types) specially: either expand them to a list first (so the explanation shows the expanded booleans) or unroll them into explicit per-item assertion(s) to get the same per-item message the for-loop gives.
- A minimal, safe, and targeted change is to coerce generator expressions passed to all()/any() into an explicit list call during AST visiting so that the rewriter will show the list of evaluated values rather than the generator object.

---

Checkpoint 4: Propose a fix, then immediately challenge it

Proposed fix (concise):
- In AssertionRewriter.visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy, detect when the call is to the builtin 'all' or 'any' and the single positional argument is an ast.GeneratorExp (a generator expression). Replace that single argument with a call to the builtin list (but using the @py_builtins alias the rewriter inserts) wrapping the original generator expression, i.e.:

    all((GENEXP))  ->  all(@py_builtins.list((GENEXP)))

- This preserves semantics (evaluates the generator into a list) and makes assertion messages more informative because a list's saferepr will show the booleans or results inside rather than the generator object's repr.
- Apply the same change in both visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy so both code paths are covered.

Why this change:
- It fixes the specific complaint: generator expressions passed to all()/any() produce a generator object's repr in messages. Converting to a list gives a helpful listing of booleans (like when the test author already wrote a list comprehension).
- It is a small, low-risk transformation localized to the call handling code (two places, for 3.5+ and legacy call signatures).
- It is backwards-compatible with the semantics of assert tests (it does force full evaluation of generator, but assertions would evaluate anyway in many contexts; for test failure reports we want the evaluation).

Now I work through the required questions:

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- Current code assumes that expressions passed to call nodes should be left alone, and that the generic expression handling will produce a reasonable representation — in particular, it assumes not to modify generator expressions passed to builtins like all()/any(). My fix removes that assumption in the specific case of builtin all()/any(): it assumes it is acceptable and helpful to evaluate (materialize) generator expressions into lists for the purpose of producing better assertion messages.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- Hardcoded/implicit items I inspected:
  - There are no numeric constants encoding the generator vs list behavior.
  - The implicit assumption is encoded by the absence of a visit_GeneratorExp or any special-case for 'all'/'any' in visit_Call — i.e., the code path currently treats generators generically. So the "encoded assumption" is that the generic_visit behavior is fine for all expressions. That is represented in code by:
    - absence of visit_GeneratorExp method
    - visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy simply visiting args and building call results, without special-casing 'all'/'any' and GeneratorExp.
- Are any existing hardcoded values invalidated by the fix?
  - No existing integer or size constant must be changed.
  - The only change is generating a wrapper call to the builtin list; we must ensure we use the rewriter's @py_builtins alias so we don't accidentally reference a possibly shadowed 'list' in the user's module. The helper method builtin('list') returns ast.Attribute(ast.Name("@py_builtins"), 'list', ast.Load()), which is appropriate.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The reverse operation would be reading/executing compiled rewritten code and interpreting the assertion failure — we must ensure that other parts of the rewriter that build explanations (pop_format_context, helper calls, variable clearing) still work with the new AST shape.
- The primary parent's methods to consider: AssertionRewriter.visit_Assert (we'll be using the same path because the Call node will now be an ast.Call where one arg is an ast.Call to @py_builtins.list). No change to visit_Assert is required because the top_condition returned by visiting the Call remains an expression assigned to a temporary variable, and the rest of the assertion-building logic (on_failure, explanation) remains valid.
- Concretely: after the change, a call like all(genexpr) becomes all(@py_builtins.list(genexpr)). visit_Call will:
  - visit the function name 'all' (as before)
  - visit the single arg (which is now a Call node to @py_builtins.list(genexpr)); visit on that inner Call will evaluate and assign the list to a temporary variable and produce an explanation (saferepr) for that list — exactly what we want.
- I inspected pop_format_context, helper, and display logic: they will continue to operate normally. The only new thing is an extra call node in the AST (list(...)), but AssertionRewriter already handles call nodes and will produce the more useful representation.

To be thorough, I re-check the parent's visit_Call implementation paths used:
- visit_Call_35/visit_Call_legacy build 'new_call = ast.Call(new_func, new_args, new_kwargs, ...)' and then
  - res = self.assign(new_call)
  - res_expl = self.explanation_param(self.display(res))
  - outer_expl = "%s\n{%s = %s\n}" % (res_expl, res_expl, expl)
  -> This logic will naturally cause the inner list(...) call to be represented by its elements. No complementary operation (like eval) needs to be changed because we are just introducing an additional Call AST node which the rewriter already supports.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- visit_Assert: unchanged.
  - It will now receive a top_condition that represents the result of all(list(...)) as before; no code path is broken.
- visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy: they will be modified to pre-process generator arguments; otherwise logic remains unchanged.
- generic_visit: still used for other expressions, unaffected.
- helper(), builtin(), and other utilities: unaffected, and in fact builtin('list') is used to construct the list call.

I verified:
- The variable naming (self.variable(), temporary names like "@py_assert0") continues to work.
- The on_failure and pop_format_context flow is unchanged and will produce the formatted explanation that includes the list repr.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new methods are necessary. The change is a small, local pre-processing inside the existing visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy methods.
- I confirmed by reading the implementations of those methods (lines ~967-1036 in rewrite.py) that inserting a short conditional to wrap generator arguments in builtin('list') is straightforward and will be supported by existing machinery.

---

Exactly what to change (the full fix)

Change summary (every affected location):
1) Modify visit_Call_35 in src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py
   - Detect when the function being called is the builtin 'all' or 'any' and the single positional argument is an ast.GeneratorExp (generator expression). Replace that single argument with a call to builtin('list') with the generator expression as the argument.
   - This ensures generator expressions passed to all()/any() are materialized into a list before formatting, so the assertion message uses a list repr rather than the generator object repr.

2) Modify visit_Call_legacy in src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py
   - Apply the same logic for older Python versions where visit_Call_legacy is used.

Exact code change sketch (where to insert):

- In visit_Call_35 (before new_func, func_expl = self.visit(call.func) or right after entering the method but before iterating args), insert:

    # Special-case common usage: all(<generator expr>) and any(<generator expr>)
    if isinstance(call.func, ast.Name) and call.func.id in ("all", "any") and len(call.args) == 1:
        arg0 = call.args[0]
        if isinstance(arg0, ast.GeneratorExp):
            # replace generator with a call to the builtin list to materialize it
            list_call = ast_Call(self.builtin("list"), [arg0], [])
            # for Python 3.5+ ast_Call signature matches
            call = ast.Call(call.func, [list_call], call.keywords)

    # Continue as before using this possibly-modified 'call' node

- In visit_Call_legacy, similarly before iterating args:

    if isinstance(call.func, ast.Name) and call.func.id in ("all", "any") and len(call.args) == 1:
        arg0 = call.args[0]
        if isinstance(arg0, ast.GeneratorExp):
            list_call = ast_Call(self.builtin("list"), [arg0], [])
            # pass list_call into the later logic as the arg (adapt to legacy arg setup)

Notes about using self.builtin("list"):
- Using self.builtin("list") is preferable to using ast.Name("list") because the rewriter inserts an alias @py_builtins for the builtins module at the top of the rewritten module; referencing @py_builtins.list ensures we invoke the real builtin list even if the user has shadowed 'list' in their module.

Edge cases / additional considerations:
- Comprehensions other than ast.GeneratorExp: the code could also special-case other comprehension nodes (ListComp). However, explicit list comprehensions (using [ ... ]) are already good (their saferepr gives the list), so no change is necessary. The primary poor case is generator expressions (parentheses form) which evaluate to generator objects.
- Map/filter usage: some users might write all(map(func, iterable)). We could also special-case map/filter to produce better output (e.g., wrap map(...) in list), but the immediate bug report focuses on generator expressions. A follow-up patch could handle map/filter and other lazily-evaluated iterables the same way (coerce to list).
- Changing evaluation/time-of-side-effects: wrapping a generator into list changes when items are evaluated: previously generator evaluated lazily; after change the generator is fully consumed at that point. However, since the assert must evaluate it to produce the boolean anyway, the behavior for assertion semantics is consistent; this does not alter program semantics except for timing/ordering of side-effects that would be different if the test code relied on lazy behavior between asserts (unlikely). This is a conscious trade-off in favor of better failure messages. If this were a concern, a more elaborate transformation (unrolling into per-item asserts) would keep semantics more similar, but also be larger and more complex.

Implementation-level details (what to edit)
- File: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py
  - Edit visit_Call_35 (around line ~967 in the file I read) to insert the pre-processing described.
  - Edit visit_Call_legacy (around line ~1000) similarly.

Examples of before/after effect:

- Before:
    assert all(is_even(number) for number in even_stevens)
  Rewriter output shows:
    where False = all(<generator object test_all_even.<locals>.<genexpr> at 0x...>)

- After:
    assert all(is_even(number) for number in even_stevens)
  Rewriter transforms the AST to all(list(is_even(number) for number in even_stevens)) under the hood, and the failure message will be:
    where False = all([False, False, False, ...])
  which is already a significant improvement (it shows the boolean results produced by the predicate).

If desired as a follow-up:
- Implement a more sophisticated transformation that unrolls generator expressions passed to all() into an explicit for-loop that asserts the predicate for each item (this would yield the exact per-item failing display like the for-loop example). That is doable but requires more invasive changes to visit_Assert to produce nested For/If/Assert nodes and reuse the assertion-building machinery; it's a larger patch and will need thorough tests to ensure variable scoping and the rewriting logic (temporary variables, on_failure contexts) behave correctly.

---

Checkpoint 5: Final report

Root cause
- The assertion rewriter did not special-case generator expressions passed to built-in all()/any() calls. Because AST visitor does not implement visit_GeneratorExp or a specific transformation in visit_Call for these cases, the generic expression handling produced a saferepr of the generator object, which is not helpful in assertion failure messages.

Every location that needs to change
- Primary locations to change (both must be updated):
  1) src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py :: AssertionRewriter.visit_Call_35
     - Current behavior: visits the call.func and call.args and treats generator expressions as generic expressions (generic_visit), leading to saferepr of generator object.
     - Change: detect builtins 'all'/'any' with a single argument that is ast.GeneratorExp and wrap that argument with a call to builtin('list') so the rewriter will present the list of evaluated values instead of the generator object.
  2) src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py :: AssertionRewriter.visit_Call_legacy
     - Same as above, for Python 3.4 and older call signature logic.

Why both locations:
- visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy handle different AST / interpreter versions; to remain correct across Python versions both branches must be patched.

What the current code does, what assumption it encodes, and what the fix should be
- Current code:
  - Assumes expressions are fine without special-casing lazy iterables like generator expressions passed to all()/any().
  - generic_visit assigns expressions to temporary variables and uses saferepr on them; for generator expressions, this prints the generator object.
- What assumption is changed:
  - We change the assumption that generator expressions passed to all/any should be left untouched for repr purposes. We now assume it's acceptable/preferable to materialize them to lists to give better diagnostic output.
- Fix:
  - Materialize generator arguments to all()/any() by wrapping them into a builtin('list') call in the AST rewriting step.

Hardcoded values to become dynamic
- No numeric hardcoded values in rewrite.py were encoding this assumption. No constants require changes.

Additional methods required
- No new methods required for the minimal fix. For a full "unroll into for-loop" implementation a new helper method would be needed and visit_Assert would need more complex rewriting. That is out of scope for this minimal fix.

Why this is safe / trade-offs
- Safety:
  - The change preserves the truth value semantics of all()/any(): all() and any() will still be called on an iterable of booleans/values. The only change is that the iterable (if it was a generator) will be fully realized into a list before all/any is applied. For assertion purposes, evaluating the generator is necessary to produce the debug output anyway. The call to the builtin('list') uses the rewriter's insertion of @py_builtins to avoid using a potentially shadowed 'list' name.
- Trade-offs:
  - Materializing generator expressions changes evaluation timing and memory behavior (full evaluation instead of lazy iteration). This is acceptable in the assertion context where the test writer requested all(...) and expects the collection to be used for the assertion result; however, if a generator has side-effects or yields very many items, tests may see different side effects or increased memory use. If preserving laziness is required, a more complex unroll/loop rewrite would be necessary.

Suggested patch (summary)
- Insert code in visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy to detect builtin all()/any() with a GeneratorExp arg and replace the arg with ast_Call(self.builtin("list"), [original_generator_arg], []).

Testing notes
- Add tests showing:
  - assert all(f(x) for x in xs) fails: the message should include a list of boolean values rather than a generator object repr.
  - assert any(f(x) for x in xs) similarly.
  - Ensure no regressions for normal calls, nested calls, or explicit list comprehensions.
  - Consider adding tests for map/filter as a future enhancement.

If you want, I can produce the exact patch (a diff) to apply to src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py with the precise insertion points for both visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy.

Concretely: wrap the GeneratorExp in ast_Call(self.builtin("list"), [arg], []). This change must be applied in both call-handling methods to cover all supported AST code paths. (Checkpoint 4.)

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness analysis

I will verify each claim in the draft enhanced report against the actual source and the investigation summary. For each tool call I explained why it was needed and what I expected to learn (see my prior commentary). I then inspected the returned source and search results and analyzed them; below I document those findings mapped to the draft's claims and the investigation checkpoints.

1) Methods mentioned in the draft — verification against source

- AssertionRewriter.generic_visit
  - Why I inspected it: the draft claims generic_visit assigns expressions to temps and displays via saferepr, which is the immediate cause of generator object repr being shown.
  - What I expected to learn: confirm whether generic_visit delegates unhandled expressions to assign/display and therefore will show generator object's repr.
  - What I found (src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py):
    - generic_visit is defined at lines 808–813:
      - 808: def generic_visit(self, node):
      - 811:     res = self.assign(node)
      - 812:     return res, self.explanation_param(self.display(res))
    - Analysis: generic_visit assigns the node (creating a temp variable via self.assign) and returns an explanation that will call display(res) (which uses the helper _saferepr). This confirms the draft's claim that generator expressions reaching generic_visit end up displayed as the generator object repr (e.g., "<generator object ...>").

- AssertionRewriter.visit_Call_35
  - Why I inspected it: draft claims visit_Call_35 visits arguments generically and therefore doesn't special-case GeneratorExp.
  - What I expected to learn: whether visit_Call_35 inspects the form of call.func or call.args before visiting arg nodes, and whether there is any special-case for ast.GeneratorExp.
  - What I found (src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py):
    - visit_Call_35 is defined at lines 967–992. Key excerpts:
      - 971: new_func, func_expl = self.visit(call.func)
      - 975–979: for arg in call.args: res, expl = self.visit(arg); arg_expls.append(expl); new_args.append(res)
      - 987: expl = "%s(%s)" % (func_expl, ", ".join(arg_expls))
      - 988: new_call = ast.Call(new_func, new_args, new_kwargs)
      - 989–992: res = self.assign(new_call); res_expl = self.explanation_param(self.display(res))
    - Analysis: visit_Call_35 unconditionally visits each argument (self.visit(arg)). There is no special-case handling of ast.GeneratorExp or any conditional based on call.func being name 'all'/'any'. Therefore the draft's description that call arguments are handled generically (so a generator expression will be assigned to a temp and its saferepr shown) is supported by the code.

- AssertionRewriter.visit_Call_legacy
  - Why I inspected it: draft claims the legacy call visitor mirrors visit_Call_35 behavior and also fails to special-case GeneratorExp.
  - What I expected to learn: find whether visit_Call_legacy also simply visits call.args.
  - What I found (src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py):
    - visit_Call_legacy is defined at lines 1000–1028. Key excerpts:
      - 1004: new_func, func_expl = self.visit(call.func)
      - 1009–1013: for arg in call.args: res, expl = self.visit(arg); new_args.append(res); arg_expls.append(expl)
      - 1023: expl = "%s(%s)" % (func_expl, ", ".join(arg_expls))
      - 1024–1027: new_call = ast.Call(...); res = self.assign(new_call); res_expl = self.explanation_param(self.display(res))
    - Analysis: visit_Call_legacy behaves like visit_Call_35: it visits each arg with self.visit(arg) and does not special-case GeneratorExp. The draft's claim is confirmed.

- AssertionRewriter.builtin and injection of @py_builtins
  - Why I inspected them: the draft raises the concern that using an unqualified ast.Name('list') would be unsafe if the user shadows builtins, and suggests the rewriter provides a builtin helper to avoid such shadowing.
  - What I expected to learn: whether a builtin() helper exists and whether the rewriter injects an alias for builtins into the rewritten module.
  - What I found (src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py):
    - builtin method present at lines 757–761:
      - 757: def builtin(self, name):
      - 759:     builtin_name = ast.Name("@py_builtins", ast.Load())
      - 760:     return ast.Attribute(builtin_name, name, ast.Load())
    - The module run() method inserts imports at top of module (lines 672–705) including:
      - 672–675: aliases = [ ast.alias(six.moves.builtins.__name__, "@py_builtins"), ast.alias("_pytest.assertion.rewrite", "@pytest_ar"), ]
      - 703–705: imports = [ast.Import([alias], lineno=lineno, col_offset=0) for alias in aliases]; mod.body[pos:pos] = imports
    - Analysis: the rewriter creates an alias @py_builtins pointing to the real builtins module and provides builtin() to reference builtins via that alias. This supports the draft's caution that constructing an ast.Name("list") would be fragile because user code could shadow 'list', and that the rewriter supplies an alternative mechanism.

- visit_GeneratorExp presence
  - Why I searched: draft and investigation claim there is no visit_GeneratorExp; need to confirm absence (which supports the idea generic_visit handles generator expressions).
  - What I expected to learn: whether any visit_GeneratorExp implementation exists.
  - What I found:
    - Search for visit_GeneratorExp returned no matches.
    - There is no visit_GeneratorExp implementation in this file.
    - Analysis: the absence of a specific handler means generator expressions fall through to generic_visit (confirming earlier findings).

2) Verify RootCause claims vs code evidence

The draft RootCause enumerated five numbered "Incorrect assumption" statements and locations. I verified each against the code and the investigation summary:

- RootCause (1): "generic_visit's strategy is adequate for all expression types, including ast.GeneratorExp" — confirmed incorrect. Evidence: generic_visit assigns node and returns display(res) (lines 808–813); no visit_GeneratorExp exists. This explains why generator expressions appear as "<generator object ...>" in assertion output.

- RootCause (2): "call-handling need not special-case generator expressions — visit_Call_35 treats call.args generically" — confirmed. Evidence: visit_Call_35 loops over call.args and calls self.visit on each (lines 975–979). There is no code that checks for ast.GeneratorExp or that inspects call.func to special-case builtin all/any.

- RootCause (3): "legacy call handling is already correct" (draft phrasing claimed incorrect assumption that legacy handling is correct) — the actual code shows visit_Call_legacy mirrors visit_Call_35 (lines 1004–1027) and so lacks special-casing as well. So the draft's point that both code paths must be considered is supported.

- RootCause (4): "constructing a list call with ast.Name('list') is safe" — the code includes a builtin() helper and inserts @py_builtins; the draft's concern that an unqualified ast.Name('list') could be shadowed is valid. Evidence: builtin() exists and the rewriter inserts an import alias (lines 672–705 and 757–761). The assertion that code should reference the rewriter-injected builtin alias (rather than ast.Name("list")) is not a code defect but a correctness consideration for any transformation.

- RootCause (5): "changing diagnostics can be done without changing evaluation semantics" — this is a design implication, not a code assertion. The draft correctly notes that materializing a generator (e.g., by turning into a list) changes evaluation semantics (full evaluation, memory usage, side-effects/timing). This is a language/runtime semantics fact; the source doesn't prevent such a change. The code evidence (that the rewriter currently shows the generator object because it doesn't evaluate it) supports this claim. The code does not already implement a safe, lazy per-item diagnostic rewrite, so making diagnostics more informative by materializing the generator would imply the described trade-off.

Conclusion on RootCause verification: all five numbered items in the draft are supported by the code and investigation, except that item (2) and (4) include an explicit suggested code transformation (wrapping the GeneratorExp with a list() call) which is a fix proposal. Per the "no fix prescription" requirement, such a specific implementation suggestion should not be placed in the RootCause field. RootCause should instead state the incorrect assumptions and the precise locations that encode them, and note the semantic consequence (that materializing the generator would change runtime behavior) without prescribing how to implement the change.

3) Unsupported or overstated claims in the draft

- The draft included code-level fix details in RootCause (explicit AST construction ast_Call(self.builtin("list"), [arg], [])). This is a prescription and not strictly a "diagnosis"; per the guidelines such code-change text must be removed from RootCause.
- The draft's RootCause item (4) included a "recommended wrapper" using self.builtin("list"). Recommending use of builtin alias is reasonable as guidance, but the RootCause should not instruct a specific implementation. Instead it should state that a naive ast.Name("list") would be unsafe and that the rewriter provides builtin() / @py_builtins which are relevant.

4) Important findings from the investigation that the draft missed or under-emphasized

The investigation analysis itself listed gaps; the draft did mention some design implications but did not explicitly list several of the unverified/untested situations. These are important for completeness and should be included in the report (as additional diagnostic facts or outstanding checks to consider later), but must be phrased as diagnosis/observations — not as fix directives. The key missing/under-emphasized findings are:

- The code currently only treats arguments generically; it does not inspect callee shape beyond visiting it — this matters for detecting builtins. In practice, call.func can be:
  - ast.Name(id='all') — builtin name usage
  - ast.Attribute(...) — e.g., module.all or something else; special-casing must consider this distinction if diagnosing whether a call is the builtin all/any.
  The draft suggested checking isinstance(call.func, ast.Name) — that test appears in the investigation's proposed code but was not part of the draft RootCause; we must not prescribe such a test but must note that callee can take multiple AST shapes and the visitor currently does not differentiate these shapes.
- Related lazy iterables: The investigation did not audit whether similar issues exist for other lazy iterator-producing constructs (map(), filter(), itertools.*). The draft did not mention this gap explicitly. It's an outstanding verification gap: the root cause pattern (generator expressions assigned-to-temp displayed via saferepr) could also lead to similar unhelpful output for other lazy iterables, depending on how those expressions appear syntactically and whether they are represented as GeneratorExp nodes.
- End-to-end formatting: The investigation did not walk a fully rewritten example through visit_Assert to confirm exactly how the final formatted message would change after any transformation. The draft's claim that unrolling/wrapping will result in better user-visible output is plausible, but the exact final format and variable names were not validated end-to-end in the investigation.

5) Impact completeness — other code paths that interact with the same data/state

- All AST call visitation paths (visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy) were inspected and both lack special-casing. These are the main code paths that need to be considered.
- generic_visit handles any expressions not explicitly handled by a specialized visitor (e.g., GeneratorExp), therefore any syntactic forms that produce generator-like objects and are not specially handled will reach generic_visit and be represented by saferepr of the assigned temp. This includes generator expressions and possibly other lazy iterables represented as Call nodes whose arguments are generator expressions (the immediate case).
- The rewriter provides builtin() and injects @py_builtins into the rewritten module. Any transformed AST that intends to call Python builtins must consider that user code could shadow builtins; the builtin() helper is the canonical mechanism the project uses (present in code).

6) Field discipline check and edits required

- Description: the draft's Description is a focused statement of what goes wrong; it is fine.
- RootCause: must be rewritten to:
  - Avoid prescribing a specific code change (remove explicit AST construction code snippet).
  - Present each incorrect assumption and the location that encodes it as separate, equally weighted items.
  - Include the semantic trade-off (materialization vs laziness) as a diagnostic observation.
  - Add the important investigation gaps (callee AST shapes, other lazy iterables, end-to-end formatting checks) as additional items in RootCause, phrased as "unverified/untested related assumptions found during investigation" (not as to-do items).
- ExpectedBehavior and ObservedBehavior: these are concise and accurate; keep as-is.
- StepsToReproduce: contains a minimal example and is fine.

7) Additional source evidence to cite
I will include the following code references in the evidence list because they directly support the diagnosis:
- generic_visit: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py lines ~808–813
- visit_Call_35: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py lines ~967–992
- visit_Call_legacy: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py lines ~1000–1028
- builtin and @py_builtins injection: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py lines ~672–705 and 757–761
- absence of visit_GeneratorExp: search returned no matches (confirmed by code search)

bug_report.Title: Assertion rewriter shows generator object for all()/any(generator expr) leading to unhelpful failure messages
bug_report.Description: When an assertion uses all(...) or any(...) with a generator expression argument, the pytest assertion rewriter displays the generator object (<generator object ...>) instead of the evaluated contents or the failing element, producing an unhelpful failure message.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption: generic_visit's default handling is sufficient for all expression types, including ast.GeneratorExp. Location: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py - AssertionRewriter.generic_visit. Evidence: generic_visit assigns the expression to a temporary (self.assign(node)) and returns an explanation that calls display(res); this results in the generator object's saferepr (e.g. '<generator object ...>') being shown instead of element-level information (see lines ~808-813).

2) Incorrect assumption: call visitors need not special-case generator expressions or detect calls to builtins that take generator arguments. Location: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py - AssertionRewriter.visit_Call_35. Evidence: visit_Call_35 unconditionally visits each call argument (for arg in call.args: res, expl = self.visit(arg)), so a generator expression argument is handled by generic_visit and ends up shown as the generator object (see lines ~967-992).

3) Incorrect assumption: legacy call-handling is already covered. Location: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py - AssertionRewriter.visit_Call_legacy. Evidence: visit_Call_legacy mirrors visit_Call_35 (visiting each arg with self.visit) and likewise does not special-case generator expressions; both call-handling paths therefore encode the same assumption and must be considered equally (see lines ~1000-1028).

4) Incorrect assumption: referring to builtins by an unqualified ast.Name (e.g., ast.Name('list')) is safe. Location: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py - AssertionRewriter.builtin and top-of-module import injection. Evidence: the rewriter injects an alias @py_builtins and provides builtin(name) which returns an attribute on @py_builtins (see lines ~672-705 and ~757-761); this indicates unqualified names may be shadowed by user code and that transformations that insert calls to builtins must account for that.

5) Incorrect assumption (design implication): diagnostic-only changes can always be made without changing program behavior. Location: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py - visit_Call_35 / visit_Call_legacy (design implication). Evidence: materializing a GeneratorExp (for the purpose of richer diagnostics) would force full evaluation and thus change timing, memory characteristics, and side-effect ordering of the program; this behavioral trade-off is a real consequence of any minimal change aimed at improving the assertion message.

6) Investigation / coverage gaps discovered during analysis (equal-weight findings):
   - The visitor currently does not distinguish between different AST shapes for the callee (e.g., ast.Name('all') versus ast.Attribute(...)). Any diagnosis or transformation that aims to affect only builtin all/any must consider these AST shapes and the possibility that 'all' or 'any' could be shadowed or accessed via attributes (noted but not validated by the investigation).
   - The analysis did not audit other lazy-iterator producing constructs (e.g., map(), filter(), itertools functions) to determine whether they produce the same unhelpful output pattern in asserts; this remains unverified.
   - The investigation did not run a full end-to-end example through visit_Assert to confirm the exact final formatted assertion message after any transformation; the exact user-visible output (template text and variable names) was not confirmed in an end-to-end test run.
StepsToReproduce: Run pytest on the test file (e.g., `pytest test_file.py`).
Observe the assertion failure output for the all(...) / any(...) assertion.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: When an all(...) or any(...) assertion using a generator expression fails, the pytest assertion output should show useful information about the failing element(s) or the evaluated contents (e.g., the specific item/value that caused the assertion to fail), similar in informativeness to the equivalent for-loop assertion.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior:
pytest prints an unhelpful failure message showing the generator object, e.g.:

E   assert False
E    +  where False = all(<generator object ...>)

which does not reveal which item failed or the evaluated values.

Checkpoint 4: Propose a fix, then immediately challenge it

Proposed fix (summary):
- Display the fixture scope when listing fixtures for both:
  - pytest --fixtures (function _showfixtures_main)
  - pytest --fixtures-per-test (function _show_fixtures_per_test)
- Minimal changes: include fixturedef.scope in the displayed funcargspec string both in verbose and non-verbose modes.

Concrete changes (edits to src/_pytest/python.py):
- In _show_fixtures_per_test.write_fixture change the formatting to include the fixture scope, e.g.:

  Replace:
  - if verbose > 0:
      bestrel = get_best_relpath(fixture_def.func)
      funcargspec = "{} -- {}".format(argname, bestrel)
    else:
      funcargspec = argname

  With:
  - scope = getattr(fixture_def, "scope", None) or ""
    if verbose > 0:
      bestrel = get_best_relpath(fixture_def.func)
      funcargspec = "{} ({}) -- {}".format(argname, scope, bestrel)
    else:
      funcargspec = "{} ({})".format(argname, scope)

- In _showfixtures_main, in the loop where funcargspec is built, change similar formatting:

  Replace:
  - if verbose > 0:
        funcargspec = "%s -- %s" % (argname, bestrel)
    else:
        funcargspec = argname

  With:
  - scope = getattr(fixturedef, "scope", None) or ""
    if verbose > 0:
        funcargspec = "%s (%s) -- %s" % (argname, scope, bestrel)
    else:
        funcargspec = "%s (%s)" % (argname, scope)

Why both places? Because there are two separate commands / code paths that print fixtures:
- _showfixtures_main prints "all available fixtures"
- _show_fixtures_per_test prints fixtures used by each test

This addresses the feature request comprehensively.

Now, challenge the proposed fix by answering 4a..4e.

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- One-sentence answer: The current code assumes that fixture listings need not display fixture scope; the fix changes this by assuming the scope string (fixturedef.scope) is available and should be shown alongside the fixture name.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- I inspected all hardcoded values and constants in the component classes (fixtures.py). The principal hardcoded values relevant to fixture scope are:
  - scopes = ["session", "package", "module", "class", "function"] (fixtures.py)
  - scopenum_function (index of "function")
- For each:
  - Are they still correct when showing scope? Yes — they are the canonical set of scope names and ordering used everywhere. Printing fixturedef.scope uses the same string (e.g. "function") that is already canonical in the system. There is no assumption that the printed string must be omitted.
- Other hardcoded format strings:
  - funcargspec formatting in both functions is being changed to include scope. This is not a "hardcoded number" but a format string that encoded the previous assumption (omitted scope). My fix replaces that format string to include scope dynamically from fixturedef.scope.
- For special cases:
  - PseudoFixtureDef has attribute scope and will be able to provide a scope string too. I used getattr(..., "scope", None) so code will not fail if a particular object lacks scope, though in practice the objects printed in _showfixtures_main are FixtureDef instances.

Conclusion: The only "value" that encoded the assumption was the omission in the format strings; the scopes list remains correct and unchanged.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- Reasoning: If we change "writing" (the printed output), is there a complementary "reading/parsing" code that consumes that textual output? Where reading of printed output happens in the codebase:
  - There's no code path that parses the printed fixture list. The printed output is intended for humans, and tests assert on output strings, but tests use fnmatch patterns that are permissive.
- I re-checked parent modules and any methods that might expect the old format:
  - The printing functions are standalone and not parsed by the program, so there is no reverse operation that needs updating.
- Tests:
  - Existing tests in testing/python/fixtures.py inspect the stdout using fnmatch patterns. I verified the existing patterns are sufficiently permissive:
    - e.g., result.stdout.fnmatch_lines(["*tmpdir*", "*temporary directory*"]) — unchanged
    - verbose test uses "*tmpdir*--*tmpdir.py*" — adding "(function)" between argname and "--" still matches because '*' matches the intervening characters and the literal "--" still appears (spaces around it do not prevent substring match).
  - I verified test_show_fixtures_testmodule and test_show_fixtures_conftest look for "*arg1*" and "*hello world*", still true.
- Therefore no complementary read/deserialization paths require changes.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- I reviewed all usages of the printing functions' internal variables:
  - We only change the local variable funcargspec to include scope; we do not change function signatures or side effects.
  - getlocation(fixturedef.func, curdir) usage is unchanged.
  - write_docstring and other text rendering are unchanged.
- No other methods in the module depend on the exact text format, so existing methods still work.
- I checked the FixtureDef class for presence of 'scope' attribute; it's always set in __init__ (fixtures created via parsefactories) and PseudoFixtureDef also has 'scope', so accessing fixturedef.scope will succeed in practice. I still use getattr to be defensive.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new methods are required. Both printing functions are updated to include scope, and no other helper or override is necessary.
- I considered whether to add a helper function to format a fixture's display string (to avoid duplicating code), but duplication is minimal (two short format strings) and adding a helper would be extra churn; optional but not required. If desired, we could factor out a small helper format_fixture_display(fixturedef, bestrel, verbose) to keep DRY, but it's not required for correctness.

Checkpoint 5: Final report — root cause, all locations to change, and the fix

Root cause
- The code paths that print fixture lists (_showfixtures_main and _show_fixtures_per_test in src/_pytest/python.py) never formatted or displayed the fixture scope attribute stored on FixtureDef. The scope is available (FixtureDef.scope) but omitted from user-facing output. The bug is simply an omission in the formatting used when printing fixture entries.

Every location that needs to change
- src/_pytest/python.py:
  1) _show_fixtures_per_test: inner function write_fixture — it builds funcargspec and prints fixture name and optionally bestrel; must include scope.
     - Current behavior: prints "argname" (or "argname -- path" when verbose).
     - Assumption encoded: scope is not shown.
     - Fix: include scope string, e.g. "argname (scope) -- path" in verbose mode, "argname (scope)" in non-verbose.
  2) _showfixtures_main: in the main loop that prints the available fixtures — build and print funcargspec similarly to include scope.
     - Current behavior: prints "argname" (or "argname -- path" when verbose).
     - Fix: include scope string: "%s (%s) -- %s" etc.

- No changes are needed in src/_pytest/fixtures.py (FixtureDef already stores scope), but we must ensure we read scope via fixturedef.scope (or getattr defensively) so the code won't break for pseudo objects.

Patch (conceptual, minimal changes) — two small edits in src/_pytest/python.py.

1) _show_fixtures_per_test.write_fixture (replace the formatting portion):

Before:
```
    if verbose > 0:
        bestrel = get_best_relpath(fixture_def.func)
        funcargspec = "{} -- {}".format(argname, bestrel)
    else:
        funcargspec = argname
    tw.line(funcargspec, green=True)
```

After:
```
    scope = getattr(fixture_def, "scope", None) or ""
    if verbose > 0:
        bestrel = get_best_relpath(fixture_def.func)
        funcargspec = "{} ({}) -- {}".format(argname, scope, bestrel)
    else:
        funcargspec = "{} ({})".format(argname, scope)
    tw.line(funcargspec, green=True)
```

2) _showfixtures_main main loop (replace the formatting portion):

Before:
```
    if verbose > 0:
        funcargspec = "%s -- %s" % (argname, bestrel)
    else:
        funcargspec = argname
    tw.line(funcargspec, green=True)
```

After:
```
    scope = getattr(fixturedef, "scope", None) or ""
    if verbose > 0:
        funcargspec = "%s (%s) -- %s" % (argname, scope, bestrel)
    else:
        funcargspec = "%s (%s)" % (argname, scope)
    tw.line(funcargspec, green=True)
```

Rationale for defensiveness:
- I use getattr(fixturedef, "scope", None) because in the wild there are PseudoFixtureDef objects and other wrappers; although the fixtures printed by _showfixtures_main come from FixtureDef lists in FixtureManager, being defensive avoids unexpected AttributeError. PseudoFixtureDef has scope so this is extra safe.

Effects on tests and other code
- Existing tests in the test suite assert printed fixture-related strings using fnmatch, not exact matching; the new output contains the previous substrings (fixture names, docstrings, and "-- <path>") and additionally includes the scope in parentheses. The existing fnmatch patterns are permissive and will still match; hence tests should continue to pass.
- No other code parses the fixture listing output, so no reverse/parsing changes are needed.
- No behavior of fixture execution, lifetime, or semantics is changed.

Why this is a complete fix
- Both entry points that display fixtures are updated:
  - The global fixtures listing (pytest --fixtures)
  - The per-test fixtures listing (pytest --fixtures-per-test)
- The data source for scope (FixtureDef.scope) is already present and used elsewhere; no modifications to how scope is stored, validated, or used in fixture lifetimes are necessary.
- I included a defensive getattr to avoid unforeseen attribute errors.

Suggested small refactor (optional)
- If desired, factor the formatting into a small helper:
  - def format_fixture_display(name, scope, bestrel=None, verbose=False): ...
  - Replace duplicated code in both write_fixture sites.
- Not necessary for correctness, but it reduces duplication.

If you want, I can:
- Produce a patch/diff (git unified diff) with the exact edits to src/_pytest/python.py.
- Run through the tests that reference fixture output (or list the updated test expectations if you prefer to make the scope presence explicit in tests).

Summary
- Root cause: printing functions omitted fixture.scope when showing fixtures.
- Fix: include fixture.scope in printed output in both _showfixtures_main and _show_fixtures_per_test.
- Locations changed: two small formatting edits in src/_pytest/python.py.
- No change to fixture management behavior or internal constants; no additional overrides needed.

===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug: pytest's fixture listings (pytest --fixtures and pytest --fixtures-per-test) omit the fixture scope; the code assumes scope need not be shown. (Found in Checkpoint 1/2.)
- Root cause & location: printing functions in src/_pytest/python.py never include FixtureDef.scope when formatting fixture entries. Data is present: FixtureDef.scope is stored in src/_pytest/fixtures.py (class FixtureDef). (Found in Checkpoints 1c and 2.)
- Fix strategy: change the formatting in two places in src/_pytest/python.py to include the scope string (fixturedef.scope) for each printed fixture: (1) _showfixtures_main loop, and (2) the nested write_fixture inside show_fixtures_per_test/_show_fixtures_per_test. This covers both global and per-test fixture listings. (Proposed in Checkpoint 4.)
- Constants/hardcoded values: no constants need to change; the canonical scopes list is in src/_pytest/fixtures.py: scopes = "session package module class function".split(). The fix reads the existing scope strings — no reorder or constant modification required. (Verified in Checkpoint 3.)
- Methods to add/override: none required. Small formatting edits only. (Determined in Checkpoint 4.)
- Investigation gaps: the agent did not run the test suite or apply the patch; did not search documentation/help output or third-party tools that might parse the fixture listing; did not update tests to assert the new formatting explicitly. These are noted as gaps to address before landing. (Observed in Checkpoint 4e / final review.)

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: Printing omits fixture scope (root cause)
What: _showfixtures_main in src/_pytest/python.py builds funcargspec as either "argname" or "argname -- path" and never includes fixturedef.scope. This directly causes --fixtures to omit scope. (Found in Checkpoint 1c / 2.)

### Finding 2: Per-test fixture listing also omits scope
What: show_fixtures_per_test (src/_pytest/python.py) contains an inner write_fixture that formats fixture names the same way and omits scope for fixtures used by a test. Both listing code paths must be changed. (Found in Checkpoint 1c / 2.)

### Finding 3: Scope is available on fixture objects
What: FixtureDef objects in src/_pytest/fixtures.py include a scope attribute (FixtureDef.scope) and PseudoFixtureDef also carries scope — the data needed to display scope already exists and should be used. (Found in Checkpoint 2/3.)

### Finding 4: Canonical scope strings and ordering are defined and must be preserved
What: The canonical scope names are defined as scopes = "session package module class function".split() in src/_pytest/fixtures.py; other code uses these strings/indexes, so the fix should display the existing string and not change enum/order. (Found in Checkpoint 3.)

### Finding 5: Tests likely unaffected but were not executed
What: Existing tests in testing/python/fixtures.py match fixture output with fnmatch patterns (e.g. "*tmpdir*--*tmpdir.py*"), which are permissive and should still match after inserting "(scope)" between name and "--". However the agent did not run tests; a few tests might match exact strings elsewhere and may need updates. (Found in Checkpoint 1c / 4.)

### Finding 6: No reverse/parse dependency detected
What: No code parses the printed fixture listings; the output is for humans and tests only. So showing scope is not expected to break program logic, but the agent did not check docs/man pages or downstream parsers. (Found in Checkpoint 4c / 4e.)

### Finding 7: Minimal change required; be defensive against wrapper objects
What: Use getattr(fixturedef, "scope", "") when formatting to avoid AttributeError for any non-FixtureDef wrappers (defensive change). No new helper method required but factoring formatting into a small helper is optional. (Found in Checkpoint 4.)

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: src/_pytest/python.py:_showfixtures_main
- Wrong assumption: The code assumes fixture listings need only show "argname" (or "argname -- path" in verbose); it omits fixturedef.scope entirely.
- Evidence: In the loop that prints available fixtures the code does:
  - non-verbose: funcargspec = argname
  - verbose: funcargspec = "%s -- %s" % (argname, bestrel)
  (exact strings observed in file around lines ~1343-1350)
  The code never references fixturedef.scope.
- Related locations: src/_pytest/python.py:_show_fixtures_per_test.write_fixture depends on the same formatting assumption; fixture scope is stored on src/_pytest/fixtures.py:FixtureDef.scope.

### Location: src/_pytest/python.py:show_fixtures_per_test -> write_fixture (nested)
- Wrong assumption: The per-test fixture printer assumes the same format (omit scope) and builds funcargspec as "argname" or "argname -- path".
- Evidence: write_fixture constructs funcargspec without scope:
  - if verbose > 0: funcargspec = "{} -- {}".format(argname, bestrel)
  - else: funcargspec = argname
  (observed in _show_fixtures_per_test near lines ~1286-1291)
- Related locations: src/_pytest/python.py:_showfixtures_main and tests in testing/python/fixtures.py that assert printed output.

### Location: src/_pytest/fixtures.py:FixtureDef.scope
- Wrong assumption (encoded elsewhere): The printing code has implicitly assumed that scope is irrelevant for display even though it is available.
- Evidence: FixtureDef.__init__ sets self.scope (and scopenum derived from scopes list). The global scopes list is:
  - scopes = "session package module class function".split()
  This proves scope information is canonical and already present on the object printed by the listing functions.
- Related locations: FixtureManager stores fixtures in fm._arg2fixturedefs; _showfixtures_main iterates fm._arg2fixturedefs to collect fixturedef objects. PseudoFixtureDef also exposes scope (attr.s fields).

### Location: testing/python/fixtures.py (relevant tests)
- Wrong assumption: Tests assume fixture listings contain name/doc/path substrings but not scope; they use fnmatch patterns that are permissive.
- Evidence: Examples:

  - test_show_fixtures_verbose: result.stdout.fnmatch_lines(["*tmpdir*--*tmpdir.py*", "*temporary directory*"])
  These patterns will still match after inserting "(scope)" because '*' in fnmatch covers extra substrings.
- Related locations: The tests that assert exact substrings or exact format (if any) might need updates; the agent did not exhaustively search all tests for strict assertions.
