bug_report.Title: str(ExceptionInfo) (pytest.raises context) truncates multi-line exception messages to first line
bug_report.Description: Printing the pytest.raises context variable (an _pytest._code.ExceptionInfo instance) via str(excinfo) yields a file:lineno prefix plus only the first line of a multi-line exception message instead of the full message.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) ExceptionInfo.__str__ (src/_pytest/_code/code.py:537-543): constructs a ReprFileLocation using self.exconly() and returns str(loc). By delegating to ReprFileLocation, ExceptionInfo.__str__ exposes the behavior of that formatter instead of directly returning the full exception text.

2) ReprFileLocation.toterminal (src/_pytest/_code/code.py:941-956): explicitly truncates its message at the first newline before emitting the file:lineno: message line (implementation: i = msg.find("\n"); if i != -1: msg = msg[:i]). This enforces a single-line summary for file:lineno output and is the direct cause of losing subsequent lines when a multi-line message is passed through it.

3) ExceptionInfo.exconly (src/_pytest/_code/code.py:452-467): returns the full exception text (potentially containing embedded newlines) by joining format_exception_only output. The full multi-line message is therefore available from excinfo.exconly() and excinfo.value, but ExceptionInfo.__str__ passes that full text to ReprFileLocation which then truncates it.

4) Related/common uses (multiple locations): ReprFileLocation is used in other repr/reporting code paths that expect a short single-line file:lineno summary, e.g. ExceptionInfo._getreprcrash (src/_pytest/_code/code.py:472-476), FormattedExcinfo.repr_traceback_entry / ReprEntry.__str__ (src/_pytest/_code/code.py:668-693 and 935-938), and doctest reporting (src/_pytest/doctest.py:235-236). Because ReprFileLocation.toterminal enforces single-line output, any caller that constructs a ReprFileLocation with a potentially multi-line message will see the same truncation behavior.
StepsToReproduce: 1) Inside a normal try/except, raise an exception whose message contains embedded newlines and print(str(e)) in the except block. Example: try: raise LookupError('A\nB\nC') except LookupError as e: print(str(e)) — this prints the full multi-line message.

2) Use pytest.raises as a context manager to capture the same exception and print(str(excinfo)). Example: with pytest.raises(LookupError) as excinfo: raise LookupError('A\nB\nC') ; print(str(excinfo)) — this prints a file:lineno summary showing only the first line of the message.
3) Observe the difference: the first case shows all message lines; the pytest.raises captured ExceptionInfo shows only a file:lineno plus the first line.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: Printing the pytest.raises context object (str(excinfo)) should present a file:lineno prefix followed by the full exception text including embedded newlines (equivalent to str(excinfo.value) for multi-line messages), so the full message is visible by default.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Printing str(excinfo) shows only a single-line summary consisting of file:lineno plus the first line of the exception message (for example: "<console>:3: LookupError: A"), with subsequent lines of a multi-line message omitted.

method_cache.src/_pytest/assertion/__init__.py:
"""
support for presenting detailed information in failing assertions.
"""
import sys

from _pytest.assertion import rewrite
from _pytest.assertion import truncate
from _pytest.assertion import util


def pytest_addoption(parser):
    group = parser.getgroup("debugconfig")
    group.addoption(
        "--assert",
        action="store",
        dest="assertmode",
        choices=("rewrite", "plain"),
        default="rewrite",
        metavar="MODE",
        help="""Control assertion debugging tools.  'plain'
                            performs no assertion debugging.  'rewrite'
                            (the default) rewrites assert statements in
                            test modules on import to provide assert
                            expression information.""",
    )


def register_assert_rewrite(*names):
    """Register one or more module names to be rewritten on import.

    This function will make sure that this module or all modules inside
    the package will get their assert statements rewritten.
    Thus you should make sure to call this before the module is
    actually imported, usually in your __init__.py if you are a plugin
    using a package.

    :raise TypeError: if the given module names are not strings.
    """
    for name in names:
        if not isinstance(name, str):
            msg = "expected module names as *args, got {0} instead"
            raise TypeError(msg.format(repr(names)))
    for hook in sys.meta_path:
        if isinstance(hook, rewrite.AssertionRewritingHook):
            importhook = hook
            break
    else:
        importhook = DummyRewriteHook()
    importhook.mark_rewrite(*names)


class DummyRewriteHook:
    """A no-op import hook for when rewriting is disabled."""

    def mark_rewrite(self, *names):
        pass


class AssertionState:
    """State for the assertion plugin."""

    def __init__(self, config, mode):
        self.mode = mode
        self.trace = config.trace.root.get("assertion")
        self.hook = None


def install_importhook(config):
    """Try to install the rewrite hook, raise SystemError if it fails."""
    config._assertstate = AssertionState(config, "rewrite")
    config._assertstate.hook = hook = rewrite.AssertionRewritingHook(config)
    sys.meta_path.insert(0, hook)
    config._assertstate.trace("installed rewrite import hook")

    def undo():
        hook = config._assertstate.hook
        if hook is not None and hook in sys.meta_path:
            sys.meta_path.remove(hook)

    config.add_cleanup(undo)
    return hook


def pytest_collection(session):
    # this hook is only called when test modules are collected
    # so for example not in the master process of pytest-xdist
    # (which does not collect test modules)
    assertstate = getattr(session.config, "_assertstate", None)
    if assertstate:
        if assertstate.hook is not None:
            assertstate.hook.set_session(session)


def pytest_runtest_setup(item):
    """Setup the pytest_assertrepr_compare hook

    The newinterpret and rewrite modules will use util._reprcompare if
    it exists to use custom reporting via the
    pytest_assertrepr_compare hook.  This sets up this custom
    comparison for the test.
    """

    def callbinrepr(op, left, right):
        """Call the pytest_assertrepr_compare hook and prepare the result

        This uses the first result from the hook and then ensures the
        following:
        * Overly verbose explanations are truncated unless configured otherwise
          (eg. if running in verbose mode).
        * Embedded newlines are escaped to help util.format_explanation()
          later.
        * If the rewrite mode is used embedded %-characters are replaced
          to protect later % formatting.

        The result can be formatted by util.format_explanation() for
        pretty printing.
        """
        hook_result = item.ihook.pytest_assertrepr_compare(
            config=item.config, op=op, left=left, right=right
        )
        for new_expl in hook_result:
            if new_expl:
                new_expl = truncate.truncate_if_required(new_expl, item)
                new_expl = [line.replace("\n", "\\n") for line in new_expl]
                res = "\n~".join(new_expl)
                if item.config.getvalue("assertmode") == "rewrite":
                    res = res.replace("%", "%%")
                return res

    util._reprcompare = callbinrepr


def pytest_runtest_teardown(item):
    util._reprcompare = None


def pytest_sessionfinish(session):
    assertstate = getattr(session.config, "_assertstate", None)
    if assertstate:
        if assertstate.hook is not None:
            assertstate.hook.set_session(None)


# Expose this plugin's implementation for the pytest_assertrepr_compare hook
pytest_assertrepr_compare = util.assertrepr_compare

    def test_Sequence(self):

        if not hasattr(collections_abc, "MutableSequence"):
            pytest.skip("cannot import MutableSequence")
        MutableSequence = collections_abc.MutableSequence

        class TestSequence(MutableSequence):  # works with a Sequence subclass
            def __init__(self, iterable):
                self.elements = list(iterable)

            def __getitem__(self, item):
                return self.elements[item]

            def __len__(self):
                return len(self.elements)

            def __setitem__(self, item, value):
                pass

            def __delitem__(self, item):
                pass

            def insert(self, item, index):
                pass

        expl = callequal(TestSequence([0, 1]), list([0, 2]))
        assert len(expl) > 1

The bug is about when left is b'' and right is b'42' the message shows "first extra item: 52", meaning saferepr(right[len_left]) produced '52' — that's because for bytes, saferepr of a single byte returns its integer value? Need to inspect how repr for bytes behaves: repr(b'4') is "b'4'". But saferepr called on right[len_left] returns something else because right is bytes and indexing returns an int in Python 3. Indeed bytes[i] returns an int (ordinal). So when comparing sequences, indexing bytes yields ints, so extra= saferepr(right[len_left]) reprs an int 52. What I learned from util.py

What I learned from saferepr.py

1d) Clear statement of the bug and primary responsible function/class
- Bug: When two bytes objects are compared (==) with pytest's assertion rewriting, pytest treats bytes as sequences and uses the sequence-comparison routine (_compare_eq_sequence). That routine indexes into bytes; bytes indexing returns an int (ordinal of the byte) on Python 3, so saferepr prints that integer (e.g., 52) rather than a readable byte element or a whole-bytes diff. Result: confusing messages such as "first extra item: 52".
- Primary responsible function: assertrepr_compare in src/_pytest/assertion/util.py (it decides how to dispatch bytes comparisons). Secondary spot implicated: _compare_eq_sequence (which displays elements after indexing) and saferepr (which produces the integer repr).

Proposed fix (concise)
- Treat bytes (and bytearray) as text-like when deciding how to present diffs in assertrepr_compare (and for the "not in" case); dispatch bytes==bytes comparisons to _diff_text instead of _compare_eq_sequence. Concretely, in src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:
  - Replace the check:
      if istext(left) and istext(right):
          explanation = _diff_text(left, right, verbose)
    with:
      if (istext(left) and istext(right)) or (

Rationale
- _diff_text already documents support for bytes and will convert them into readable text before diffing.
- The current behavior treats bytes as sequences and shows integer element values because bytes[i] yields int on Python 3. That yields confusing output like "first extra item: 52".
- This single change routes bytes comparisons to the textual diff machinery that produces human-readable diffs (b'42' will be shown sensibly).

Now address the required checklist questions.

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- Current assumption: bytes should be treated as generic sequences (like lists, tuples) and compared element-by-element; my fix changes the assumption to bytes being text-like entities that are better represented by whole-text diffs instead of element-wise sequence diffs.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- Hardcoded values I noted earlier:
  - The 42 threshold in _diff_text for skipping identical leading/trailing characters:
    - Is it still correct? Yes. _diff_text converts bytes to readable strings before applying the threshold. The threshold is about trimming long identical prefixes/suffixes; it remains applicable for text converted from bytes.
  - The width computation in assertrepr_compare: width = 80 - 15 - len(op) - 2:
    - Is it still correct? Yes — it only affects how many characters are shown via saferepr in the summary, independent of bytes handling.
  - No other numeric constants encode the 'bytes are sequences' assumption. The logic that chooses sequence comparison vs. text comparison is implemented via istext and issequence predicates; the fix updates the conditional, not numerical constants.

Conclusion: No hardcoded numeric value must become dynamic due solely to this fix. The only change needed is the dispatch decision treating bytes as text.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The complementary operations that may be affected:
  - _compare_eq_sequence (the sequence element-wise comparison) — we are steering bytes away from this code path when both operands are bytes, so the sequence path will no longer be used for same-typed bytes comparisons. That avoids the problematic indexing into bytes yielding ints. No reverse change needed in _compare_eq_sequence (it remains correct for sequences other than bytes).
  - _notin_text: currently invoked only for istext(left) and istext(right). It uses .find and _diff_text to present where the term occurs. If we add bytes to the branch, _notin_text will be used with bytes, and since _diff_text already supports bytes, this is safe. Verify concrete trace:
    - Example: left = b'1', right = b'42' for op == "==": the new code uses _diff_text which converts bytes to readable text and produces the diff using ndiff. For "not in": if left is bytes and right is bytes, .find works for bytes, but _notin_text calls _diff_text(correct_text, text, verbose), and _diff_text accepts bytes and converts them to readable text. So the reverse operation (not in) is now consistent for bytes as well.
- Check all places where assertrepr_compare previously called isiterable and appended _compare_eq_iterable:
  - assertrepr_compare still may call _compare_eq_iterable if isiterable(left) and isiterable(right). isiterable uses iter(obj) but returns not istext(obj). Note isiterable would treat bytes as iterable (not istext since bytes is not str), so previously _compare_eq_iterable could be appended. Under the new branch when we treat bytes as text and thus produce explanation = _diff_text, the code still runs the subsequent isiterable(left/right) check and will run _compare_eq_iterable and extend explanation with that diff if verbose and isiterable True.
  - Is that desirable? For bytes we probably do not want a second appended iterable-based diff (line-based diffs via pprint) in normal cases. But the code already had that behavior for strings vs sequences: when istext True, it used _diff_text and skipped sequence branch and still later checks isiterable and may append _compare_eq_iterable. In the original code, the isiterable branch runs after the sequence/other comparisons. That means for strings, previously, when strings were both istext, explanation = _diff_text; then isiterable(left) and isiterable(right) is True (since iter() works on str), and code called _compare_eq_iterable and appended its output. So the original design already appended iterable diffs after textual diffs in some cases. We keep that behavior for bytes by using the same dispatch — consistent.
  - If we want to avoid adding the extra iterable diff for bytes, we could special-case that, but that mirrors the existing behavior for strings, so it's reasonable.

- Parent methods: (None; module-level functions instead)
- Complementary function: _notin_text:
  - Variables when left/right are bytes: left is b'a', right is b'abc'; _notin_text will call text.find(term) — since both bytes, .find works — index = 1; head and tail are bytes slices; correct_text is head + tail (bytes); diff = _diff_text(correct_text, text, verbose) — _diff_text will convert bytes to readable text and produce human readable diff. So _notin_text works for bytes provided we route bytes into it — this is consistent.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- issequence/istext/isiterable: unchanged; the fix only alters where code routes bytes comparisons. Other methods continue to work as before.
- _compare_eq_sequence: unchanged and will still be used for sequences other than bytes (lists, tuples). It remains correct.
- _diff_text: unchanged, already supports bytes; still works.
- _compare_eq_iterable: unchanged, still works when verbose is requested.
- _notin_text: will now be invoked for bytes if both operands are bytes; its behavior remains correct because _diff_text handles bytes.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new methods are necessary. Only conditional changes in assertrepr_compare (and "not in" branch) are required.
- Consider tests: adding a test to cover bytes comparisons would be desirable:
  - New test suggestion: ensure a failed assertion comparing bytes shows a human-readable byte diff, e.g. assertrepr_compare(config, "==", b"", b"42")[1:] or an end-to-end test exercising pytest to confirm output does not show integer ordinals.
  - But adding tests is optional to the code fix; it's recommended to add one test in testing/test_assertion.py to lock in the behavior.

Summary of code locations to change (complete and precise)
- File: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py
  1) Function: assertrepr_compare (lines ~116-163)
     - Change the first branch from:
         if op == "==":
             if istext(left) and istext(right):
                 explanation = _diff_text(left, right, verbose)
             else:

                     explanation = _compare_eq_sequence(left, right, verbose)
                 ...
     - To:

             if (istext(left) and istext(right)) or (
                 isinstance(left, (bytes, bytearray)) and isinstance(right, (bytes, bytearray))
             ):

             else:

                     explanation = _compare_eq_sequence(left, right, verbose)
                 ...
     - Rationale: route same-typed bytes/bytearray comparisons to _diff_text which knows how to render bytes.
  2) Function: assertrepr_compare (the "not in" branch at lines ~148-151)
     - Change:

     - Rationale: support byte-string "not in" diffs in the same readable way.

No other changes to util.py, saferepr.py, or other modules are required.

Why this is a complete fix
- The confusing output arises from bytes being treated as sequences and the element-wise representation of elements as ints. Redirecting same-typed bytes comparisons to the text diff machinery eliminates the element-wise indexing into bytes in the bytes==bytes case and uses the already prepared readable bytes conversion in _diff_text.
- Complementary operations ("not in") are handled the same way via _notin_text which uses _diff_text, so the fix is symmetric.
- Other code paths (sequence comparison for lists/tuples, set/dict/dataclass comparisons) are unchanged and still work.

Possible alternative / further improvements (optional)
- Also treat memoryview or other binary-like sequences as text-like depending on desired UX (not implemented). The minimal fix is bytes and bytearray.
- If desired, avoid also appending the iterable/pprint full-diff for bytes (the current code may append _compare_eq_iterable after _diff_text because isiterable(bytes) is True). But strings behave similarly today, so symmetry argues to keep the current logic.

Tests to add (recommended)
- Add a test in testing/test_assertion.py similar to:

- Or a higher-level test that runs pytest over a small file with assert b"" == b"42" and checks the stdout does not contain lines like "first extra item: 52" and that it shows a readable diff produced by _diff_text.

Checkpoint 5: Final report

Root cause
- The code dispatched bytes comparisons to the generic sequence-diff code path. Because bytes indexing in Python 3 yields ints (ordinals), saferepr printed the integer ordinal (e.g. 52), producing confusing messages such as "first extra item: 52" instead of showing the byte character or a readable byte-string diff. Meanwhile a dedicated text diff function, _diff_text, already exists and explicitly claims to support bytes by safely converting them to readable text.

Every location that needs to change
1) src/_pytest/assertion/util.py — function assertrepr_compare
   - Current behavior:
     - If both operands are istext (str), call _diff_text.
     - Else, if both operands are issequence (Sequence and not str) then call _compare_eq_sequence, which indexes into sequences and may index bytes producing ints.
   - Assumption encoded: bytes are not text (istext only checks for str) and are sequences.
   - Fix: treat bytes and bytearray as text-like in that dispatch. Change the conditional to call _diff_text when both operands are str OR both are bytes/bytearray.
   - Also adjust the "not in" branch to do the same for _notin_text.

Other affected code (no code change required)
- src/_pytest/assertion/util.py — _compare_eq_sequence:
  - Current behavior: indexes into sequences and uses saferepr on the element(s).
  - Why it's affected: it's the code that produced the integer ordinal output when bytes were treated as sequences.
  - Fix rationale: we don't change this function; we avoid calling it for bytes so it no longer produces confusing outputs in the bytes==bytes case.

- src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py:
  - Current behavior: saferepr on an int returns the integer repr; no change required.
  - Why left unchanged: saferepr is correct in general; the problem is the sequence index yields an int when the element is a byte, producing the integer representation. We fix the caller dispatching.

Hardcoded values that need to become dynamic
- None of the numeric constants needed to become dynamic because of this change. The 42 threshold in _diff_text and width computation in assertrepr_compare remain valid; _diff_text already accommodates bytes by converting them to readable strings.

Methods that need to be added/overridden
- None. No new methods required. Existing _diff_text already supports bytes and will generate readable diffs. We only need to route bytes comparisons to it.

Extra test recommendation
- Add tests asserting that comparing bytes results in readable diffs (and does not print integer ordinals). This can be a unit test on plugin.pytest_assertrepr_compare or an integration test invoking pytest on a small test file with e.g. assert b'' == b'42'.

Why this addresses every affected location
- The confusing output appears only when both operands are bytes (and are routed to sequence comparison). By updating the dispatch logic in assertrepr_compare and the 'not in' branch to detect bytes and route to _diff_text, we ensure bytes comparisons are treated the same way as strings — which _diff_text already supports — and avoid the element-wise integer-display problem generated by indexing bytes.

compressed_analysis:
===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug: pytest shows confusing assertion output for bytes comparisons (e.g. "first extra item: 52") because bytes are treated as generic sequences and bytes[i] yields an int (ordinal), which gets formatted as an integer instead of a readable byte/byte-string diff.
- Root cause/location: dispatch logic in src/_pytest/assertion/util.py::assertrepr_compare routes bytes to sequence-comparison (_compare_eq_sequence) rather than the text-diff routine (_diff_text) that explicitly supports bytes; saferepr then prints the integer result of indexing bytes.
- Proposed fix: in src/_pytest/assertion/util.py, change the dispatch so that when both operands are bytes or bytearray (in addition to str), assertrepr_compare uses _diff_text (and the "not in" branch uses _notin_text). This covers the bytes==bytes and bytes "not in" cases and avoids element-wise indexing into bytes.
- Hardcoded values: no numeric constants must be made dynamic; notable constants to be aware of are the 42 threshold used by _diff_text when skipping identical leading characters and the width computation width = 80 - 15 - len(op) - 2 in assertrepr_compare. These remain valid after the fix.
- Methods to add/override: none required. _diff_text already supports bytes; we only change the conditional dispatch. Recommended: add a unit/integration test to assert the new readable bytes diff and to guard against regression.
- Investigation gaps: confirm whether memoryview or other binary-like types should also be treated as text-like (not covered). Also consider whether the appended iterable/pprint diff (isiterable branch) should be suppressed for bytes to avoid redundant output (mirrors current str behavior but worth reviewing).

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: Bytes indexed as ints causes integer ordinals in messages
What was discovered and why it matters: indexing bytes in Python 3 returns an int, so _compare_eq_sequence ends up calling saferepr on that int and emits e.g. "first extra item: 52". This explains the exact confusing output. (file: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py, method: _compare_eq_sequence). Checkpoint: 1 (search + initial reading).

### Finding 2: _diff_text already supports bytes conversion
What was discovered and why it matters: _diff_text's docstring and implementation explicitly support "text or bytes" and converts bytes to readable text via escape_for_readable_diff, so it is the correct routine to present human-readable diffs for byte sequences. Using it avoids integer-ordinal outputs. (file: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py, method: _diff_text). Checkpoint: 2 (full-file read).

### Finding 3: assertrepr_compare currently treats bytes as sequences
What was discovered and why it matters: assertrepr_compare uses istext(left/right) (which returns True only for str) to pick _diff_text; bytes fail istext and pass issequence (Sequence and not str), so bytes are dispatched to sequence comparison. The dispatch logic in assertrepr_compare is the right single-change location. (file: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py, method: assertrepr_compare). Checkpoint: 2 (full-file read).

### Finding 4: saferepr is behaving correctly for ints/bytes — caller must change
What was discovered and why it matters: saferepr simply repr()s whatever it receives; when given an int (from bytes indexing) it prints the int. Thus saferepr need not be changed; the fix must be made where elements are chosen and formatted, i.e., the dispatch in assertrepr_compare. (file: src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py, class: SafeRepr, method: repr / _callhelper). Checkpoint: 2 (saferepr read).

### Finding 5: "not in" branch also uses istext predicate — needs symmetry
What was discovered and why it matters: the "not in" branch in assertrepr_compare uses istext(left/right) to call _notin_text; for bytes the branch is skipped and a less-helpful fallback occurs. To cover bytes "not in" cases, the same bytes/bytearray conditional must be added there. (file: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py, method: assertrepr_compare). Checkpoint: 3 (scoping and decision tree).

### Finding 6: Tests exist around bytes messages; add a regression test
What was discovered and why it matters: testing/test_assertrewrite.py includes assertion message tests for bytes but not for the bytes==bytes diff case that currently produces the confusing integer. Add a targeted test (unit or integration) asserting that comparing b'' vs b'42' produces readable byte diff and not an ordinal like "52". (file: testing/test_assertrewrite.py or testing/test_assertion.py). Checkpoint: 3 (test search).

### Finding 7: Minor UX choices remain (memoryview/bytearray/multiple diffs)
What was discovered and why it matters: whether to extend the bytes-as-text behavior to memoryview or to suppress iterative/pprint extra diffs for bytes was not checked. These are scope decisions, not blockers for the primary fix. (misc). Checkpoint: 4 (fix-challenge).

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:assertrepr_compare
- **Wrong assumption**: assertrepr_compare assumes only str is "text" (istext returns isinstance(x, str)), so bytes are treated as sequences, not text.
- **Evidence**: assertrepr_compare branches:
    if istext(left) and istext(right):
        explanation = _diff_text(left, right, verbose)
  else:
    if issequence(left) and issequence(right):
        explanation = _compare_eq_sequence(left, right, verbose)
  Because bytes are Sequence and not str, they go into _compare_eq_sequence, which indexes bytes.
  (Observed in full file read; Checkpoint 2.)
- **Related locations**: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:_compare_eq_sequence (caller of saferepr on indexed elements), src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:_diff_text (the correct routine to use instead).

### Location: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:_compare_eq_sequence
- **Wrong assumption**: _compare_eq_sequence formats the "first extra item" using saferepr(right[len_left]) assuming indexing yields a representable element; for bytes indexing returns int, producing ordinal output.
- **Evidence**: message format string found in the file:
    "%s contains %d more item%s, first extra item: %s"
  and code uses saferepr(right[len(left)]) — with right a bytes object, right[len(left)] is an int (e.g. 52). (Observed in full file read; Checkpoint 2.)
- **Related locations**: src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py::SafeRepr (saferepr prints repr(int) -> "52"), src/_pytest/assertion/util.py::assertrepr_compare (dispatching to _compare_eq_sequence).

### Location: src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py:SafeRepr.repr / _callhelper
- **Wrong assumption**: none — saferepr correctly reprs whatever object it receives; problem arises because the caller provided an int from bytes indexing.
- **Evidence**: SafeRepr wraps reprlib.Repr and returns repr(x). repr(52) -> "52"; no special-case for "byte-element-int" because Python bytes indexing yields int.
- **Related locations**: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py::_compare_eq_sequence (which supplies saferepr with elements), src/_pytest/assertion/util.py::_diff_text (alternative that transforms bytes into readable text before repr).

### Location: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:_diff_text
- **Wrong assumption**: none for this fix — _diff_text already assumes it may receive bytes or str and contains logic to convert bytes to human-readable text via escape_for_readable_diff. It is the correct target to produce readable output for bytes.
- **Evidence**: docstring: "Return the explanation for the diff between text or bytes. If the input are bytes they will be safely converted to text." Implementation includes escape_for_readable_diff and later uses difflib.ndiff. It also contains numeric threshold logic: it will skip leading identical chars when i > 42 and subtract 10: "if i > 42: i -= 10; explanation = 'Skipping %d identical leading characters...'" (the 42 constant). (Observed in full file read; Checkpoint 2.)
- **Related locations**: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:assertrepr_compare (should route bytes to this function), src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:_notin_text (uses _diff_text for "not in" cases).

### Location: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:assertrepr_compare (not-in branch)
- **Wrong assumption**: "not in" branch only treats str as text, skipping bytes.
- **Evidence**: code contains:
    elif op == "not in":
        if istext(left) and istext(right):
            explanation = _notin_text(left, right, verbose)
  So bytes "not in" cases do not get the readable path. (Observed in full file read; Checkpoint 3.)
- **Related locations**: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:_notin_text (which calls _diff_text), src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:_diff_text.

---

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness analysis

I inspected the referenced source files to verify each claim in the draft enhanced report. Below I document what I checked, why I checked it, what the code shows (with specific line references), and any gaps or needed clarifications.

1) assertrepr_compare dispatch (verify claim that only str is treated as "text", causing bytes to be treated as sequences)
- Why I checked: the draft claims the assertrepr_compare dispatch treats only str as text (so bytes bypass _diff_text and go to sequence diff).
- What I read: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py
  - istext is defined as isinstance(x, str) (lines 88-90).
  - assertrepr_compare uses that predicate to route equality diffs to _diff_text when both are text, otherwise it checks issequence and routes to _compare_eq_sequence (lines 127-133): 
    - lines 127-130: if op == "==" and istext(left) and istext(right): explanation = _diff_text(...)
    - lines 131-133: else if issequence(left) and issequence(right): explanation = _compare_eq_sequence(...)
  - The "not in" branch uses the same istext check (lines 148-151): if op == "not in": if istext(left) and istext(right): explanation = _notin_text(...)
- Conclusion: The code confirms the draft's claim: istext is str-only, so bytes (which are not instances of str) will not take the _diff_text path and instead are treated as sequences.

2) _compare_eq_sequence and "first extra item" formatting (verify claim that bytes indexing yields ints that get saferepr()'d)
- Why I checked: the draft claims _compare_eq_sequence formats the "first extra item" using saferepr on the indexed element; for bytes this is an int ordinal.
- What I read: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py
  - _compare_eq_sequence uses saferepr(left[len_right]) or saferepr(right[len_left]) for the "extra" element (lines 271 and 275).
  - The plural message template is "%s contains %d more items, first extra item: %s" (lines 283-285).
  - Because bytes indexing (e.g. right[len_left]) yields an int (Python semantics), saferepr receives an int and produces its repr (e.g. "52").
- Conclusion: The code verifies the explanation: when the right/left operand is bytes and has extra elements, the code passes an int (the byte ordinal) into saferepr, producing integer ordinal messages like "first extra item: 52".

3) saferepr behavior (verify that saferepr is not performing a bytes-specialized formatting)
- Why I checked: the draft claims saferepr simply returns repr(x) and will therefore print the integer it is given.
- What I read: src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py
  - saferepr uses SafeRepr and ultimately calls reprlib.Repr.repr or repr, wrapping calls to capture exceptions and truncation (SafeRepr.repr, SafeRepr._callhelper). The returned string is the repr of the argument (lines 25-27, 49-55, 66-78).
- Conclusion: saferepr does not special-case "byte-element-int"; it returns repr(x), so when given an int it prints an integer string. This supports the draft's claim that the caller should avoid passing an int when a readable byte/bytes diff is desired.

4) _diff_text supports bytes and has threshold behavior (verify claim that _diff_text converts bytes via escape_for_readable_diff and that it contains the 42 threshold)
- Why I checked: the draft claims _diff_text already converts bytes to readable text via escape_for_readable_diff and contains the "i > 42" skip logic.
- What I read: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py
  - _diff_text docstring says "Return the explanation for the diff between text or bytes. If the input are bytes they will be safely converted to text." (lines 166-173).
  - The function escape_for_readable_diff and the conversion are present: if isinstance(left, bytes): left = escape_for_readable_diff(left) (lines 178-187, 189-192).
  - The leading/trailing skip logic uses i > 42 and i -= 10 to provide context (lines 194-206 and 198-206).
- Conclusion: _diff_text already supports bytes and contains the numeric threshold behavior referenced in the draft.

5) "not in" branch symmetry (verify claim that bytes are similarly excluded from the text path in "not in")
- Why I checked: the draft mentions the "not in" branch uses istext-only as well.
- What I read: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py
  - The "not in" branch checks istext(left) and istext(right) and only then calls _notin_text (lines 148-151).
  - _notin_text in turn calls _diff_text (line 380).
- Conclusion: The code confirms bytes "not in" cases do not hit the _notin_text/_diff_text path and thus also avoid the readable-text formatting.

6) Tests coverage (verify claim that tests lack a regression test ensuring human-readable bytes==bytes diff)
- Why I checked: the draft says there's no regression test asserting readable byte diffs (e.g. b'' vs b'42').
- What I read:
  - testing/test_assertrewrite.py contains tests that assert bytes used in assertion messages are printed (test_assertion_messages_bytes: creating an assertion message with bytes, checking the message contains b'ohai!' — lines 296-301). This covers bytes appearing as the assertion message, not bytes equality diffs.
  - testing/test_assertion.py contains sequence-related assertion message tests (including templates with "first extra item: 2" for integer sequences) (lines 442-460 show examples). There are tests checking sequence diffs for integer sequences but I did not find tests that specifically assert the human-readable byte-diff for bytes-vs-bytes equality (searches for the "first extra item" string show test coverage for integers, not bytes).
- Conclusion: There are existing tests exercising bytes in assertion messages, and many sequence-diff tests, but I did not find a test that asserts the desired human-readable bytes==bytes diff (i.e., that the "first extra item" for bytes is shown as a readable byte/escape rather than an integer ordinal). The draft's statement that there is "no regression test that asserts the human-readable bytes==bytes diff" is supported by what I found (there is a bytes message test, but not the specific equality-diff regression test).

7) Related types and scope (bytearray/memoryview)
- Why I checked: the investigation analysis raised whether bytearray or other binary-like types should be included; the draft mentions bytes (and in some places bytearray).
- What I read:
  - _diff_text's bytes conversion checks isinstance(..., bytes) (lines 189-192). It does not convert bytearray or memoryview to readable text there.
  - bytearray is a Sequence (and not str), so under the current dispatch it will be treated as a sequence; indexing a bytearray yields an int (like bytes), so the same ordinal-int behavior would be produced by _compare_eq_sequence.
- Conclusion: The same root cause (routing to sequence-diff code that takes indexed elements, which for binary sequences yield ints) applies to bytearray and similar binary sequences; _diff_text only special-cases bytes (not bytearray or memoryview). The draft did not fully enumerate bytearray/memoryview as affected in RootCause, so that needs to be added to ensure completeness.

8) Other code paths that interact with same state (isiterable path)
- Why I checked: assertrepr_compare appends _compare_eq_iterable output if isiterable(left) and isiterable(right). For bytes, isiterable returns True (it iterates and is not text). This could lead to additional diffs.
- What I read: isiterable returns iter(obj) and not istext(obj) (lines 108-113). Later, in assertrepr_compare, if isiterable both sides, the code computes expl = _compare_eq_iterable(...) and appends or sets explanation (lines 142-147).
- Conclusion: For bytes, after sequence diff selection occurs, iterable diff output may also be appended depending on verbosity. This is part of the behavior but not the primary root cause; still, it's a related code path that can affect output.

9) Claims unsupported or that needed softening
- The draft's diagnosis is generally supported by the code. A couple of clarifications/softenings are needed:
  - The draft's RootCause item (3) claims "no incorrect behavior" in saferepr — that's supported, but phrase it as "saferepr behaves as designed: it repr()s the object it is given" to avoid implying absolutes.
  - The draft's RootCause should explicitly mention bytearray/memoryview as additional affected types (they share the same root cause pattern).
  - The draft's Tests gap should be softened to say: existing tests include bytes assertion messages but do not include an equality-diff regression test for bytes-vs-bytes readable diffs (I verified a test for message bytes but not for equality diffs).

Summary of verification vs draft findings
- Supported and accurate:
  - assertrepr_compare dispatch uses istext (str-only) -> bytes go to sequence branch. (src/_pytest/assertion/util.py: lines 88-90, 127-133)
  - _compare_eq_sequence uses saferepr on indexed elements; for bytes these are ints -> integer ordinals appear. (src/_pytest/assertion/util.py: lines 271, 275, 283-285)
  - saferepr returns repr of the passed object; it does not special-case this case. (src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py: lines 25-27, 66-78)
  - _diff_text already supports bytes and converts them via escape_for_readable_diff; it contains the 42 threshold logic. (src/_pytest/assertion/util.py: lines 166-176, 178-187, 189-192, 194-206)
  - "not in" branch uses istext too, so bytes also avoid _notin_text/_diff_text. (src/_pytest/assertion/util.py: lines 148-151, 375-381)
- Missing/needs addition:
  - The same root cause applies to bytearray (and likely memoryview/other binary sequences); _diff_text only converts bytes. I did not see handling for bytearray/memoryview. (src/_pytest/assertion/util.py: lines 189-192)
  - Tests: there is a test asserting bytes in assertion messages (testing/test_assertrewrite.py: lines 296-301) and plenty of sequence-diff tests (testing/test_assertion.py), but I found no test that asserts the human-readable bytes==bytes diff (i.e., no test watching for absence of an integer ordinal like "52" in that equality-diff context).

Phase 1 conclusion and recommended changes to the draft report (diagnostic-only)
- The draft report's diagnosis is supported by the code; the root cause is the dispatch in assertrepr_compare that routes byte sequences into sequence diffing and then formatting the single extra element via saferepr on an int. The draft's individual numbered root-cause items are accurate, but I made the following content and emphasis edits to ensure completeness and strict diagnostic focus:
  - Make explicit that bytearray (and similar binary sequences) share the same root-cause pattern because _diff_text checks only isinstance(..., bytes).
  - Soften wording around tests to avoid implying there are no byte-related tests at all — there are tests for bytes in assertion messages but not for bytes-vs-bytes equality diffs.
  - Ensure RootCause lists all locations with equal weight and describes the incorrect assumptions at each location (istext predicate, use of saferepr on indexed element, and _diff_text's byte-only conversion).
  - Keep the report strictly diagnostic (no fixes or code changes suggested).

- src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:
  - issequence/istext definitions: lines 84-90
  - assertrepr_compare dispatch (== branch): lines 116-133
  - assertrepr_compare "not in" branch: lines 148-151
  - _diff_text docstring and bytes conversion: lines 166-176, 178-187, 189-192
  - _diff_text skip threshold: lines 194-206 (i > 42; i -= 10)
  - _compare_eq_sequence saferepr usage and message template: lines 271, 275, 283-285
  - _notin_text calling _diff_text: lines 375-381
- src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py:
  - SafeRepr.repr/_callhelper behavior, saferepr wrapper: lines 25-27, 49-55, 66-78
- Tests:
  - testing/test_assertrewrite.py: test_assertion_messages_bytes (lines 296-301)
  - testing/test_assertion.py: sequence-diff tests including "first extra item: 2" (lines 442-460)

bug_report.Title: pytest assertion output shows integer ordinals for bytes comparisons (e.g. "first extra item: 52")
bug_report.Description: When pytest reports diffs for byte-string comparisons it prints integer ordinals for byte elements (e.g. "first extra item: 52") because bytes are treated as generic sequences and their indexed elements are ints, producing confusing assertion messages.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:assertrepr_compare — the dispatch assumes only str is "text": istext(x) is implemented as isinstance(x, str) (istext definition). assertrepr_compare uses this predicate to decide whether to call the text diff routine (_diff_text) or the sequence diff routine (_compare_eq_sequence). Because bytes are not instances of str, they do not satisfy istext and are dispatched as generic sequences (see assertrepr_compare == branch).

2) src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:_compare_eq_sequence — the sequence-diff code assumes indexed sequence elements are representable as their original element type. It formats the message using saferepr(left[len_right]) or saferepr(right[len_left]) and the template "%s contains %d more items, first extra item: %s". For bytes (and other binary sequences that yield integer elements when indexed), right[len(left)] (or left[len(right)]) is an int (the byte ordinal), so saferepr prints e.g. "52" — producing the integer-ordinal message rather than a readable byte/escape representation.

3) src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py:SafeRepr / saferepr — saferepr returns a size-limited repr(value) and does not special-case "byte-element-int" values. Therefore, when it is given an int it correctly returns the integer representation (e.g. "52"). This confirms the formatting choice is a consequence of the caller providing an int rather than a human-readable byte sequence.

4) src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:_diff_text — this routine already supports both text and bytes: its docstring states it handles "text or bytes" and it converts bytes into readable text using escape_for_readable_diff before producing a diff. _diff_text also contains the leading/trailing identical-character skip threshold logic (the i > 42 check and i -= 10 adjustment) which affects the context shown in long diffs.

5) src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:assertrepr_compare ("not in" branch) — the same istext-only check is used for the "not in" branch (if istext(left) and istext(right): explanation = _notin_text(...)), so bytes "not in" cases also avoid the readable-text path and receive the sequence/iterable fallback instead.

6) Related binary sequence types (bytearray/memoryview) — _diff_text only converts instances of bytes (isinstance(..., bytes)). Other binary sequence types such as bytearray are treated as sequences by assertrepr_compare and, when indexed, yield integer elements as well; thus they share the same root cause pattern (they will also result in ordinal ints being printed by _compare_eq_sequence). This is a distinct, related location to consider because _diff_text's bytes conversion is narrow (bytes only).

7) Tests gap and coverage nuance — there are tests that exercise bytes appearing in assertion messages (e.g. testing/test_assertrewrite.py: test_assertion_messages_bytes) and many sequence-diff tests for non-bytes sequences (e.g. testing/test_assertion.py). However, I did not find a regression test that asserts the human-readable bytes==bytes equality diff (i.e., a test that would fail if the equality-diff output showed an integer ordinal like "52" rather than a readable byte/escape diff).
StepsToReproduce: Run pytest on that file (e.g. pytest test_b.py).
Observe the assertion output that reports an integer ordinal for the extra byte (e.g. 'first extra item: 52').
(Optional) Repeat with assert b"" == b"1" to observe 'first extra item: 49'.)
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: When two byte strings differ, pytest should display a human-readable byte-string diff (showing the differing bytes as readable byte characters/escapes) rather than printing integer ordinals for byte elements.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior:
Pytest prints integer ordinals for byte elements in sequence-typed diffs. Example output:

E         ?   ++

And for b'1':

def pytest_configure(config):
    xmlpath = config.option.xmlpath
    # prevent opening xmllog on slave nodes (xdist)
    if xmlpath and not hasattr(config, "slaveinput"):
        config._xml = LogXML(
            xmlpath,
            config.option.junitprefix,
            config.getini("junit_suite_name"),
            config.getini("junit_logging"),
            config.getini("junit_duration_report"),
            config.getini("junit_family"),
            config.getini("junit_log_passing_tests"),
        )
        config.pluginmanager.register(config._xml)


def pytest_unconfigure(config):
    xml = getattr(config, "_xml", None)
    if xml:
        del config._xml
        config.pluginmanager.unregister(xml)


def mangle_test_address(address):
    path, possible_open_bracket, params = address.partition("[")
    names = path.split("::")
    try:
        names.remove("()")
    except ValueError:
        pass
    # convert file path to dotted path
    names[0] = names[0].replace(nodes.SEP, ".")
    names[0] = _py_ext_re.sub("", names[0])
    # put any params back
    names[-1] += possible_open_bracket + params
    return names


class LogXML:
    def __init__(
        self,
        logfile,
        prefix,
        suite_name="pytest",
        logging="no",
        report_duration="total",
        family="xunit1",
        log_passing_tests=True,
    ):
        logfile = os.path.expanduser(os.path.expandvars(logfile))
        self.logfile = os.path.normpath(os.path.abspath(logfile))
        self.prefix = prefix
        self.suite_name = suite_name
        self.logging = logging
        self.log_passing_tests = log_passing_tests
        self.report_duration = report_duration
        self.family = family
        self.stats = dict.fromkeys(["error", "passed", "failure", "skipped"], 0)
        self.node_reporters = {}  # nodeid -> _NodeReporter
        self.node_reporters_ordered = []
        self.global_properties = []

        # List of reports that failed on call but teardown is pending.
        self.open_reports = []
        self.cnt_double_fail_tests = 0

        # Replaces convenience family with real family
        if self.family == "legacy":
            self.family = "xunit1"

    def finalize(self, report):
        nodeid = getattr(report, "nodeid", report)
        # local hack to handle xdist report order
        slavenode = getattr(report, "node", None)
        reporter = self.node_reporters.pop((nodeid, slavenode))
        if reporter is not None:
            reporter.finalize()

    def node_reporter(self, report):
        nodeid = getattr(report, "nodeid", report)
        # local hack to handle xdist report order
        slavenode = getattr(report, "node", None)

        key = nodeid, slavenode

        if key in self.node_reporters:
            # TODO: breasks for --dist=each
            return self.node_reporters[key]

        reporter = _NodeReporter(nodeid, self)

        self.node_reporters[key] = reporter
        self.node_reporters_ordered.append(reporter)

        return reporter

    def add_stats(self, key):
        if key in self.stats:
            self.stats[key] += 1

    def _opentestcase(self, report):
        reporter = self.node_reporter(report)
        reporter.record_testreport(report)
        return reporter

    def pytest_runtest_logreport(self, report):
        """handle a setup/call/teardown report, generating the appropriate
        xml tags as necessary.

        note: due to plugins like xdist, this hook may be called in interlaced
        order with reports from other nodes. for example:

        usual call order:
            -> setup node1
            -> call node1
            -> teardown node1
            -> setup node2
            -> call node2
            -> teardown node2

        possible call order in xdist:
            -> setup node1
            -> call node1
            -> setup node2
            -> call node2
            -> teardown node2
            -> teardown node1
        """
        close_report = None
        if report.passed:
            if report.when == "call":  # ignore setup/teardown
                reporter = self._opentestcase(report)
                reporter.append_pass(report)
        elif report.failed:
            if report.when == "teardown":
                # The following vars are needed when xdist plugin is used
                report_wid = getattr(report, "worker_id", None)
                report_ii = getattr(report, "item_index", None)
                close_report = next(
                    (
                        rep
                        for rep in self.open_reports
                        if (
                            rep.nodeid == report.nodeid
                            and getattr(rep, "item_index", None) == report_ii
                            and getattr(rep, "worker_id", None) == report_wid
                        )
                    ),
                    None,
                )
                if close_report:
                    # We need to open new testcase in case we have failure in
                    # call and error in teardown in order to follow junit
                    # schema
                    self.finalize(close_report)
                    self.cnt_double_fail_tests += 1
            reporter = self._opentestcase(report)
            if report.when == "call":
                reporter.append_failure(report)
                self.open_reports.append(report)
            else:
                reporter.append_error(report)
        elif report.skipped:
            reporter = self._opentestcase(report)
            reporter.append_skipped(report)
        self.update_testcase_duration(report)
        if report.when == "teardown":
            reporter = self._opentestcase(report)
            reporter.write_captured_output(report)

            for propname, propvalue in report.user_properties:
                reporter.add_property(propname, propvalue)

            self.finalize(report)
            report_wid = getattr(report, "worker_id", None)
            report_ii = getattr(report, "item_index", None)
            close_report = next(
                (
                    rep
                    for rep in self.open_reports
                    if (
                        rep.nodeid == report.nodeid
                        and getattr(rep, "item_index", None) == report_ii
                        and getattr(rep, "worker_id", None) == report_wid
                    )
                ),
                None,
            )
            if close_report:
                self.open_reports.remove(close_report)

    def update_testcase_duration(self, report):
        """accumulates total duration for nodeid from given report and updates
        the Junit.testcase with the new total if already created.
        """
        if self.report_duration == "total" or report.when == self.report_duration:
            reporter = self.node_reporter(report)
            reporter.duration += getattr(report, "duration", 0.0)

    def pytest_collectreport(self, report):
        if not report.passed:
            reporter = self._opentestcase(report)
            if report.failed:
                reporter.append_collect_error(report)
            else:
                reporter.append_collect_skipped(report)

    def pytest_internalerror(self, excrepr):
        reporter = self.node_reporter("internal")
        reporter.attrs.update(classname="pytest", name="internal")
        reporter._add_simple(Junit.error, "internal error", excrepr)

    def pytest_sessionstart(self):
        self.suite_start_time = time.time()

    def pytest_sessionfinish(self):
        dirname = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(self.logfile))
        if not os.path.isdir(dirname):
            os.makedirs(dirname)
        logfile = open(self.logfile, "w", encoding="utf-8")
        suite_stop_time = time.time()
        suite_time_delta = suite_stop_time - self.suite_start_time

        numtests = (
            self.stats["passed"]
            + self.stats["failure"]
            + self.stats["skipped"]
            + self.stats["error"]
            - self.cnt_double_fail_tests
        )
        logfile.write('<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>')

        suite_node = Junit.testsuite(
            self._get_global_properties_node(),
            [x.to_xml() for x in self.node_reporters_ordered],
            name=self.suite_name,
            errors=self.stats["error"],
            failures=self.stats["failure"],
            skipped=self.stats["skipped"],
            tests=numtests,
            time="%.3f" % suite_time_delta,
        )
        logfile.write(Junit.testsuites([suite_node]).unicode(indent=0))
        logfile.close()

    def pytest_terminal_summary(self, terminalreporter):
        terminalreporter.write_sep("-", "generated xml file: %s" % (self.logfile))

    def add_global_property(self, name, value):
        __tracebackhide__ = True
        _check_record_param_type("name", name)
        self.global_properties.append((name, bin_xml_escape(value)))

    def _get_global_properties_node(self):
        """Return a Junit node containing custom properties, if any.
        """
        if self.global_properties:
            return Junit.properties(
                [
                    Junit.property(name=name, value=value)
                    for name, value in self.global_properties
                ]
            )
        return ""

Root cause:
- The LogXML implementation of pytest's JUnit XML reporter never included timestamp and hostname attributes when constructing the <testsuite> element (explicitly built only name, errors, failures, skipped, tests, time). Thus generated reports are missing timestamp and hostname.

Locations to change:
1) src/_pytest/junitxml.py
   - What currently happens:

Hardcoded values that need to become dynamic:
- None required beyond adding computed timestamp and hostname. The previous explicit list of testsuite attributes is expanded; no other hardcoded numeric constants must change.

Edge cases / notes:
- Use the recorded suite_start_time (set in pytest_sessionstart) to produce timestamp so it reflects the start of the suite rather than the finish. If suite_start_time were absent, fallback to current time.
- The timestamp format chosen is "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S" (no fractional seconds or timezone offset) to match the typical JUnit timestamp format and the example given in the bug report. If desired, include timezone info or fractional seconds; but adding those requires deciding an exact desired format and may affect consumers that expect a specific format.
- For distributed test runs (xdist), the hostname will reflect the machine where this LogXML instance ran. If results are produced on a central node or merged, consumers should be aware that hostname is per-run.

- src/_pytest/junitxml.py:

  In pytest_sessionfinish (replace suite_node construction with):

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness analysis

I will verify the draft's claims against the source code and the investigation findings. I inspected src/_pytest/junitxml.py and testing/test_junitxml.py to confirm the exact behavior and test coverage.

Tool use summary and what I expected to learn (already executed):
- get_file_context src/_pytest/junitxml.py: to find where pytest_sessionstart/pytest_sessionfinish are implemented, whether suite_start_time is set, what attributes are passed to Junit.testsuite, and whether socket or timestamp formatting is present. I expected to find the Junit.testsuite call and confirm which kwargs are passed.
- get_file_context testing/test_junitxml.py: to check whether existing tests assert the presence of testsuite timestamp/hostname attributes.
- search_codebase for "timestamp" and "hostname" across the repo: to confirm whether these attributes are referenced anywhere (tests or other writers).

What I found (analysis of results)

1) Verification of methods and claims in the draft report
- pytest_sessionstart: The file src/_pytest/junitxml.py defines pytest_sessionstart which sets suite_start_time:
  - Found at src/_pytest/junitxml.py lines 640-642:
    - "def pytest_sessionstart(self):"
    - "    self.suite_start_time = time.time()"
  - This confirms the draft's claim that suite_start_time is recorded at session start.

- pytest_sessionfinish and Junit.testsuite call: The file contains the Junit.testsuite construction in pytest_sessionfinish:
  - Found at src/_pytest/junitxml.py lines 643-669 (key excerpt):
    - suite_stop_time = time.time()
    - suite_time_delta = suite_stop_time - self.suite_start_time
    - ...
    - suite_node = Junit.testsuite(
        self._get_global_properties_node(),
        [x.to_xml() for x in self.node_reporters_ordered],
        name=self.suite_name,
        errors=self.stats["error"],
        failures=self.stats["failure"],
        skipped=self.stats["skipped"],
        tests=numtests,
        time="%.3f" % suite_time_delta,
      )
  - This confirms the draft's claim that the testsuite element is built with exactly name, errors, failures, skipped, tests and time, and that timestamp and hostname are omitted.

- Top-level imports: The module imports time at top (src/_pytest/junitxml.py line 15: "import time"). There is no "import socket" nor any code that formats a timestamp string for testsuite in this file. The absence of socket import and absence of timestamp-formatted string are consistent with the draft's claim.

- testing/test_junitxml.py test coverage: I inspected testing/test_junitxml.py and searched the repository for "timestamp" and "hostname".
  - The tests exercise many attributes of the <testsuite> node (e.g., name, errors, failures, skipped, tests, testcase times), but I found no tests that assert a testsuite "timestamp" or "hostname" attribute.
  - A repo-wide search returned no matches for "hostname" and no uses of "timestamp" relevant to junit output. This supports the draft's claim that existing tests do not assert presence of timestamp/hostname.

2) RootCause verification
- The core root cause is supported by the code: pytest's JUnit XML writer (LogXML.pytest_sessionfinish) constructs the <testsuite> node without timestamp and hostname attributes. The Junit.testsuite(...) call at lines 660-669 omits timestamp and hostname; the code instead passes time as a formatted float string "time='%.3f' % suite_time_delta".
- The code records suite_start_time at session start and later uses it to compute suite_time_delta, but it does not compute or attach a timestamp string. Thus the code encodes the assumption that a timestamp string and hostname are not part of the emitted testsuite attributes.

3) Claims not supported by code (flagged)
- The draft includes example formulas for timestamp (time.strftime(... localtime(self.suite_start_time))) and hostname (socket.gethostname()). Those are reasonable expressions of the missing relationships (how a timestamp/hostname are typically produced), but they are implementation choices. The codebase does not currently contain those computations; the assertion that those exact formulas are required is not something the code enforces or consumers require. The investigation noted this as a gap (timezone/format/precision and consumer expectations are unverified). I will keep these formulas in RootCause only as explanations of the missing relationships (what is absent), not as prescriptive fixes.

4) Important findings from the investigation that must appear in RootCause
- Missing attributes at the single writer location (LogXML.pytest_sessionfinish).
- The exact omission is the Junit.testsuite(...) call with explicit kwargs (name/errors/failures/skipped/tests/time) and no timestamp/hostname.
- pytest_sessionstart sets self.suite_start_time; pytest_sessionfinish uses that value to compute suite_time_delta. The writer uses suite_start_time for duration calculations but does not serialize a timestamp string.
- There is a defensive-usage concern: pytest_sessionfinish uses self.suite_start_time directly (suite_time_delta = suite_stop_time - self.suite_start_time) — i.e., it assumes pytest_sessionstart always ran and suite_start_time exists. The draft flagged this; the code indeed uses self.suite_start_time without checking, which could raise AttributeError in unusual execution flows where pytest_sessionstart was not invoked.
- Testing gaps: testing/test_junitxml.py does not assert timestamp/hostname attributes; so regressions or additions would not be covered by existing tests.
- Investigation omissions: the draft/investigation flagged unverified assumptions about the exact timestamp format (timezone inclusion, fractional seconds) and how consumers expect it; these are not proven in the source and remain open questions.

5) Completeness check for affected locations and code paths
- I searched the codebase for other Junit.testsuite(...) calls or other writers that might produce testsuite attributes and found only the single construction in src/_pytest/junitxml.py. That indicates a single authoritative writer for testsuite attributes in this repo.
- The only setup of suite_start_time is in pytest_sessionstart in the same module; no other modules set or consume that attribute.
- The tests that examine junit output are all in testing/test_junitxml.py and they do not check timestamp/hostname — so adding these attributes would not be asserted by existing tests.
- No other classes in the repo hardcode a timestamp/hostname assumption relevant to this writer.

6) Field discipline issues in the draft
- Description, StepsToReproduce, ExpectedBehavior, ObservedBehavior are generally appropriate; they are focused on symptoms and user-visible behavior.
- RootCause in the draft contains some prescriptive language like "a defensive fallback is needed (e.g. ...)" and concrete code-change recommendations. Per the report guidelines, RootCause should avoid prescribing fixes and should present only incorrect assumptions / missing relationships and the locations. I will rephrase RootCause to remove prescriptive wording while preserving the relationships/formulas as the missing items that the code does not compute.
- The draft included a proposed test assertion example; tests belong to "evidence" or "changes" suggestion rather than RootCause. The RootCause should state that tests do not assert timestamp/hostname, and reference testing/test_junitxml.py. I will move the prescriptive test suggestion wording out of RootCause and keep it strictly as a diagnosis of missing assertions.

Summary of additional checks performed
- Confirmed no other tests or code reference "hostname" or "timestamp" attributes for junit output.
- Confirmed that the module imports time but not socket, consistent with no hostname computation.
- Confirmed suite_start_time is set during pytest_sessionstart and used in pytest_sessionfinish, but suite_start_time is referenced without a presence check.

Conclusion of Phase 1
- The draft's central diagnostic claim is correct and supported by the code: LogXML.pytest_sessionfinish constructs the <testsuite> element without timestamp and hostname attributes.
- The draft omitted an explicit statement about the unverified consumer expectations (timestamp format/timezone/fractional seconds) which the investigation flagged; I will add that as a separate RootCause item describing an unvalidated assumption.
- I will rewrite RootCause to be purely diagnostic (no fix instructions), include all secondary findings with equal weight, and keep the other fields concise and appropriately scoped.

bug_report.Title: Pytest JUnit XML <testsuite> missing timestamp and hostname attributes
bug_report.Description: When pytest generates JUnit XML (via --junitxml), the <testsuite> element does not include the timestamp and hostname attributes that are commonly present in JUnit reports.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) Missing testsuite attributes at the single writer location: the JUnit XML writer constructs the <testsuite> element without timestamp and hostname. Location: src/_pytest/junitxml.py, class LogXML, method pytest_sessionfinish. Evidence: the Junit.testsuite(...) call passes only name, errors, failures, skipped, tests and time and omits timestamp and hostname.

2) The writer computes suite duration but does not serialize a timestamp string: pytest_sessionfinish computes suite_time_delta from self.suite_start_time and suite_stop_time but does not compute or attach a timestamp attribute to the testsuite element. Location: src/_pytest/junitxml.py, pytest_sessionstart (sets self.suite_start_time) and pytest_sessionfinish (uses suite_start_time to compute duration but does not produce a timestamp string).

3) Missing hostname computation: the module imports time but contains no hostname retrieval (no socket.gethostname() or equivalent) and does not provide a hostname attribute on the testsuite element. Location: src/_pytest/junitxml.py (top-level imports and pytest_sessionfinish).

4) Use-of-suite_start_time assumes it exists: pytest_sessionfinish uses self.suite_start_time directly (suite_time_delta = suite_stop_time - self.suite_start_time), which encodes the assumption that pytest_sessionstart has run and set suite_start_time on all execution paths. Location: src/_pytest/junitxml.py, LogXML.pytest_sessionfinish and pytest_sessionstart.

5) Tests do not assert presence of these attributes: existing junit output tests exercise many testsuite/testcase attributes but do not assert a testsuite timestamp or hostname, so the absence of these attributes is not covered by current tests. Location: testing/test_junitxml.py.

6) Unverified consumer/format expectations: there is no canonical timestamp format or timezone/precision requirement encoded in the repository for testsuite timestamps. The codebase does not specify how a timestamp should be formatted (for example, whether it must be an ISO-like localtime string, include timezone info, or include fractional seconds), so any assumed format would be an unverified consumer expectation. Location: investigation findings (no timestamp-format specification found in repo).
StepsToReproduce: Create a minimal test file (for example test_sample.py with one passing and one failing test).
Run pytest with JUnit XML output: pytest --junitxml=out.xml
Open out.xml and inspect the <testsuite> element: verify it does not include timestamp="..." or hostname="..." attributes.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: The generated JUnit XML <testsuite> element includes a timestamp attribute with a value in the form YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS (or otherwise documented ISO-like format) and a hostname attribute containing the machine's hostname, in addition to the existing attributes (name, tests, failures, errors, skipped, time).
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: The generated JUnit XML <testsuite> element only contains attributes name, tests, failures, errors, skipped and time (time formatted as '%.3f'), and is missing timestamp and hostname.

""" core implementation of testing process: init, session, runtest loop. """
import enum
import fnmatch
import functools
import importlib
import os
import sys
from typing import Dict

import py

import _pytest._code

from _pytest.config import directory_arg

from _pytest.config import UsageError
from _pytest.outcomes import exit
from _pytest.runner import collect_one_node
from _pytest.runner import SetupState


class ExitCode(enum.IntEnum):
    """
    .. versionadded:: 5.0

    Encodes the valid exit codes by pytest.

    Currently users and plugins may supply other exit codes as well.
    """

    #: tests passed
    OK = 0
    #: tests failed
    TESTS_FAILED = 1
    #: pytest was interrupted
    INTERRUPTED = 2
    #: an internal error got in the way
    INTERNAL_ERROR = 3
    #: pytest was misused
    USAGE_ERROR = 4
    #: pytest couldn't find tests
    NO_TESTS_COLLECTED = 5


def pytest_addoption(parser):
    parser.addini(
        "norecursedirs",
        "directory patterns to avoid for recursion",
        type="args",
        default=[".*", "build", "dist", "CVS", "_darcs", "{arch}", "*.egg", "venv"],
    )
    parser.addini(
        "testpaths",
        "directories to search for tests when no files or directories are given in the "
        "command line.",
        type="args",
        default=[],
    )
    group = parser.getgroup("general", "running and selection options")
    group._addoption(
        "-x",
        "--exitfirst",
        action="store_const",
        dest="maxfail",
        const=1,
        help="exit instantly on first error or failed test.",
    ),
    group._addoption(

        metavar="num",
        action="store",

    group.addoption(

            "base temporary directory for this test run."
            "(warning: this directory is removed if it exists)"
        ),
    )


def wrap_session(config, doit):
    """Skeleton command line program"""
    session = Session(config)
    session.exitstatus = ExitCode.OK
    initstate = 0
    try:
        try:
            config._do_configure()
            initstate = 1
            config.hook.pytest_sessionstart(session=session)
            initstate = 2
            session.exitstatus = doit(config, session) or 0
        except UsageError:
            session.exitstatus = ExitCode.USAGE_ERROR
            raise
        except Failed:
            session.exitstatus = ExitCode.TESTS_FAILED
        except (KeyboardInterrupt, exit.Exception):
            excinfo = _pytest._code.ExceptionInfo.from_current()
            exitstatus = ExitCode.INTERRUPTED
            if isinstance(excinfo.value, exit.Exception):
                if excinfo.value.returncode is not None:
                    exitstatus = excinfo.value.returncode
                if initstate < 2:
                    sys.stderr.write(
                        "{}: {}\n".format(excinfo.typename, excinfo.value.msg)
                    )
            config.hook.pytest_keyboard_interrupt(excinfo=excinfo)
            session.exitstatus = exitstatus
        except:  # noqa
            excinfo = _pytest._code.ExceptionInfo.from_current()
            config.notify_exception(excinfo, config.option)
            session.exitstatus = ExitCode.INTERNAL_ERROR
            if excinfo.errisinstance(SystemExit):
                sys.stderr.write("mainloop: caught unexpected SystemExit!\n")

    finally:
        excinfo = None  # Explicitly break reference cycle.
        session.startdir.chdir()
        if initstate >= 2:
            config.hook.pytest_sessionfinish(
                session=session, exitstatus=session.exitstatus
            )
        config._ensure_unconfigure()
    return session.exitstatus


def pytest_cmdline_main(config):
    return wrap_session(config, _main)


def _main(config, session):
    """ default command line protocol for initialization, session,
    running tests and reporting. """
    config.hook.pytest_collection(session=session)
    config.hook.pytest_runtestloop(session=session)

    if session.testsfailed:
        return ExitCode.TESTS_FAILED
    elif session.testscollected == 0:
        return ExitCode.NO_TESTS_COLLECTED


def pytest_collection(session):
    return session.perform_collect()


def pytest_runtestloop(session):
    if session.testsfailed and not session.config.option.continue_on_collection_errors:
        raise session.Interrupted(
            "%d error%s during collection"
            % (session.testsfailed, "s" if session.testsfailed != 1 else "")
        )

    if session.config.option.collectonly:
        return True

    for i, item in enumerate(session.items):
        nextitem = session.items[i + 1] if i + 1 < len(session.items) else None
        item.config.hook.pytest_runtest_protocol(item=item, nextitem=nextitem)
        if session.shouldfail:
            raise session.Failed(session.shouldfail)
        if session.shouldstop:
            raise session.Interrupted(session.shouldstop)
    return True


def _in_venv(path):
    """Attempts to detect if ``path`` is the root of a Virtual Environment by
    checking for the existence of the appropriate activate script"""
    bindir = path.join("Scripts" if sys.platform.startswith("win") else "bin")
    if not bindir.isdir():
        return False
    activates = (
        "activate",
        "activate.csh",
        "activate.fish",
        "Activate",
        "Activate.bat",
        "Activate.ps1",
    )
    return any([fname.basename in activates for fname in bindir.listdir()])


def pytest_ignore_collect(path, config):
    ignore_paths = config._getconftest_pathlist("collect_ignore", path=path.dirpath())
    ignore_paths = ignore_paths or []
    excludeopt = config.getoption("ignore")
    if excludeopt:
        ignore_paths.extend([py.path.local(x) for x in excludeopt])

    if py.path.local(path) in ignore_paths:
        return True

    ignore_globs = config._getconftest_pathlist(
        "collect_ignore_glob", path=path.dirpath()
    )
    ignore_globs = ignore_globs or []
    excludeglobopt = config.getoption("ignore_glob")
    if excludeglobopt:
        ignore_globs.extend([py.path.local(x) for x in excludeglobopt])

    if any(fnmatch.fnmatch(str(path), str(glob)) for glob in ignore_globs):
        return True

    allow_in_venv = config.getoption("collect_in_virtualenv")
    if not allow_in_venv and _in_venv(path):
        return True

    return False


def pytest_collection_modifyitems(items, config):
    deselect_prefixes = tuple(config.getoption("deselect") or [])
    if not deselect_prefixes:
        return

    remaining = []
    deselected = []
    for colitem in items:
        if colitem.nodeid.startswith(deselect_prefixes):
            deselected.append(colitem)
        else:
            remaining.append(colitem)

    if deselected:
        config.hook.pytest_deselected(items=deselected)
        items[:] = remaining


class FSHookProxy:
    def __init__(self, fspath, pm, remove_mods):
        self.fspath = fspath
        self.pm = pm
        self.remove_mods = remove_mods

    def __getattr__(self, name):
        x = self.pm.subset_hook_caller(name, remove_plugins=self.remove_mods)
        self.__dict__[name] = x
        return x


class NoMatch(Exception):
    """ raised if matching cannot locate a matching names. """


class Interrupted(KeyboardInterrupt):
    """ signals an interrupted test run. """

    __module__ = "builtins"  # for py3


class Failed(Exception):
    """ signals a stop as failed test run. """


@attr.s
class _bestrelpath_cache(dict):
    path = attr.ib()

    def __missing__(self, path: str) -> str:
        r = self.path.bestrelpath(path)  # type: str
        self[path] = r
        return r


class Session(nodes.FSCollector):
    Interrupted = Interrupted
    Failed = Failed
    _setupstate = None  # type: SetupState

    def __init__(self, config):
        nodes.FSCollector.__init__(
            self, config.rootdir, parent=None, config=config, session=self, nodeid=""
        )
        self.testsfailed = 0
        self.testscollected = 0
        self.shouldstop = False
        self.shouldfail = False
        self.trace = config.trace.root.get("collection")
        self._norecursepatterns = config.getini("norecursedirs")
        self.startdir = config.invocation_dir
        self._initialpaths = frozenset()
        # Keep track of any collected nodes in here, so we don't duplicate fixtures
        self._node_cache = {}
        self._bestrelpathcache = _bestrelpath_cache(
            config.rootdir
        )  # type: Dict[str, str]
        # Dirnames of pkgs with dunder-init files.
        self._pkg_roots = {}

        self.config.pluginmanager.register(self, name="session")

    def __repr__(self):
        return "<%s %s exitstatus=%r testsfailed=%d testscollected=%d>" % (
            self.__class__.__name__,
            self.name,
