Checkpoint 3: Map the architecture

3a) Component classes (from primary class attributes) — None (SafeRepr doesn’t have class attributes pointing to other classes). Still, SafeRepr calls out to helper functions in the same module: _format_repr_exception and _try_repr_or_str. I inspected them for hardcoded values & assumptions:
- _try_repr_or_str: fallback returns '{}("{}")'.format(type(obj).__name__, obj)
  - Assumption: calling format(obj) is safe (i.e., str(obj) does not raise).
  - Risk: str(obj) may call user-defined __str__ or __format__ which can raise or call back into failing __getattribute__/__repr__.
- _format_repr_exception: returns "<[{} raised in repr()] {} object at 0x{:x}>".format(exc_info, obj.__class__.__name__, id(obj))
  - Assumption: accessing obj.__class__.__name__ is safe.
  - Risk: accessing obj.__class__ triggers instance attribute lookup and may call user-defined __getattribute__ (or other descriptors) which can raise or have side effects.

Other hardcoded values:
- In _ellipsize: they use 3 as length of "..." — that's correct and represents the ellipsis length; unrelated to this bug.

3b) Parent class (reprlib.Repr)
- I could not read the stdlib reprlib.Repr from this repo, but SafeRepr overrides:
  - repr(self,x) — overriding parent repr
  - repr_instance(self,x,level) — overriding parent method that handles instance repr dispatch
- SafeRepr inherits other repr_* methods from reprlib.Repr without overriding. Those methods ultimately will call repr_instance for user instances; SafeRepr.repr and repr_instance wrap calls to repr() / super().repr(x) in try/except and then call _format_repr_exception on error, so _format_repr_exception must be safe and not call user code.

3c) Sibling classes inheriting from same parent (reprlib.Repr) — none in this repo. Not relevant.

3d) Call graph around SafeRepr
- code.py -> FormattedExcinfo.repr_locals uses saferepr(value)
- ExceptionInfo.__repr__ etc call saferepr
- Many places in pytest call saferepr() to safely render objects when building exception reports. So saferepr is heavily used during exception formatting.

Conclusion of architecture mapping:
- saferepr is the central safety gasket used across pytest when formatting arbitrary user objects. Its helpers must not perform any operations which could invoke arbitrary user code (like obj.__class__ lookup or obj.__str__), because they're invoked when repr(obj) failed and we are already in an exception reporting context.

---

Checkpoint 4: Propose a fix, then immediately challenge it

Proposed fix (concrete changes to src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py):

1) In _try_repr_or_str, do not attempt to call str(obj) / repr(obj) fallback that includes obj directly. Instead, on failure of repr(obj), return a representation that does not call back into user code (use type(obj).__name__ and id(obj)). For example:

def _try_repr_or_str(obj):
    try:
        return repr(obj)
    except (KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit):
        raise
    except BaseException:
        # Avoid calling obj.__str__ (which may execute user code); use safe info
        try:
            return "{} object at 0x{:x}".format(type(obj).__name__, id(obj))
        except Exception:
            return "{}".format(type(obj).__name__)

2) In _format_repr_exception use type(obj).__name__ instead of obj.__class__.__name__ (the later may call instance attribute access on obj and thus user code). So change:

return "<[{} raised in repr()] {} object at 0x{:x}>".format(
    exc_info, type(obj).__name__, id(obj)
)

These two changes avoid calling any user-defined attribute access or __str__/__repr__ while formatting a failing repr.

Patch (conceptual diff):

--- a/src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py
+++ b/src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py
@@
 def _try_repr_or_str(obj):
     try:
         return repr(obj)
     except (KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit):
         raise
     except BaseException:
-        return '{}("{}")'.format(type(obj).__name__, obj)
+        # Don't call obj.__str__ or obj.__repr__ here: they may raise or execute
+        # arbitrary user code. Use class name and id as a safe fallback.
+        try:
+            return "{} object at 0x{:x}".format(type(obj).__name__, id(obj))
+        except Exception:
+            return "{}".format(type(obj).__name__)
@@
 def _format_repr_exception(exc: BaseException, obj: Any) -> str:
     try:
         exc_info = _try_repr_or_str(exc)
@@
-    return "<[{} raised in repr()] {} object at 0x{:x}>".format(
-        exc_info, obj.__class__.__name__, id(obj)
-    )
+    # Use type(obj).__name__ instead of obj.__class__.__name__ to avoid invoking
+    # instance attribute access (which could call user code / __getattribute__).
+    return "<[{} raised in repr()] {} object at 0x{:x}>".format(
+        exc_info, type(obj).__name__, id(obj)
+    )

Now, immediately challenge the fix under the required questions.

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- The original code assumed it was safe to access obj.__class__.__name__ and to format the object (obj) in _try_repr_or_str's fallback — in other words, it assumed that calling obj.__str__ / obj.__repr__ or accessing __class__ will not raise or run arbitrary user code. My fix removes that assumption: it no longer calls any instance-level attribute access or str(obj); it uses type(obj).__name__ and id(obj) which are safe and do not call into user code.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- In the saferepr module, the problematic hardcoded usages were:
  - '{}("{}")'.format(type(obj).__name__, obj) — this encodes the assumption that inserting obj into format() is safe (i.e., str(obj) won't throw). Under the new assumption (we cannot call str(obj)), this value is not correct and must be replaced; I replace it with "{} object at 0x{:x}".format(type(obj).__name__, id(obj)) which does not call user code.
  - obj.__class__.__name__ in the final formatted string — this encodes the assumption that attribute access of __class__ on an instance is safe. Under the new assumption this is unsafe, so replaced with type(obj).__name__ which does not invoke instance attribute lookup.

Other hardcoded numbers (like 3 from _ellipsize) do not encode the unsafe assumption; they are unrelated and remain correct.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The reverse operation here would be any code that expects the exact old textual format produced by _format_repr_exception/_try_repr_or_str. I inspected tests in testing/io/test_saferepr.py and other places that match expected strings including "<[... raised in repr()] BrokenRepr object at 0x{:x}>". The change preserves the outer structure ("<[{exc_info} raised in repr()] {ClassName} object at 0x{id}>") and only changes how we obtain the ClassName (type(obj).__name__ vs obj.__class__.__name__) and how exc_info fallback is produced (no inner obj insertion). For the vast majority of cases, the results are identical (type(obj).__name__ equals obj.__class__.__name__), but now we don't risk calling obj.__getattribute__.

Concrete parent-method checks:
- The parent reprlib.Repr methods call into our repr_instance/ repr. We did not change the signature or behavior; the only change is that exceptions raised while building the fallback string won't cause a second exception: instead we return a safe string. Thus no complementary operation needs to be overridden.

I read the relevant parent's parts (manifested behavior): SafeRepr.repr and repr_instance catch exceptions from repr(x) and call _format_repr_exception to produce a fallback string. Since _format_repr_exception is now safe, no additional overrides are needed.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- Methods in saferepr.py:
  - _try_repr_or_str: still tries repr(obj) first. On repr failure it now returns a safe string built from type(obj).__name__ and id(obj); this should not break callers (they only consume the returned string).
  - _format_repr_exception: now uses type(obj).__name__ and id(obj) — still returns the same semantic format and is safe for objects with broken __getattribute__ / __repr__.
  - SafeRepr.__init__, repr, repr_instance: logic unchanged; they will catch exceptions and call our safer _format_repr_exception. They continue to function.
  - safeformat and saferepr: unchanged except they use the new safe helpers.
  - AlwaysDispatchingPrettyPrinter: unchanged.

Therefore all methods continue to work; the change reduces the chance of raising an extra exception while formatting.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new methods are strictly required. We're only changing two helper behaviors to avoid calling user code while formatting exceptions. The parent reprlib.Repr methods are unchanged and still behave correctly; SafeRepr still overrides repr and repr_instance as before.

I also scanned the rest of the pytest codebase files for similar risky patterns:
- There are a few occurrences of obj.__class__.__name__ or self.__class__.__name__ elsewhere, but these are used on pytest-internal objects (e.g. self is CaptureManager) where attribute access is controlled. However, to be defensive one might consider changing code that prints arbitrary user objects to use type(obj).__name__ when the object comes from untrusted user code. For this particular bug (INTERNALERROR while printing a user object repr), the correct minimal and complete fix is to harden saferepr's fallback code as above. Broader surgery (systematically replacing obj.__class__ usages) is not required here and could be intrusive.

---

Checkpoint 5: Final report (complete analysis and patch locations)

Root cause
- While formatting a failing repr() of a user object, pytest's saferepr code attempted to call instance-level operations that can invoke user code:
  - _format_repr_exception used obj.__class__.__name__ (this triggers instance attribute lookup and may call a broken __getattribute__ on the object).
  - _try_repr_or_str's fallback attempted to include obj in a format string, calling str(obj)/__format__ and thereby potentially invoking user code (broken __str__, __repr__, or __getattribute__).
- When the object has broken __getattribute__ or __repr__ (raising errors), these extra invocations cause more exceptions while pytest is already handling an exception, culminating in an INTERNALERROR.

All locations that need to change (complete fix)
1) File: src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py
   - Change 1: _try_repr_or_str fallback — replace the use of the object (obj) in the fallback format with a safe fallback that does not call obj.__str__ or obj.__repr__:
     - Current: return '{}("{}")'.format(type(obj).__name__, obj)
     - New: return "{} object at 0x{:x}".format(type(obj).__name__, id(obj)) (with a tiny additional fallback to just the type name if even that fails).
     - Why: avoids calling into user-defined __str__/__repr__ or __format__, which can raise or cause further problems.
   - Change 2: _format_repr_exception — avoid accessing obj.__class__.__name__:
     - Current: ... obj.__class__.__name__ ...
     - New: ... type(obj).__name__ ...
     - Why: obj.__class__ attribute access can be implemented via instance-level descriptors / __getattribute__ and thus call user code; type(obj) is guaranteed to return the object's type without invoking instance-level code and is safe.

Why these are the only locations necessary
- saferepr is the central place used when building safe representations of arbitrary user objects during exception formatting. The problem was specifically unsafe instance-level accesses performed by saferepr's fallback code. Changing these two spots prevents saferepr itself from triggering user code when it is trying to recover from a failing repr, eliminating the INTERNALERROR observed. Other uses of __class__.__name__ within pytest are typically used on pytest-internal objects (self = objects owned by pytest) and are not exposed to arbitrary user implementations; those are not strictly required to change for this bug fix.

Any hardcoded values that need to become dynamic
- No numeric constants needed change. The only "hardcoded" format strings were the ones calling obj and obj.__class__.__name__, which were unsafe; replacing them with type(obj).__name__ and id(obj) avoids dynamic calls on the user object. The ellipsize arithmetic (uses 3 for "...") remains correct.

Methods to add/override
- None. All parent methods still apply; SafeRepr continues to override repr and repr_instance as before. No additional overrides needed.

Extra notes and rationale
- Using type(obj).__name__ and id(obj) is safe: neither invokes instance-level code on obj, and id(obj) is a built-in that just returns the memory address/value and does not involve attribute access. object.__repr__(obj) could be used as an alternative for a safe object repr, but the existing code historically returns "ClassName object at 0x..."; so using type(obj).__name__ and id(obj) preserves that style.
- The fix respects the existing public-facing strings (we keep the same outer angle-bracketed format), while making the exception formatting robust against objects with broken __getattribute__, __repr__, or __str__ methods.
- I reviewed tests in testing/io/test_saferepr.py to ensure behavior is preserved for the tested cases (the new fallback is only used when repr(exc) fails; in most test cases repr(exc) still succeeds; where it fails, returning a safe type-name+id is arguably better than invoking user code and risking INTERNALERROR). The new behavior is aligned with the intent: saferepr should never raise.

Proposed patch (ready-to-apply)
- File: src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py — apply the two small changes described above. (Diff provided in Checkpoint 4 earlier.)

If you want, I can:
- Produce an exact patch file / git diff that you can apply.
- Run through other code locations to propose further defensive hardenings (e.g., other places that might call obj.__class__.__name__ for arbitrary user objects), but the minimal safe fix is confined to saferepr and is sufficient to prevent the INTERNALERROR reported.
compressed_analysis:
===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug summary: pytest raises an INTERNALERROR when formatting an exception if the object whose repr() is being rendered has broken/raising __repr__ or __getattribute__. The wrong assumption: fallback formatting in saferepr calls user-level operations (str()/repr() or instance attribute lookup) while already handling a repr failure.
- Root cause & location: unsafe fallback logic lives in src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py — specifically _try_repr_or_str and _format_repr_exception (used by SafeRepr.repr and SafeRepr.repr_instance). These functions invoke obj or obj.__class__.__name__, which can call user code.
- Fix strategy: stop calling instance-level operations when building the fallback. Replace uses of obj in formatted strings and obj.__class__.__name__ with safe alternatives that do not invoke user code (use type(obj).__name__ and id(obj)). Apply only in src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py; SafeRepr will continue to catch repr failures and use the hardened fallback.
- Hardcoded values to change: the fallback string '{}("{}")'.format(type(obj).__name__, obj) and the use of obj.__class__.__name__ in the final "<[... raised in repr()] ... object at 0x...>" format must be changed to avoid calling obj or doing instance attribute lookups.
- Methods to add/override: none — no new methods needed. Existing SafeRepr.repr and SafeRepr.repr_instance continue to be used; we only harden helper functions.
- Investigation gaps: the agent did not audit every other place in the codebase that uses obj.__class__.__name__ on potentially user-controlled objects; a quick repo search found other occurrences (e.g., src/_pytest/capture.py, src/_pytest/main.py) that should be checked to ensure they don't run on arbitrary user objects.

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: saferepr fallback invokes user code (root cause) (Checkpoint 1 & 2)
What: src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py::_try_repr_or_str and _format_repr_exception call into user-defined operations while formatting a failed repr. Why it matters: when repr(obj) fails, the fallback itself executes obj-dependent code and can raise a new exception (example: "RuntimeError: No active exception to reraise"), causing an INTERNALERROR instead of a safe representation.

### Finding 2: _try_repr_or_str fallback uses the object in format() (Checkpoint 2)
What: _try_repr_or_str currently does:
  return '{}("{}")'.format(type(obj).__name__, obj)
Why it matters: formatting with obj invokes obj.__format__/str(obj), which may call obj.__str__/__repr__ and/or __getattribute__, re-enter user code and raise again.

### Finding 3: _format_repr_exception reads obj.__class__.__name__ (Checkpoint 2)
What: _format_repr_exception uses obj.__class__.__name__ when building "<[... raised in repr()] ... object at 0x{:x}>". Why it matters: accessing obj.__class__ on an instance goes through instance attribute lookup which can be overridden via __getattribute__ and may invoke user code or raise.

### Finding 4: SafeRepr catches repr errors but relies on unsafe helper (Checkpoint 2 & 3)
What: SafeRepr.repr and SafeRepr.repr_instance catch BaseException and call _format_repr_exception(exc, x). Why it matters: the wrapper works only if _format_repr_exception is itself safe; currently it is not, so an exception in the fallback propagates and leads to INTERNALERROR.

### Finding 5: Proposed concrete fix (Checkpoint 4)
What: Replace unsafe fallbacks:
  - In _try_repr_or_str, return a safe string that does not include obj (use "{} object at 0x{:x}".format(type(obj).__name__, id(obj))). 
  - In _format_repr_exception, use type(obj).__name__ instead of obj.__class__.__name__.
Why it matters: these avoid any instance-level attribute access or calls to obj.__str__/__repr__, preventing re-entrancy into user code while formatting a failing repr.

### Finding 6: Remaining audit gap — other uses of instance-level lookups (Checkpoint 3)
What: The agent located other repo occurrences of "__class__.__name__" (e.g., src/_pytest/capture.py, src/_pytest/main.py) but did not determine whether those are invoked on arbitrary user objects. Why it matters: similar unsafe patterns elsewhere could produce similar INTERNALERRORs if they operate on user objects; they should be inspected to confirm safety.

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py:_try_repr_or_str
- Wrong assumption: The fallback assumes it is safe to include obj directly in a format string (i.e., calling str(obj)/__format__ will not invoke user code or raise).
- Evidence: Current code (exact text):
  def _try_repr_or_str(obj):
      try:
          return repr(obj)
      except (KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit):
          raise
      except BaseException:
          return '{}("{}")'.format(type(obj).__name__, obj)
  (file: src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py, lines 6–12 per inspection)
  The format expression passes obj into .format(), calling obj.__format__/str(obj).
- Related locations: _format_repr_exception (uses _try_repr_or_str on exception objects), SafeRepr.repr and SafeRepr.repr_instance rely on this helper when repr() fails.

(Found in Checkpoint 2 and fix proposed in Checkpoint 4.)

### Location: src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py:_format_repr_exception
- Wrong assumption: The code assumes reading obj.__class__.__name__ is safe and does not invoke instance-level lookup or user code.
- Evidence: Current code (exact text):
  def _format_repr_exception(exc: BaseException, obj: Any) -> str:
      try:
          exc_info = _try_repr_or_str(exc)
      except (KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit):
          raise
      except BaseException as exc:
          exc_info = "unpresentable exception ({})".format(_try_repr_or_str(exc))
      return "<[{} raised in repr()] {} object at 0x{:x}>".format(
          exc_info, obj.__class__.__name__, id(obj)
      )
  (file: src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py, lines 15–24)
  Accessing obj.__class__ triggers instance attribute lookup which may call user-defined __getattribute__ and raise (exact reproduction in bug report).
- Related locations: called by SafeRepr.repr and SafeRepr.repr_instance; tests in testing/io/test_saferepr.py expect output of this formatting, so change will preserve external shape but avoid unsafe instance access.

(Found in Checkpoint 2; fix proposed in Checkpoint 4.)

### Location: src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py:SafeRepr.repr and SafeRepr.repr_instance
- Wrong assumption: These methods correctly assume that _format_repr_exception returns a safe string; they do not guard against _format_repr_exception itself raising.
- Evidence: Current code (exact text excerpts):
  class SafeRepr(reprlib.Repr):
      def repr(self, x: Any) -> str:
          try:
              s = super().repr(x)
          except (KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit):
              raise
          except BaseException as exc:
              s = _format_repr_exception(exc, x)
          return _ellipsize(s, self.maxsize)
      def repr_instance(self, x: Any, level: int) -> str:
          try:
              s = repr(x)
          except (KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit):
              raise
          except BaseException as exc:
              s = _format_repr_exception(exc, x)
          return _ellipsize(s, self.maxsize)
  (file: src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py, lines ~45–60)
  If _format_repr_exception performs instance-level ops (it does), SafeRepr may receive another exception and fail to produce a repr.
- Related locations: many places call saferepr()/SafeRepr in _pytest/_code/code.py (e.g., ExceptionInfo.__repr__, Traceback formatting) — these are the callers that surface the INTERNALERROR.

(Found in Checkpoint 2 and highlighted in Checkpoint 3.)

--- End of evidence locker.

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness check

I will verify the draft report's claims against the source and the investigation analysis. I used the code-inspection tools to confirm the exact code for the functions and to locate other occurrences of the same pattern in the repo. Below I document why I called each tool, what I expected to learn, and what the tool returned and implies.

1) Inspect saferepr implementation to verify claims about the helpers and SafeRepr
- Reason for tool call: The draft names specific functions and lines in src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py (_try_repr_or_str, _format_repr_exception, SafeRepr.repr, SafeRepr.repr_instance). I needed to read the file to confirm the exact code and the presence of the problematic format usage and attribute access.
- Tool call: get_file_context for src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py (lines 1–103).
- What I expected: to see whether the fallback uses obj in .format(...) and whether the fallback uses obj.__class__.__name__.
- Result analysis: The file content confirms the draft's claims exactly. Relevant excerpts:
  - _try_repr_or_str:
    - Lines 6–12:
      def _try_repr_or_str(obj):
          try:
              return repr(obj)
          except (KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit):
              raise
          except BaseException:
              return '{}("{}")'.format(type(obj).__name__, obj)
    This shows the fallback includes obj as a format argument, which will call obj.__format__/str(obj) and may re-enter user code.
  - _format_repr_exception:
    - Lines 15–24:
      def _format_repr_exception(exc: BaseException, obj: Any) -> str:
          try:
              exc_info = _try_repr_or_str(exc)
          except (KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit):
              raise
          except BaseException as exc:
              exc_info = "unpresentable exception ({})".format(_try_repr_or_str(exc))
          return "<[{} raised in repr()] {} object at 0x{:x}>".format(
              exc_info, obj.__class__.__name__, id(obj)
          )
    This shows obj.__class__.__name__ is read while building the fallback string.
  - SafeRepr.repr and repr_instance:
    - Lines 45–61:
      def repr(self, x: Any) -> str:
          try:
              s = super().repr(x)
          except (KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit):
              raise
          except BaseException as exc:
              s = _format_repr_exception(exc, x)
          return _ellipsize(s, self.maxsize)
    
      def repr_instance(self, x: Any, level: int) -> str:
          try:
              s = repr(x)
          except (KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit):
              raise
          except BaseException as exc:
              s = _format_repr_exception(exc, x)
          return _ellipsize(s, self.maxsize)
    These methods catch repr failures and call _format_repr_exception; if that helper raises, the exception will propagate.

  - safeformat and saferepr also call these helpers:
    - Lines 69–73 and 75–82 show safeformat returns _format_repr_exception(exc, obj) on pprint failures; saferepr uses SafeRepr(...).repr(obj).

Conclusion: The draft's specific claims about the saferepr code are accurate and supported by exact code lines. The unsafe patterns (using obj in format and reading obj.__class__.__name__) exist as stated.

2) Check repository for other occurrences of __class__.__name__ (audit gap)
- Reason for tool call: The draft and investigation analysis mention an "audit gap" and list example occurrences (src/_pytest/capture.py, src/_pytest/main.py). I searched the codebase for "__class__.__name__" to confirm other occurrences and whether they represent the same risky pattern.
- Tool call: search_codebase for "__class__.__name__".
- What I expected: find files that use this attribute; I wanted to record them to evaluate whether they are used on arbitrary user objects or on internal pytest objects.
- Result analysis: The search returned multiple occurrences:
  - src/_pytest/_code/code.py:547
  - src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py:23 (already inspected)
  - src/_pytest/capture.py:544 and 633
  - src/_pytest/main.py:415
  - src/_pytest/nodes.py:178
  - src/_pytest/outcomes.py:48
  - src/_pytest/reports.py:267 and 348
  - plus some test files that assert on __class__.__name__ values.

I then inspected two cited files to understand context:

- src/_pytest/capture.py (lines around the occurrences):
  - The two occurrences at 544 and 633 are inside __repr__ implementations of FDCaptureBinary.__repr__ and SysCaptureBinary.__repr__. These format strings use self.__class__.__name__ but the 'self' here is an internal pytest capture object, not an arbitrary user object.
- src/_pytest/main.py (lines around 413–420):
  - __repr__ of the Session-class prints self.__class__.__name__, again applied to an internal pytest object.

Conclusion: the repository contains multiple uses of __class__.__name__; some are in pytest-internal objects (e.g., capture, main), where the attribute access is on internal instances. The draft's audit-gap statement was correct in noting other occurrences exist, but whether each occurrence is invoked on arbitrary user-controlled objects is context-dependent and requires manual review. The search confirms "other occurrences exist" (supporting the draft's audit gap note), but it does not prove all are vulnerable in the same way as the saferepr case.

3) Verify other claims from investigation analysis
- The investigation highlighted that SafeRepr's docstring promises saferepr "takes care to never raise exceptions itself." The code demonstrates that SafeRepr catches exceptions from repr but relies on helpers that perform instance-level operations (confirmed above), so the docstring's claim is inconsistent with the observed code behavior: _format_repr_exception can itself access instance attributes and therefore potentially raise.
- The investigation also noted that _try_repr_or_str passes type(obj).__name__ and obj to format; code confirms this.

4) Check for unsupported or overstated claims in the draft
- The draft's RootCause item (4) stated "Audit gap in repository - other modules (example occurrences found: src/_pytest/capture.py, src/_pytest/main.py) use obj.__class__.__name__ on potentially user-controlled objects; these locations were not fully audited and may encode the same unsafe assumption." This is partly supported: these files do use __class__.__name__, but in the inspected contexts they access it on pytest-internal 'self' objects. It is therefore accurate to call out an "audit gap" (other occurrences exist), but claiming they are "on potentially user-controlled objects" is not verified. I will soften the language to state they are occurrences that should be audited rather than asserting they are definitely operating on user objects.
- The draft's RootCause statements about unsafe assumptions in saferepr are fully supported by the code.

5) Completeness check against the investigation's secondary findings
- The investigation called out that _try_repr_or_str, _format_repr_exception, and SafeRepr methods are all part of the same root cause. The draft includes those; I verified the file and will preserve each finding with equal weight and exact locations.
- The investigation noted calls into saferepr from other modules (e.g., _code/code.py). The search found src/_pytest/_code/code.py using saferepr in traceback formatting (search result shows saferepr(self._excinfo[1]) nearby). This confirms callers exist that will surface the issue to user-visible test reporting. I will ensure _code/code.py is included in evidence and mention it as a caller in the analysis (but not in RootCause as a location encoding the unsafe assumption — RootCause should only list the locations that encode the incorrect assumptions).

6) Field discipline checks
- Description: The draft's Description is concise and diagnostic-focused; it does not prescribe fixes. I will keep it mostly unchanged but make it slightly crisper.
- RootCause: The draft's RootCause named multiple findings but included some prescriptive wording and uneven emphasis. I will rewrite RootCause to be a focused list of incorrect assumptions and their exact code locations. Each finding will be a short statement: the incorrect assumption and the file:line(s) where it is encoded. I will preserve every finding from the investigation analysis and give them equal weight.
- ExpectedBehavior and ObservedBehavior: The draft's ObservedBehavior is accurate; ExpectedBehavior must describe only the user-visible correct behavior. I will keep them concise and focused.

Summary of required edits
- Confirmed and kept the saferepr-related findings; will quote exact lines in evidence.
- Softened the audit-gap language to avoid claiming other modules are definitely vulnerable; will state that multiple other occurrences of __class__.__name__ were found and should be audited.
- Rewrote RootCause to be diagnostic-only: list the incorrect assumptions and exact file locations with equal weight.
- Preserved StepsToReproduce, ExpectedBehavior, and ObservedBehavior but made small wording adjustments for clarity and discipline.

bug_report.Title: INTERNALERROR when formatting exception if object has failing __repr__/__getattribute__
bug_report.Description: When pytest formats an exception and the object whose repr() is being rendered has a broken __repr__ or __getattribute__ that raises, pytest can raise an INTERNALERROR instead of producing a safe representation. The unsafe behavior arises while building a fallback representation in src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py::_try_repr_or_str — incorrect assumption: including the object instance as a format argument is safe when repr(obj) fails. The fallback uses '{}("{}")'.format(type(obj).__name__, obj) (see file lines 6–12), which passes obj into .format() and thus invokes obj.__format__/str(obj) (potentially calling user-defined __repr__/__getattribute__) and can raise while formatting the fallback.

2) src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py::_format_repr_exception — incorrect assumption: accessing obj.__class__.__name__ is a safe, attribute-free operation. The helper composes "<[{} raised in repr()] {} object at 0x{:x}>" using obj.__class__.__name__ (see file lines 15–24). Accessing obj.__class__ performs instance attribute lookup and may invoke user-defined __getattribute__ and raise.

3) src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py:SafeRepr.repr and SafeRepr.repr_instance — incorrect assumption: the formatting helper _format_repr_exception cannot itself raise. These methods catch exceptions from repr() and call _format_repr_exception(exc, x) (see file lines 45–61); because that helper performs instance-level operations, a second exception can escape and cause an INTERNALERROR instead of producing a safe repr.

4) Repository occurrences of the same pattern (audit gap) — other files contain uses of __class__.__name__ or similar instance-level attribute access that should be reviewed to determine whether they operate on arbitrary user objects and therefore encode the same unsafe assumption. Example occurrences found by repository search include: src/_pytest/_code/code.py (uses saferepr on exception objects), src/_pytest/capture.py, src/_pytest/main.py, src/_pytest/nodes.py, src/_pytest/outcomes.py, src/_pytest/reports.py. These occurrences were not exhaustively audited and merit review to confirm whether they are invoked on user-controlled objects.
StepsToReproduce: Create a test file containing:
class SomeClass:
def __getattribute__(self, attr):
raise
def __repr__(self):
raise
def test():
SomeClass().attr
Run pytest on the file (e.g., `pytest -q`).
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: Pytest should not raise an INTERNALERROR while formatting the exception. It should present a safe representation indicating that repr() failed (for example a short placeholder mentioning the type and id) and continue with normal test reporting, displaying the original exception that caused the failure.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Pytest raises an INTERNALERROR while building the exception representation. The traceback shows saferepr helpers invoking formatting or instance attribute access that re-enters user code (e.g., via obj.__format__ or obj.__getattribute__), leading to a secondary exception (for example "RuntimeError: No active exception to reraise") and preventing normal test reporting.

method_cache.src/_pytest/_code/code.py:
def exconly(self, tryshort: bool = False) -> str:
        """ return the exception as a string

            when 'tryshort' resolves to True, and the exception is a
            _pytest._code._AssertionError, only the actual exception part of
            the exception representation is returned (so 'AssertionError: ' is
            removed from the beginning)
        """
        lines = format_exception_only(self.type, self.value)
        text = "".join(lines)
        text = text.rstrip()
        if tryshort:
            if text.startswith(self._striptext):
                text = text[len(self._striptext) :]
        return text

    def errisinstance(
        self, exc: Union["Type[BaseException]", Tuple["Type[BaseException]", ...]]
    ) -> bool:
        """ return True if the exception is an instance of exc """
        return isinstance(self.value, exc)

    def _getreprcrash(self) -> "ReprFileLocation":
        exconly = self.exconly(tryshort=True)
        entry = self.traceback.getcrashentry()
        path, lineno = entry.frame.code.raw.co_filename, entry.lineno
        return ReprFileLocation(path, lineno + 1, exconly)

    def getrepr(
        self,
        showlocals: bool = False,
        style: "_TracebackStyle" = "long",
        abspath: bool = False,
        tbfilter: bool = True,
        funcargs: bool = False,
        truncate_locals: bool = True,
        chain: bool = True,
    ) -> Union["ReprExceptionInfo", "ExceptionChainRepr"]:
        """
        Return str()able representation of this exception info.

        :param bool showlocals:
            Show locals per traceback entry.
            Ignored if ``style=="native"``.

        :param str style: long|short|no|native|value traceback style

        :param bool abspath:
            If paths should be changed to absolute or left unchanged.

        :param bool tbfilter:
            Hide entries that contain a local variable ``__tracebackhide__==True``.
            Ignored if ``style=="native"``.

        :param bool funcargs:
            Show fixtures ("funcargs" for legacy purposes) per traceback entry.

        :param bool truncate_locals:
            With ``showlocals==True``, make sure locals can be safely represented as strings.

        :param bool chain: if chained exceptions in Python 3 should be shown.

        .. versionchanged:: 3.9

            Added the ``chain`` parameter.
        """
        if style == "native":
            return ReprExceptionInfo(
                ReprTracebackNative(
                    traceback.format_exception(
                        self.type, self.value, self.traceback[0]._rawentry
                    )
                ),
                self._getreprcrash(),
            )

        fmt = FormattedExcinfo(
            showlocals=showlocals,
            style=style,
            abspath=abspath,
            tbfilter=tbfilter,
            funcargs=funcargs,
            truncate_locals=truncate_locals,
            chain=chain,
        )
        return fmt.repr_excinfo(self)

    def match(self, regexp: "Union[str, Pattern]") -> "Literal[True]":
        """
        Check whether the regular expression `regexp` matches the string
        representation of the exception using :func:`python:re.search`.
        If it matches `True` is returned.
        If it doesn't match an `AssertionError` is raised.
        """
        __tracebackhide__ = True
        assert re.search(
            regexp, str(self.value)
        ), "Pattern {!r} does not match {!r}".format(regexp, str(self.value))
        # Return True to allow for "assert excinfo.match()".
        return True


@attr.s
class FormattedExcinfo:
    """ presenting information about failing Functions and Generators. """

    # for traceback entries
    flow_marker = ">"
    fail_marker = "E"

    showlocals = attr.ib(type=bool, default=False)
    style = attr.ib(type="_TracebackStyle", default="long")
    abspath = attr.ib(type=bool, default=True)
    tbfilter = attr.ib(type=bool, default=True)
    funcargs = attr.ib(type=bool, default=False)
    truncate_locals = attr.ib(type=bool, default=True)
    chain = attr.ib(type=bool, default=True)
    astcache = attr.ib(default=attr.Factory(dict), init=False, repr=False)

    def _getindent(self, source: "Source") -> int:
        # figure out indent for given source
        try:
            s = str(source.getstatement(len(source) - 1))
        except KeyboardInterrupt:
            raise
        except BaseException:
            try:
                s = str(source[-1])
            except KeyboardInterrupt:
                raise
            except BaseException:
                return 0
        return 4 + (len(s) - len(s.lstrip()))

    def _getentrysource(self, entry: TracebackEntry) -> Optional["Source"]:
        source = entry.getsource(self.astcache)
        if source is not None:
            source = source.deindent()
        return source

    def repr_args(self, entry: TracebackEntry) -> Optional["ReprFuncArgs"]:
        if self.funcargs:
            args = []
            for argname, argvalue in entry.frame.getargs(var=True):
                args.append((argname, saferepr(argvalue)))
            return ReprFuncArgs(args)
        return None

    def get_source(
        self,
        source: "Source",
        line_index: int = -1,
        excinfo: Optional[ExceptionInfo] = None,
        short: bool = False,
    ) -> List[str]:
        """ return formatted and marked up source lines. """

We will need to update at least nodes.Node._repr_failure_py and config.notify_exception to pass the invocation startdir.

Checkpoint 4: Propose a fix, then challenge it

Proposed fix (high level)
- Change FormattedExcinfo._makepath to use a configurable base path instead of py.path.local() (current working dir). Add an optional parameter to ExceptionInfo.getrepr called startpath (or basepath) and thread it into FormattedExcinfo. If startpath is provided, use startpath.bestrelpath(path) as the way to compute a short relative path; otherwise, fall back to the existing behavior (py.path.local().bestrelpath(path)). Then update callers that have the invocation directory available to pass it:
  - nodes.Node._repr_failure_py should call excinfo.getrepr(..., startpath=self.session.startdir)
  - config.Config.notify_exception should call excinfo.getrepr(..., startpath=self.invocation_dir)
  - Possibly update other call sites that have a useful startdir; but the parameter is optional so existing usages keep current behavior.

Concrete code changes (patch outline)

1) src/_pytest/_code/code.py

- Change ExceptionInfo.getrepr signature to accept startpath: Optional[py.path.local] = None
- Pass startpath into FormattedExcinfo constructor:

    fmt = FormattedExcinfo(
        showlocals=showlocals,
        style=style,
        abspath=abspath,
        tbfilter=tbfilter,
        funcargs=funcargs,
        truncate_locals=truncate_locals,
        chain=chain,
        startpath=startpath,
    )

- Add a new attribute to FormattedExcinfo:

    startpath = attr.ib(default=None)

- Modify _makepath to use startpath if present:

    def _makepath(self, path):
        if not self.abspath:
            try:
                if self.startpath is not None:
                    np = self.startpath.bestrelpath(path)
                else:
                    np = py.path.local().bestrelpath(path)
            except OSError:
                return path
            if len(np) < len(str(path)):
                path = np
        return path

Notes:
- The parameter name 'startpath' is used to match Session.startdir / Config.invocation_dir, which are py.path.local objects.
- Keep default behavior (startpath None) to preserve backward compatibility for other call sites that don't have an invocation dir (e.g., early exceptions during config parsing).

2) src/_pytest/nodes.py

- Update Node._repr_failure_py call to excinfo.getrepr to pass startpath=self.session.startdir:

Now answer the required checkpoints 4a–4e in detail.

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- Current assumption: traceback file paths should be shortened relative to the current process working directory (py.path.local()), which may have been changed by the test/fixture.
- New assumption (after fix): when available, use the original invocation directory (session.startdir / config.invocation_dir) as the base for producing relative paths in tracebacks; only fall back to current working directory when no invocation dir is available.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- The hardcoded use of py.path.local() in FormattedExcinfo._makepath — this explicitly uses current working directory as the base for bestrelpath. Under the new assumption, py.path.local() is not correct when tests change CWD; it must be replaced by a dynamic base (startpath) which is supplied by callers that know the invocation directory (session or config).

  - terminal.py uses self.startdir.bestrelpath(...) (already correct).
- Conclusion: the specific hardcoded value to change is the reliance on py.path.local() inside _makepath. It must be made dynamic (use startpath when provided).

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The reverse operation would be: editors/IDEs take the displayed relative path and join it with some directory to find the file (the editor's CWD or workspace). If pytest outputs relative path "test_path_error.py:29", editors typically resolve that relative to the project root (the directory where pytest was invoked). Previously, because pytest used current working directory, editors would attempt to open file at path starting from their own project root and might fail because pytest displayed it relative to some nested "ddd" directory. By changing FormattedExcinfo to use startpath=self.session.startdir, the reverse operation (editor resolving relative paths) will succeed: editors can join the displayed path with the known project root to find the file.

  - Parent methods that perform the complementary operation: none in code.py. But Node.location uses session._node_location_to_relpath which uses config.rootdir. That continues to produce node locations relative to the repository root.
  - For every inherited method that performs complementary operations: we checked Node._repr_failure_py (caller) — we now pass startpath=self.session.startdir so the formatting uses startdir.
- Concretely: ExceptionInfo.getrepr previously used py.path.local().bestrelpath internally; we now let callers pass startpath (invocation dir). The reverse operation (editor interpreting displayed path) is fixed by making the displayed path relative to the invocation directory rather than the current working directory changed by test code. No other complementary operations need an override because getrepr is still available in other contexts and defaults to existing behavior if startpath is not supplied.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- FormattedExcinfo methods:
  - All methods remain unchanged except _makepath. We only added an optional startpath attribute and used it instead of py.path.local() when provided. This is backward compatible: when startpath is None, behavior is unchanged.
- ExceptionInfo.getrepr:

- Node._repr_failure_py:

- Config.notify_exception:

- Other methods calling getrepr without startpath continue to get the prior behavior (bestrelpath relative to current cwd). Those cases are generally early boot errors where we don't have an invocation dir available (or the current cwd might be desired), so leaving default is acceptable.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new methods are required. We added a new optional parameter to ExceptionInfo.getrepr and a new attribute on FormattedExcinfo (startpath) to carry the base. Because the parameter is optional (defaults to None), no callers are forced to change. We updated the two key callers that do have the correct base available (Node._repr_failure_py and Config.notify_exception) so they pass startpath and formatting will use it. No additional override methods are necessary.

Edge cases and further considerations:
- The code still checks abspath flag in Node._repr_failure_py based on whether os.getcwd() is accessible. That logic remains unchanged. The only change is the base for path shortening when abspath is False and startpath is provided.
- The change is minimal, targeted, and backward compatible.
- This also harmonizes well with terminal.py's use of startdir when showing other path references — now tracebacks will be shown relative to the same invocation directory as terminal reporting.

- _makepath does not have access to Session or Config. It's best to keep FormattedExcinfo generic and accept a startpath from callers. Some call sites (early config parsing) don't have a Config/Session; default behavior should be preserved there, so we keep a fallback to py.path.local().

Now: respond to each required Checkpoint 4 subquestion explicitly with reference to code read

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- The current code assumes it's correct to shorten traceback file paths relative to the process current working directory (py.path.local()) at formatting time; my fix changes that assumption to "when available, use the original pytest invocation directory (session.startdir / config.invocation_dir) as the base for relative paths shown in tracebacks."

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- The specific hardcoded value is the use of py.path.local() inside FormattedExcinfo._makepath (code.py line ~794 in this commit). py.path.local() is the process current working directory. If the assumption (that current CWD is the right base) is no longer true, this value is not correct and must be made dynamic; that's exactly why we add startpath to FormattedExcinfo and use startpath.bestrelpath instead.
- I searched for other occurrences of bestrelpath: main.Session._bestrelpathcache uses config.rootdir (which remains correct), and terminal.py uses self.startdir.bestrelpath (already correct). So no other hardcoded constants need to be changed.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The complementary operation is editors/IDEs taking the displayed relative path and resolving it to an actual file to open. They typically do so relative to the project root (invocation directory). Before the fix, pytest printed relative paths relative to the test-modified current working directory, so editors could not find the files. After the fix, Node._repr_failure_py passes session.startdir into excinfo.getrepr, so FormattedExcinfo._makepath will produce paths relative to invocation dir (startdir), which is what editors expect and so the reverse operation (editor resolving displayed relative path to actual file) succeeds.
- Concrete trace through parent's methods:
  - Parent method: Node._repr_failure_py calls excinfo.getrepr(...). Previously getrepr used current cwd via py.path.local() and produced string path = "../test_path_error.py". Editor could not resolve to the actual test file when opening from project root.
  - After change: Node._repr_failure_py provides startpath=self.session.startdir (a py.path.local pointing at invocation_dir), so FormattedExcinfo._makepath uses startpath.bestrelpath(path) and produces "test_path_error.py". Editor joining that with project root opens the correct file.
- We also updated config.notify_exception to pass self.invocation_dir so internal error reporting uses a stable base.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- Yes.
  - ExceptionInfo.getrepr: signature extended with startpath optional parameter — old callers unaffected.
  - FormattedExcinfo methods: _makepath now optionally uses self.startpath; if startpath is None behavior is unchanged. No other methods that rely on _makepath are impacted.
  - Node._repr_failure_py now passes a new optional arg; the rest of the method is unchanged.
  - Config.notify_exception now passes invocation_dir; unchanged semantics otherwise.

I went through each method in FormattedExcinfo used in traceback formatting — they all still behave correctly because the only change is to the _makepath shortening base (it is now controlled by the new attribute).

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new methods are needed. We added an optional parameter to getrepr and a new attribute on FormattedExcinfo, which are sufficient. Because default behavior remains unchanged if startpath is omitted, we do not need to add wrapper functions or extra interfaces elsewhere.

Checkpoint 5: Final report (complete analysis and exact fixes)

Root cause
- The traceback formatting (FormattedExcinfo._makepath) used the current working directory (via py.path.local().bestrelpath) as the base for making paths relative. Tests/fixtures that change the current working directory cause pytest to print file paths relative to that new directory, which is not what editors/IDEs expect (they assume paths are relative to the pytest invocation directory or are absolute). This produced the wrong string "../test_path_error.py:29" instead of "test_path_error.py:29".

Complete fix (all affected locations)
1) src/_pytest/_code/code.py (primary fix)
- Change ExceptionInfo.getrepr signature to accept an optional startpath parameter (py.path.local):
  - def getrepr(..., chain: bool = True, startpath: Optional[py.path.local] = None) -> ...
- Pass startpath into FormattedExcinfo on construction:
  - fmt = FormattedExcinfo(..., startpath=startpath)
- Add a new attribute to FormattedExcinfo:
  - startpath = attr.ib(default=None)
- Update FormattedExcinfo._makepath to use startpath.bestrelpath(path) if startpath is not None, else fallback to py.path.local().bestrelpath(path):
  - if self.startpath is not None:
        np = self.startpath.bestrelpath(path)
    else:
        np = py.path.local().bestrelpath(path)
- Rationale: make the path-shortening base configurable; callers that know the real invocation directory can pass it; others will get the old behavior.

2) src/_pytest/nodes.py
- Update Node._repr_failure_py to pass startpath=self.session.startdir into excinfo.getrepr:
  - return excinfo.getrepr(..., truncate_locals=truncate_locals, startpath=self.session.startdir)
- Rationale: the Session has startdir == config.invocation_dir, which is the directory pytest was invoked from. Using that as the base for relative paths is the expected behavior for displayed file paths.

3) src/_pytest/config/__init__.py
- Update Config.notify_exception to pass startpath=self.invocation_dir when it calls excinfo.getrepr:
  - excrepr = excinfo.getrepr(..., style=style, startpath=self.invocation_dir)
- Rationale: keep internal error reporting consistent and usable by editors/tools.

Why this fixes the original bug
- With these changes, traceback entries are shortened relative to the pytest invocation directory when that directory is available, rather than the current working directory which can be changed by test code. This ensures that printed file paths remain valid for editors/IDEs which expect paths relative to the project root or absolute paths.

Compatibility and safety
- getrepr gains a new optional parameter with a default of None; code that calls getrepr without startpath will keep the previous fallback behavior (shortening relative to current working directory), so backward compatibility is maintained.
- Only call sites that can and should supply a stable base (Session/Config) are updated.

Further notes / possible follow-ups
- There might be additional callers of getrepr introduced elsewhere in plugins or tests; since the new parameter is optional the change is backwards compatible, but tests/plugins that care about the base path might prefer to pass a startpath if they have one.
- I intentionally did not change the logic that sets abspath in nodes.Node._repr_failure_py (it checks os.getcwd() availability). The new change only replaces the base used for shortening when abspath is False and a startpath is provided.

Summary of code-level diffs (recap)
- src/_pytest/_code/code.py:
  - Add `startpath` optional argument to ExceptionInfo.getrepr and pass it into FormattedExcinfo.
  - Add `startpath` attribute to FormattedExcinfo and use it in _makepath.
- src/_pytest/nodes.py:
  - Pass `startpath=self.session.startdir` when calling excinfo.getrepr in Node._repr_failure_py.
- src/_pytest/config/__init__.py:
  - Pass `startpath=self.invocation_dir` when calling excinfo.getrepr in Config.notify_exception.

Why this is a COMPLETE fix
- It touches every place that: (a) formats tracebacks, and (b) calls getrepr where session/config are present and know the invocation directory. The key bug is that _makepath used py.path.local() — this single change plus passing the correct startpath from the two primary call sites (Node._repr_failure_py and Config.notify_exception) makes traceback display stable with respect to the invocation dir for regular test failures and internal error reporting. No other files need to be changed to fix the reported failure.

If you want, I can:
- Produce an actual patch file (unified diff) you can apply,
- Run through a few example traces and show before/after formatted outputs,
- Or search for any other call sites of getrepr and suggest whether they should pass startpath too.
compressed_analysis:
===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug: pytest formats traceback file paths relative to the process current working directory (py.path.local()) at failure time, so when a fixture changes cwd the displayed path becomes incorrect for editors (e.g. "../test_path_error.py:29" instead of "test_path_error.py:29").
- Root cause (where it lives): src/_pytest/_code/code.py, class FormattedExcinfo, method _makepath — it calls py.path.local().bestrelpath(path) and thus uses the runtime CWD as the base for path-shortening. (Checkpoint 1)
- Proposed fix: make the shortening base configurable. Add an optional startpath parameter to ExceptionInfo.getrepr(...), thread it into FormattedExcinfo (new attribute startpath), and change _makepath to use startpath.bestrelpath(path) when provided; update callers that have the invocation dir to pass it (Node._repr_failure_py → session.startdir, Config.notify_exception → invocation_dir). This fixes all visible traceback formatting without breaking backward compatibility (startpath is optional). (Checkpoint 4)
- Hardcoded constant to change: the direct use of py.path.local() inside FormattedExcinfo._makepath (exact code: np = py.path.local().bestrelpath(path)). Replaceable by startpath.bestrelpath(path). (Checkpoint 2)
- API/behavior changes: no new public method needed; add an optional parameter to ExceptionInfo.getrepr (startpath) and an attribute on FormattedExcinfo; update two internal call sites to pass the invocation dir. Existing callers keep current behavior because the parameter defaults to None. (Checkpoint 4)
- Gaps / things not fully traced: investigation did not run the code with concrete filesystem values (no runtime verification); plugins or third-party callers of getrepr were not exhaustively scanned (we found the main call sites but plugins could call getrepr). These are noted as remaining checks before commit. (Checkpoint 4)

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: Traceback path shortening uses current CWD (root cause)
What: src/_pytest/_code/code.py — class FormattedExcinfo, method _makepath contains the line "np = py.path.local().bestrelpath(path)". This makes traceback entries be shortened relative to the process CWD, so tests that chdir() cause displayed paths to be relative to that transient directory. Why it matters: editors expect paths relative to the pytest invocation directory, not a test's mutated cwd. (Found while reading code.py) (Checkpoint 1)

### Finding 2: Caller doesn't supply a stable base path
What: src/_pytest/nodes.py — method _repr_failure_py calls excinfo.getrepr(...) but does not pass any invocation/startdir argument (no base path). Why it matters: the traceback formatter has no knowledge of the invocation directory available on the Session (session.startdir) and therefore falls back to current CWD. (Found inspecting nodes.py where getrepr is invoked) (Checkpoint 1 & 3)

### Finding 3: Terminal reporter already uses invocation dir (sibling correct behavior)
What: src/_pytest/terminal.py uses self.startdir.bestrelpath(fspath) when printing other paths. Why it matters: terminal reporter is already using config.invocation_dir/session.startdir as the correct base, proving the intended UX is to relativize to invocation dir; the traceback formatter should match that. (Found inspecting terminal.py) (Checkpoint 3)

### Finding 4: Minimal, backward-compatible fix sketched
What: Add optional parameter startpath to ExceptionInfo.getrepr(...), add attr startpath to FormattedExcinfo, change _makepath to use startpath.bestrelpath(path) if startpath provided, else fallback to py.path.local().bestrelpath(path). Then change callers that know the invocation dir: pass startpath=self.session.startdir from Node._repr_failure_py and startpath=self.invocation_dir from Config.notify_exception. Why it matters: fixes the bug where it matters, keeps existing behavior where caller cannot provide a base, and matches terminal reporter behavior. (Proposed change) (Checkpoint 4)

### Finding 5: Investigation gaps and compatibility notes
What: I searched for getrepr(...) call sites and found the primary ones (nodes._repr_failure_py and config.notify_exception) and one early config error call without a config object; however I did not exhaustively search plugin code or third-party usage of getrepr, and I did not run unit tests to confirm. Why it matters: plugins could call getrepr and expect the old behavior, but the new parameter is optional so compatibility is preserved; still recommend running test suite and searching plugin APIs. (Checkpoint 4)

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: src/_pytest/_code/code.py:FormattedExcinfo._makepath
- **Wrong assumption**: It assumes the right base for shortening paths is the current process working directory by calling py.path.local().bestrelpath(path).
- **Evidence**: exact line found in code.py: "np = py.path.local().bestrelpath(path)" (code inspection). This produces "../test_path_error.py" when the test fixture chdir()'d into 'ddd'.
- **Related locations**: ExceptionInfo.getrepr (calls/formats into FormattedExcinfo); nodes._repr_failure_py (caller that doesn't pass any base); terminal.py (sibling code that already uses startdir.bestrelpath).

  With:

    else:
        np = py.path.local().bestrelpath(path)
- Also add new attribute on FormattedExcinfo: startpath (type: py.path.local or None), and pass startpath through from ExceptionInfo.getrepr(..., startpath=...).

(Discovery recorded in Checkpoint 1 and elaborated in Checkpoint 4)

---

### Location: src/_pytest/main.py:Session.startdir and _bestrelpathcache usage
- **Wrong assumption (highlighted as context)**: node locations are already computed relative to config.rootdir via Session._bestrelpathcache; terminal reporter uses startdir.bestrelpath. The inconsistent assumption in _makepath (current CWD) is therefore the anomaly, not Session/main behavior.
- **Evidence**: exact attributes:
    self.startdir = config.invocation_dir
    self._bestrelpathcache = _bestrelpath_cache(config.rootdir)
  (found in main.py).
- **Related locations**: src/_pytest/terminal.py (uses startdir.bestrelpath), src/_pytest/_code/code.py (should match this behavior).

This demonstrates which stable base we should pass into the ExceptionInfo.getrepr call sites (use session.startdir / config.invocation_dir). (Checkpoint 3 & 4)

---

GAPS / remaining checks (evidence locker):
- I found the main call sites of getrepr and updated the two that have a reliable invocation dir. I did not exhaustively search third-party plugins for direct getrepr uses; because getrepr's new parameter is optional, backward compatibility is preserved, but recommend a quick repository-wide search for ".getrepr(" in plugin code (or run tests). (Checkpoint 4)

- I did not execute the code to confirm produced strings in a running environment (no runtime validation). Recommend running the pytest test-suite or reproducer after applying the above diffs to verify display output changes (expected: "test_path_error.py:29" instead of "../test_path_error.py:29"). (Checkpoint 4)

Clarified and tightened RootCause wording to list each finding and location with equal weight and to avoid implementation prescriptions.
Left StepsToReproduce, ExpectedBehavior, and ObservedBehavior focused on user-visible behavior and removed implementation details from those fields.
reviewer_evidence: src/_pytest/_code/code.py: FormattedExcinfo._makepath uses py.path.local().bestrelpath(path) — see lines around 791-795 where np = py.path.local().bestrelpath(path) is invoked.
src/_pytest/_code/code.py: ExceptionInfo.getrepr signature has no startpath parameter — see getrepr signature and the construction of FormattedExcinfo at lines ~569-626.
src/_pytest/nodes.py: Node._repr_failure_py calls excinfo.getrepr(...) without any startpath/invocation dir parameter — see the call at lines ~370-377.
src/_pytest/config/__init__.py: Config.notify_exception calls excinfo.getrepr(...) with no invocation/startpath parameter — see notify_exception at lines ~894-901.

src/_pytest/config/__init__.py: early ConftestImportFailure handling calls exc_info.getrepr(style='short', chain=False) without a startpath — see main()/ConftestImportFailure handling at lines ~108-123.
src/_pytest/terminal.py: terminal reporter relativizes paths using self.startdir.bestrelpath(fspath) — see write_fspath_result at lines ~336-346, demonstrating the intended behavior.
src/_pytest/main.py: Session.__init__ sets self.startdir = config.invocation_dir (line ~386), showing a stable invocation directory is available in the session.

bug_report.Title: Wrong path to test file when directory changed in fixture
bug_report.Description: When a fixture (or code under test) changes the process working directory, pytest formats traceback file paths relative to that transient CWD. The failure lines shown in the test output become relative to the mutated directory (for example "../test_path_error.py:29") instead of the pytest invocation directory, preventing editors from jumping to the correct file location.
bug_report.RootCause:
Multiple pieces of core formatting code assume the appropriate base for producing shortened/relative file paths is the current process working directory (the runtime CWD), and there is no existing mechanism in ExceptionInfo.getrepr/FormattedExcinfo for callers to provide the pytest invocation directory as an explicit base. These facts combine to cause traceback lines to be relativized against the transient CWD when tests or fixtures call os.chdir(). The relevant, equally-weighted findings are:

1) FormattedExcinfo._makepath shortens file paths using the process CWD as the base (src/_pytest/_code/code.py). The implementation calls py.path.local().bestrelpath(path) when abspath is False, which uses the current working directory for relativity. (See FormattedExcinfo._makepath where np = py.path.local().bestrelpath(path) is invoked.)

2) ExceptionInfo.getrepr currently has no parameter to accept a caller-supplied invocation/base path (src/_pytest/_code/code.py). The getrepr signature accepts showlocals, style, abspath, tbfilter, funcargs, truncate_locals, chain — there is no startpath or equivalent argument that would allow a caller to specify the stable pytest invocation directory as the base for path-shortening.

3) Core call sites that format exceptions call getrepr without any way to supply an invocation base (all call sites below call getrepr with no base argument):
   - Node._repr_failure_py (src/_pytest/nodes.py): calls excinfo.getrepr(...) without supplying any invocation/startpath parameter.
   - Config.notify_exception (src/_pytest/config/__init__.py): calls excinfo.getrepr(...) when reporting internal errors, with no invocation/startpath parameter.
   - ConftestImportFailure handling during early config in main (src/_pytest/config/__init__.py): exc_info.getrepr(style="short", chain=False) is called without a startpath.

4) A stable base for relativizing paths is available elsewhere in core: the session/config invocation directory is stored as session.startdir / config.invocation_dir (src/_pytest/main.py), and the terminal reporter already relativizes file system paths against that base (src/_pytest/terminal.py uses self.startdir.bestrelpath(fspath)). This demonstrates the intended UX: shorten paths relative to the pytest invocation directory rather than the transient process CWD.

Net effect: because the formatted-exception code shortens paths relative to py.path.local() (runtime CWD) and callers have no API-surface to provide the invocation directory, failure output shown by pytest can display file paths relative to a directory changed during test execution, producing paths (like "../test_path_error.py:29") that are incorrect/unusable for editors expecting paths relative to the pytest invocation directory.
StepsToReproduce: Create the file test_path_error.py in the project root using the provided fixture that creates and chdirs into directory 'ddd' and a failing test (as in the original report).
From the project root, run: pytest

Observe the failure output: the traceback line shows the test file path relative to the fixture-changed CWD (for example "../test_path_error.py:29: AssertionError") instead of relative to the pytest invocation directory ("test_path_error.py:29: AssertionError").
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: When a test fails, pytest should display traceback file paths relative to the pytest invocation directory (the session/config invocation dir). For the provided repro, the failure line should read "test_path_error.py:29: AssertionError" so editors can open the file directly from the output.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: When a test or fixture changes the process working directory, pytest displays failure file paths relative to that transient CWD (for example "../test_path_error.py:29: AssertionError"), preventing editors from locating the file by the shown path.

method_cache.src/_pytest/skipping.py:
""" support for skip/xfail functions and markers. """
from typing import Optional
from typing import Tuple

from _pytest.config import Config
from _pytest.config import hookimpl
from _pytest.config.argparsing import Parser
from _pytest.mark.evaluate import MarkEvaluator
from _pytest.nodes import Item
from _pytest.outcomes import fail
from _pytest.outcomes import skip
from _pytest.outcomes import xfail
from _pytest.python import Function
from _pytest.reports import BaseReport
from _pytest.runner import CallInfo
from _pytest.store import StoreKey


skipped_by_mark_key = StoreKey[bool]()
evalxfail_key = StoreKey[MarkEvaluator]()
unexpectedsuccess_key = StoreKey[str]()


def pytest_addoption(parser: Parser) -> None:
    group = parser.getgroup("general")
    group.addoption(
        "--runxfail",
        action="store_true",
        dest="runxfail",
        default=False,
        help="report the results of xfail tests as if they were not marked",
    )

    parser.addini(
        "xfail_strict",
        "default for the strict parameter of xfail "
        "markers when not given explicitly (default: False)",
        default=False,
        type="bool",
    )


def pytest_configure(config: Config) -> None:
    if config.option.runxfail:
        # yay a hack
        import pytest

        old = pytest.xfail
        config._cleanup.append(lambda: setattr(pytest, "xfail", old))

        def nop(*args, **kwargs):
            pass

        nop.Exception = xfail.Exception  # type: ignore[attr-defined] # noqa: F821
        setattr(pytest, "xfail", nop)

    config.addinivalue_line(
        "markers",
        "skip(reason=None): skip the given test function with an optional reason. "
        'Example: skip(reason="no way of currently testing this") skips the '
        "test.",
    )
    config.addinivalue_line(
        "markers",
        "skipif(condition): skip the given test function if eval(condition) "
        "results in a True value.  Evaluation happens within the "
        "module global context. Example: skipif('sys.platform == \"win32\"') "
        "skips the test if we are on the win32 platform. see "
        "https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/skipping.html",
    )
    config.addinivalue_line(
        "markers",
        "xfail(condition, reason=None, run=True, raises=None, strict=False): "
        "mark the test function as an expected failure if eval(condition) "
        "has a True value. Optionally specify a reason for better reporting "
        "and run=False if you don't even want to execute the test function. "
        "If only specific exception(s) are expected, you can list them in "
        "raises, and if the test fails in other ways, it will be reported as "
        "a true failure. See https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/skipping.html",
    )


@hookimpl(tryfirst=True)
def pytest_runtest_setup(item: Item) -> None:
    # Check if skip or skipif are specified as pytest marks
    item._store[skipped_by_mark_key] = False
    eval_skipif = MarkEvaluator(item, "skipif")
    if eval_skipif.istrue():
        item._store[skipped_by_mark_key] = True
        skip(eval_skipif.getexplanation())

    for skip_info in item.iter_markers(name="skip"):
        item._store[skipped_by_mark_key] = True
        if "reason" in skip_info.kwargs:
            skip(skip_info.kwargs["reason"])
        elif skip_info.args:
            skip(skip_info.args[0])
        else:
            skip("unconditional skip")

    item._store[evalxfail_key] = MarkEvaluator(item, "xfail")
    check_xfail_no_run(item)


@hookimpl(hookwrapper=True)
def pytest_pyfunc_call(pyfuncitem: Function):
    check_xfail_no_run(pyfuncitem)
    outcome = yield
    passed = outcome.excinfo is None
    if passed:
        check_strict_xfail(pyfuncitem)


def check_xfail_no_run(item: Item) -> None:
    """check xfail(run=False)"""
    if not item.config.option.runxfail:
        evalxfail = item._store[evalxfail_key]
        if evalxfail.istrue():
            if not evalxfail.get("run", True):
                xfail("[NOTRUN] " + evalxfail.getexplanation())


def check_strict_xfail(pyfuncitem: Function) -> None:
    """check xfail(strict=True) for the given PASSING test"""
    evalxfail = pyfuncitem._store[evalxfail_key]
    if evalxfail.istrue():
        strict_default = pyfuncitem.config.getini("xfail_strict")
        is_strict_xfail = evalxfail.get("strict", strict_default)
        if is_strict_xfail:
            del pyfuncitem._store[evalxfail_key]
            explanation = evalxfail.getexplanation()
            fail("[XPASS(strict)] " + explanation, pytrace=False)


@hookimpl(hookwrapper=True)
def pytest_runtest_makereport(item: Item, call: CallInfo[None]):
    outcome = yield
    rep = outcome.get_result()
    evalxfail = item._store.get(evalxfail_key, None)
    # unittest special case, see setting of unexpectedsuccess_key
    if unexpectedsuccess_key in item._store and rep.when == "call":
        reason = item._store[unexpectedsuccess_key]
        if reason:
            rep.longrepr = "Unexpected success: {}".format(reason)
        else:
            rep.longrepr = "Unexpected success"
        rep.outcome = "failed"

    elif item.config.option.runxfail:
        pass  # don't interfere
    elif call.excinfo and isinstance(call.excinfo.value, xfail.Exception):
        assert call.excinfo.value.msg is not None
        rep.wasxfail = "reason: " + call.excinfo.value.msg
        rep.outcome = "skipped"
    elif evalxfail and not rep.skipped and evalxfail.wasvalid() and evalxfail.istrue():
        if call.excinfo:
            if evalxfail.invalidraise(call.excinfo.value):
                rep.outcome = "failed"
            else:
                rep.outcome = "skipped"
                rep.wasxfail = evalxfail.getexplanation()
        elif call.when == "call":
            strict_default = item.config.getini("xfail_strict")
            is_strict_xfail = evalxfail.get("strict", strict_default)
            explanation = evalxfail.getexplanation()
            if is_strict_xfail:
                rep.outcome = "failed"
                rep.longrepr = "[XPASS(strict)] {}".format(explanation)
            else:
                rep.outcome = "passed"
                rep.wasxfail = explanation
    elif (
        item._store.get(skipped_by_mark_key, True)
        and rep.skipped
        and type(rep.longrepr) is tuple
    ):
        # skipped by mark.skipif; change the location of the failure
        # to point to the item definition, otherwise it will display
        # the location of where the skip exception was raised within pytest
        _, _, reason = rep.longrepr
        filename, line = item.reportinfo()[:2]
        assert line is not None
        rep.longrepr = str(filename), line + 1, reason


# called by terminalreporter progress reporting


def pytest_report_teststatus(report: BaseReport) -> Optional[Tuple[str, str, str]]:
    if hasattr(report, "wasxfail"):
        if report.skipped:
            return "xfailed", "x", "XFAIL"
        elif report.passed:
            return "xpassed", "X", "XPASS"
    return None

from typing import Any
from typing import cast
from typing import Dict
from typing import Generic
from typing import TypeVar
from typing import Union


__all__ = ["Store", "StoreKey"]


T = TypeVar("T")
D = TypeVar("D")


class StoreKey(Generic[T]):
    """StoreKey is an object used as a key to a Store.

    A StoreKey is associated with the type T of the value of the key.

    A StoreKey is unique and cannot conflict with another key.
    """

    __slots__ = ()


class Store:
    """Store is a type-safe heterogenous mutable mapping that
    allows keys and value types to be defined separately from
    where it (the Store) is created.

    Usually you will be given an object which has a ``Store``:

    .. code-block:: python

        store: Store = some_object.store

    If a module wants to store data in this Store, it creates StoreKeys
    for its keys (at the module level):

    .. code-block:: python

        some_str_key = StoreKey[str]()
        some_bool_key = StoreKey[bool]()

    To store information:

    .. code-block:: python

        # Value type must match the key.
        store[some_str_key] = "value"
        store[some_bool_key] = True

    To retrieve the information:

    .. code-block:: python

        # The static type of some_str is str.
        some_str = store[some_str_key]
        # The static type of some_bool is bool.
        some_bool = store[some_bool_key]

    Why use this?
    -------------

    Problem: module Internal defines an object. Module External, which
    module Internal doesn't know about, receives the object and wants to
    attach information to it, to be retrieved later given the object.

    Bad solution 1: Module External assigns private attributes directly on
    the object. This doesn't work well because the type checker doesn't
    know about these attributes and it complains about undefined attributes.

    Bad solution 2: module Internal adds a ``Dict[str, Any]`` attribute to
    the object. Module External stores its data in private keys of this dict.
    This doesn't work well because retrieved values are untyped.

    Good solution: module Internal adds a ``Store`` to the object. Module
    External mints StoreKeys for its own keys. Module External stores and
    retrieves its data using these keys.
    """

    __slots__ = ("_store",)

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._store = {}  # type: Dict[StoreKey[Any], object]

    def __setitem__(self, key: StoreKey[T], value: T) -> None:
        """Set a value for key."""
        self._store[key] = value

    def __getitem__(self, key: StoreKey[T]) -> T:
        """Get the value for key.

        Raises KeyError if the key wasn't set before.
        """
        return cast(T, self._store[key])

    def get(self, key: StoreKey[T], default: D) -> Union[T, D]:
        """Get the value for key, or return default if the key wasn't set
        before."""
        try:
            return self[key]
        except KeyError:
            return default

    def setdefault(self, key: StoreKey[T], default: T) -> T:
        """Return the value of key if already set, otherwise set the value
        of key to default and return default."""
        try:
            return self[key]
        except KeyError:
            self[key] = default
            return default

    def __delitem__(self, key: StoreKey[T]) -> None:
        """Delete the value for key.

        Raises KeyError if the key wasn't set before.
        """
        del self._store[key]

    def __contains__(self, key: StoreKey[T]) -> bool:
        """Returns whether key was set."""
        return key in self._store

is_simple_module = "/" not in fn and fn.endswith(".py")
        is_package = fn.count("/") == 1 and fn.endswith("__init__.py")
        if is_simple_module:
            module_name, _ = os.path.splitext(fn)
            # we ignore "setup.py" at the root of the distribution
            if module_name != "setup":

    if not seen_some:

        # "src" based source trees for example)

        new_package_files = []
        for fn in package_files:
