def pytest_addoption(parser: Parser) -> None:
    group = parser.getgroup("general")
    group.addoption(
        "--fixtures",
        "--funcargs",
        action="store_true",
        dest="showfixtures",
        default=False,
        help="show available fixtures, sorted by plugin appearance "
        "(fixtures with leading '_' are only shown with '-v')",
    )
    group.addoption(
        "--fixtures-per-test",
        action="store_true",
        dest="show_fixtures_per_test",
        default=False,
        help="show fixtures per test",
    )
    parser.addini(
        "python_files",
        type="args",
        # NOTE: default is also used in AssertionRewritingHook.
        default=["test_*.py", "*_test.py"],
        help="glob-style file patterns for Python test module discovery",
    )
    parser.addini(
        "python_classes",
        type="args",
        default=["Test"],
        help="prefixes or glob names for Python test class discovery",
    )
    parser.addini(
        "python_functions",
        type="args",
        default=["test"],
        help="prefixes or glob names for Python test function and method discovery",
    )
    parser.addini(
        "disable_test_id_escaping_and_forfeit_all_rights_to_community_support",
        type="bool",
        default=False,
        help="disable string escape non-ascii characters, might cause unwanted "
        "side effects(use at your own risk)",
    )


def pytest_cmdline_main(config: Config) -> Optional[Union[int, ExitCode]]:
    if config.option.showfixtures:
        showfixtures(config)
        return 0
    if config.option.show_fixtures_per_test:
        show_fixtures_per_test(config)
        return 0
    return None


def pytest_generate_tests(metafunc: "Metafunc") -> None:
    for marker in metafunc.definition.iter_markers(name="parametrize"):
        metafunc.parametrize(*marker.args, **marker.kwargs, _param_mark=marker)


def pytest_configure(config: Config) -> None:
    config.addinivalue_line(
        "markers",
        "parametrize(argnames, argvalues): call a test function multiple "
        "times passing in different arguments in turn. argvalues generally "
        "needs to be a list of values if argnames specifies only one name "
        "or a list of tuples of values if argnames specifies multiple names. "
        "Example: @parametrize('arg1', [1,2]) would lead to two calls of the "
        "decorated test function, one with arg1=1 and another with arg1=2."
        "see https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/how-to/parametrize.html for more info "
        "and examples.",
    )
    config.addinivalue_line(
        "markers",
        "usefixtures(fixturename1, fixturename2, ...): mark tests as needing "
        "all of the specified fixtures. see "
        "https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/explanation/fixtures.html#usefixtures ",
    )


def async_warn_and_skip(nodeid: str) -> None:
    msg = "async def functions are not natively supported and have been skipped.\n"
    msg += (
        "You need to install a suitable plugin for your async framework, for example:\n"
    )
    msg += "  - anyio\n"
    msg += "  - pytest-asyncio\n"
    msg += "  - pytest-tornasync\n"
    msg += "  - pytest-trio\n"
    msg += "  - pytest-twisted"
    warnings.warn(PytestUnhandledCoroutineWarning(msg.format(nodeid)))
    skip(msg="async def function and no async plugin installed (see warnings)")


@hookimpl(trylast=True)
def pytest_pyfunc_call(pyfuncitem: "Function") -> Optional[object]:
    testfunction = pyfuncitem.obj
    if is_async_function(testfunction):
        async_warn_and_skip(pyfuncitem.nodeid)
    funcargs = pyfuncitem.funcargs
    testargs = {arg: funcargs[arg] for arg in pyfuncitem._fixtureinfo.argnames}
    result = testfunction(**testargs)
    if hasattr(result, "__await__") or hasattr(result, "__aiter__"):
        async_warn_and_skip(pyfuncitem.nodeid)
    return True


def pytest_collect_file(fspath: Path, parent: nodes.Collector) -> Optional["Module"]:
    if fspath.suffix == ".py":
        if not parent.session.isinitpath(fspath):
            if not path_matches_patterns(
                fspath, parent.config.getini("python_files") + ["__init__.py"]
            ):
                return None
        ihook = parent.session.gethookproxy(fspath)
        module: Module = ihook.pytest_pycollect_makemodule(fspath=fspath, parent=parent)
        return module
    return None


def path_matches_patterns(path: Path, patterns: Iterable[str]) -> bool:
    """Return whether path matches any of the patterns in the list of globs given."""
    return any(fnmatch_ex(pattern, path) for pattern in patterns)


def pytest_pycollect_makemodule(fspath: Path, parent) -> "Module":
    if fspath.name == "__init__.py":
        pkg: Package = Package.from_parent(parent, path=fspath)
        return pkg
    mod: Module = Module.from_parent(parent, path=fspath)
    return mod


@hookimpl(trylast=True)
def pytest_pycollect_makeitem(collector: "PyCollector", name: str, obj: object):
    # Nothing was collected elsewhere, let's do it here.
    if safe_isclass(obj):
        if collector.istestclass(obj, name):
            return Class.from_parent(collector, name=name, obj=obj)
    elif collector.istestfunction(obj, name):
        # mock seems to store unbound methods (issue473), normalize it.
        obj = getattr(obj, "__func__", obj)
        # We need to try and unwrap the function if it's a functools.partial
        # or a functools.wrapped.
        # We mustn't if it's been wrapped with mock.patch (python 2 only).
        if not (inspect.isfunction(obj) or inspect.isfunction(get_real_func(obj))):
            filename, lineno = getfslineno(obj)
            warnings.warn_explicit(
                message=PytestCollectionWarning(
                    "cannot collect %r because it is not a function." % name
                ),
                category=None,
                filename=str(filename),
                lineno=lineno + 1,
            )
        elif getattr(obj, "__test__", True):
            if is_generator(obj):
                res = Function.from_parent(collector, name=name)
                reason = "yield tests were removed in pytest 4.0 - {name} will be ignored".format(
                    name=name
                )
                res.add_marker(MARK_GEN.xfail(run=False, reason=reason))
                res.warn(PytestCollectionWarning(reason))
            else:
                res = list(collector._genfunctions(name, obj))
            return res


class PyobjMixin(nodes.Node):
    """this mix-in inherits from Node to carry over the typing information

    as its intended to always mix in before a node
    its position in the mro is unaffected"""

    _ALLOW_MARKERS = True

    @property
    def module(self):
        """Python module object this node was collected from (can be None)."""
        node = self.getparent(Module)
        return node.obj if node is not None else None

    @property
    def cls(self):
        """Python class object this node was collected from (can be None)."""
        node = self.getparent(Class)
        return node.obj if node is not None else None

    @property
    def instance(self):
        """Python instance object this node was collected from (can be None)."""
        node = self.getparent(Instance)
        return node.obj if node is not None else None

    @property
    def obj(self):
        """Underlying Python object."""
        obj = getattr(self, "_obj", None)
        if obj is None:
            self._obj = obj = self._getobj()
            # XXX evil hack
            # used to avoid Instance collector marker duplication
            if self._ALLOW_MARKERS:
                self.own_markers.extend(get_unpacked_marks(self.obj))
        return obj

    @obj.setter
    def obj(self, value):
        self._obj = value

    def _getobj(self):
        """Get the underlying Python object. May be overwritten by subclasses."""
        # TODO: Improve the type of `parent` such that assert/ignore aren't needed.
        assert self.parent is not None
        obj = self.parent.obj  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
        return getattr(obj, self.name)

    def getmodpath(self, stopatmodule: bool = True, includemodule: bool = False) -> str:
        """Return Python path relative to the containing module."""
        chain = self.listchain()
        chain.reverse()
        parts = []
        for node in chain:
            if isinstance(node, Instance):
                continue
            name = node.name
            if isinstance(node, Module):
                name = os.path.splitext(name)[0]
                if stopatmodule:
                    if includemodule:
                        parts.append(name)
                    break
            parts.append(name)
        parts.reverse()
        return ".".join(parts)

    def reportinfo(self) -> Tuple[Union[LEGACY_PATH, str], int, str]:
        # XXX caching?
        obj = self.obj
        compat_co_firstlineno = getattr(obj, "compat_co_firstlineno", None)
        if isinstance(compat_co_firstlineno, int):
            # nose compatibility
            file_path = sys.modules[obj.__module__].__file__
            if file_path.endswith(".pyc"):
                file_path = file_path[:-1]
            fspath: Union[LEGACY_PATH, str] = file_path
            lineno = compat_co_firstlineno
        else:
            path, lineno = getfslineno(obj)
            if isinstance(path, Path):
                fspath = legacy_path(path)
            else:
                fspath = path
        modpath = self.getmodpath()
        assert isinstance(lineno, int)
        return fspath, lineno, modpath


# As an optimization, these builtin attribute names are pre-ignored when
# iterating over an object during collection -- the pytest_pycollect_makeitem
# hook is not called for them.
# fmt: off
class _EmptyClass: pass  # noqa: E701
IGNORED_ATTRIBUTES = frozenset.union(  # noqa: E305
    frozenset(),
    # Module.
    dir(types.ModuleType("empty_module")),
    # Some extra module attributes the above doesn't catch.
    {"__builtins__", "__file__", "__cached__"},
    # Class.
    dir(_EmptyClass),
    # Instance.
    dir(_EmptyClass()),
)
del _EmptyClass
# fmt: on


class PyCollector(PyobjMixin, nodes.Collector):
    def funcnamefilter(self, name: str) -> bool:
        return self._matches_prefix_or_glob_option("python_functions", name)

    def isnosetest(self, obj: object) -> bool:
        """Look for the __test__ attribute, which is applied by the
        @nose.tools.istest decorator.
        """
        # We explicitly check for "is True" here to not mistakenly treat
        # classes with a custom __getattr__ returning something truthy (like a
        # function) as test classes.
        return safe_getattr(obj, "__test__", False) is True

    def classnamefilter(self, name: str) -> bool:
        return self._matches_prefix_or_glob_option("python_classes", name)

    def istestfunction(self, obj: object, name: str) -> bool:
        if self.funcnamefilter(name) or self.isnosetest(obj):
            if isinstance(obj, staticmethod):
                # staticmethods need to be unwrapped.
                obj = safe_getattr(obj, "__func__", False)
            return callable(obj) and fixtures.getfixturemarker(obj) is None
        else:
            return False

    def istestclass(self, obj: object, name: str) -> bool:
        return self.classnamefilter(name) or self.isnosetest(obj)

    def _matches_prefix_or_glob_option(self, option_name: str, name: str) -> bool:
        """Check if the given name matches the prefix or glob-pattern defined
        in ini configuration."""
        for option in self.config.getini(option_name):
            if name.startswith(option):
                return True
            # Check that name looks like a glob-string before calling fnmatch
            # because this is called for every name in each collected module,
            # and fnmatch is somewhat expensive to call.
            elif ("*" in option or "?" in option or "[" in option) and fnmatch.fnmatch(
                name, option
            ):
                return True
        return False

    def collect(self) -> Iterable[Union[nodes.Item, nodes.Collector]]:
        if not getattr(self.obj, "__test__", True):
            return []

        # NB. we avoid random getattrs and peek in the __dict__ instead
        # (XXX originally introduced from a PyPy need, still true?)
        dicts = [getattr(self.obj, "__dict__", {})]
        for basecls in self.obj.__class__.__mro__:
            dicts.append(basecls.__dict__)
        seen: Set[str] = set()
        values: List[Union[nodes.Item, nodes.Collector]] = []
        ihook = self.ihook
        for dic in dicts:
            # Note: seems like the dict can change during iteration -
            # be careful not to remove the list() without consideration.
            for name, obj in list(dic.items()):
                if name in IGNORED_ATTRIBUTES:
                    continue
                if name in seen:
                    continue
                seen.add(name)
                res = ihook.pytest_pycollect_makeitem(
                    collector=self, name=name, obj=obj
                )
                if res is None:
                    continue
                elif isinstance(res, list):
                    values.extend(res)
                else:
                    values.append(res)

        def sort_key(item):
            fspath, lineno, _ = item.reportinfo()
            return (str(fspath), lineno)

        values.sort(key=sort_key)
        return values

    def _genfunctions(self, name: str, funcobj) -> Iterator["Function"]:
        modulecol = self.getparent(Module)
        assert modulecol is not None
        module = modulecol.obj
        clscol = self.getparent(Class)
        cls = clscol and clscol.obj or None
        fm = self.session._fixturemanager

        definition = FunctionDefinition.from_parent(self, name=name, callobj=funcobj)
        fixtureinfo = definition._fixtureinfo

        metafunc = Metafunc(
            definition=definition,
            fixtureinfo=fixtureinfo,
            config=self.config,
            cls=cls,
            module=module,
            _ispytest=True,
        )
        methods = []
        if hasattr(module, "pytest_generate_tests"):
            methods.append(module.pytest_generate_tests)
        if cls is not None and hasattr(cls, "pytest_generate_tests"):
            methods.append(cls().pytest_generate_tests)

        self.ihook.pytest_generate_tests.call_extra(methods, dict(metafunc=metafunc))

        if not metafunc._calls:
            yield Function.from_parent(self, name=name, fixtureinfo=fixtureinfo)
        else:
            # Add funcargs() as fixturedefs to fixtureinfo.arg2fixturedefs.
            fixtures.add_funcarg_pseudo_fixture_def(self, metafunc, fm)

            # Add_funcarg_pseudo_fixture_def may have shadowed some fixtures
            # with direct parametrization, so make sure we update what the
            # function really needs.
            fixtureinfo.prune_dependency_tree()

            for callspec in metafunc._calls:
                subname = f"{name}[{callspec.id}]"
                yield Function.from_parent(
                    self,
                    name=subname,
                    callspec=callspec,
                    callobj=funcobj,
                    fixtureinfo=fixtureinfo,
                    keywords={callspec.id: True},
                    originalname=name,
                )


class Module(nodes.File, PyCollector):
    """Collector for test classes and functions."""

    def _getobj(self):
        return self._importtestmodule()

    def collect(self) -> Iterable[Union[nodes.Item, nodes.Collector]]:
        self._inject_setup_module_fixture()
        self._inject_setup_function_fixture()
        self.session._fixturemanager.parsefactories(self)
        return super().collect()

    def _inject_setup_module_fixture(self) -> None:
        """Inject a hidden autouse, module scoped fixture into the collected module object
        that invokes setUpModule/tearDownModule if either or both are available.

        Using a fixture to invoke this methods ensures we play nicely and unsurprisingly with
        other fixtures (#517).
        """
        setup_module = _get_first_non_fixture_func(
            self.obj, ("setUpModule", "setup_module")
        )
        teardown_module = _get_first_non_fixture_func(
            self.obj, ("tearDownModule", "teardown_module")
        )

        if setup_module is None and teardown_module is None:
            return

        @fixtures.fixture(
            autouse=True,
            scope="module",
            # Use a unique name to speed up lookup.
            name=f"_xunit_setup_module_fixture_{self.obj.__name__}",
        )
        def xunit_setup_module_fixture(request) -> Generator[None, None, None]:
            if setup_module is not None:
                _call_with_optional_argument(setup_module, request.module)
            yield
            if teardown_module is not None:
                _call_with_optional_argument(teardown_module, request.module)

        self.obj.__pytest_setup_module = xunit_setup_module_fixture

    def _inject_setup_function_fixture(self) -> None:
        """Inject a hidden autouse, function scoped fixture into the collected module object
        that invokes setup_function/teardown_function if either or both are available.

        Using a fixture to invoke this methods ensures we play nicely and unsurprisingly with
        other fixtures (#517).
        """
        setup_function = _get_first_non_fixture_func(self.obj, ("setup_function",))
        teardown_function = _get_first_non_fixture_func(
            self.obj, ("teardown_function",)
        )
        if setup_function is None and teardown_function is None:
            return

        @fixtures.fixture(
            autouse=True,
            scope="function",
            # Use a unique name to speed up lookup.
            name=f"_xunit_setup_function_fixture_{self.obj.__name__}",
        )
        def xunit_setup_function_fixture(request) -> Generator[None, None, None]:
            if request.instance is not None:
                # in this case we are bound to an instance, so we need to let
                # setup_method handle this
                yield
                return
            if setup_function is not None:
                _call_with_optional_argument(setup_function, request.function)
            yield
            if teardown_function is not None:
                _call_with_optional_argument(teardown_function, request.function)

        self.obj.__pytest_setup_function = xunit_setup_function_fixture

    def _importtestmodule(self):
        # We assume we are only called once per module.
        importmode = self.config.getoption("--import-mode")
        try:
            mod = import_path(self.path, mode=importmode, root=self.config.rootpath)
        except SyntaxError as e:
            raise self.CollectError(
                ExceptionInfo.from_current().getrepr(style="short")
            ) from e
        except ImportPathMismatchError as e:
            raise self.CollectError(
                "import file mismatch:\n"
                "imported module %r has this __file__ attribute:\n"
                "  %s\n"
                "which is not the same as the test file we want to collect:\n"
                "  %s\n"
                "HINT: remove __pycache__ / .pyc files and/or use a "
                "unique basename for your test file modules" % e.args
            ) from e
        except ImportError as e:
            exc_info = ExceptionInfo.from_current()
            if self.config.getoption("verbose") < 2:
                exc_info.traceback = exc_info.traceback.filter(filter_traceback)
            exc_repr = (
                exc_info.getrepr(style="short")
                if exc_info.traceback
                else exc_info.exconly()
            )
            formatted_tb = str(exc_repr)
            raise self.CollectError(
                "ImportError while importing test module '{path}'.\n"
                "Hint: make sure your test modules/packages have valid Python names.\n"
                "Traceback:\n"
                "{traceback}".format(path=self.path, traceback=formatted_tb)
            ) from e
        except skip.Exception as e:
            if e.allow_module_level:
                raise
            raise self.CollectError(
                "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed. "
                "To decorate a test function, use the @pytest.mark.skip "
                "or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators instead, and to skip a "
                "module use `pytestmark = pytest.mark.{skip,skipif}."
            ) from e
        self.config.pluginmanager.consider_module(mod)
        return mod


class Package(Module):
    def __init__(
        self,
        fspath: Optional[LEGACY_PATH],
        parent: nodes.Collector,
        # NOTE: following args are unused:
        config=None,
        session=None,
        nodeid=None,
        path=Optional[Path],
    ) -> None:
        # NOTE: Could be just the following, but kept as-is for compat.
        # nodes.FSCollector.__init__(self, fspath, parent=parent)
        path, fspath = nodes._imply_path(path, fspath=fspath)
        session = parent.session
        nodes.FSCollector.__init__(
            self,
            fspath=fspath,
            path=path,
            parent=parent,
            config=config,
            session=session,
            nodeid=nodeid,
        )
        self.name = path.parent.name

    def setup(self) -> None:
        # Not using fixtures to call setup_module here because autouse fixtures
        # from packages are not called automatically (#4085).
        setup_module = _get_first_non_fixture_func(
            self.obj, ("setUpModule", "setup_module")
        )
        if setup_module is not None:
            _call_with_optional_argument(setup_module, self.obj)

        teardown_module = _get_first_non_fixture_func(
            self.obj, ("tearDownModule", "teardown_module")
        )
        if teardown_module is not None:
            func = partial(_call_with_optional_argument, teardown_module, self.obj)
            self.addfinalizer(func)

    def gethookproxy(self, fspath: "os.PathLike[str]"):
        warnings.warn(FSCOLLECTOR_GETHOOKPROXY_ISINITPATH, stacklevel=2)
        return self.session.gethookproxy(fspath)

    def isinitpath(self, path: Union[str, "os.PathLike[str]"]) -> bool:
        warnings.warn(FSCOLLECTOR_GETHOOKPROXY_ISINITPATH, stacklevel=2)
        return self.session.isinitpath(path)

    def _recurse(self, direntry: "os.DirEntry[str]") -> bool:
        if direntry.name == "__pycache__":
            return False
        fspath = Path(direntry.path)
        ihook = self.session.gethookproxy(fspath.parent)
        if ihook.pytest_ignore_collect(fspath=fspath, config=self.config):
            return False
        norecursepatterns = self.config.getini("norecursedirs")
        if any(fnmatch_ex(pat, fspath) for pat in norecursepatterns):
            return False
        return True

    def _collectfile(
        self, fspath: Path, handle_dupes: bool = True
    ) -> Sequence[nodes.Collector]:
        assert (
            fspath.is_file()
        ), "{!r} is not a file (isdir={!r}, exists={!r}, islink={!r})".format(
            fspath, fspath.is_dir(), fspath.exists(), fspath.is_symlink()
        )
        ihook = self.session.gethookproxy(fspath)
        if not self.session.isinitpath(fspath):
            if ihook.pytest_ignore_collect(fspath=fspath, config=self.config):
                return ()

        if handle_dupes:
            keepduplicates = self.config.getoption("keepduplicates")
            if not keepduplicates:
                duplicate_paths = self.config.pluginmanager._duplicatepaths
                if fspath in duplicate_paths:
                    return ()
                else:
                    duplicate_paths.add(fspath)

        return ihook.pytest_collect_file(fspath=fspath, parent=self)  # type: ignore[no-any-return]

    def collect(self) -> Iterable[Union[nodes.Item, nodes.Collector]]:
        this_path = self.path.parent
        init_module = this_path / "__init__.py"
        if init_module.is_file() and path_matches_patterns(
            init_module, self.config.getini("python_files")
        ):
            yield Module.from_parent(self, path=init_module)
        pkg_prefixes: Set[Path] = set()
        for direntry in visit(str(this_path), recurse=self._recurse):
            path = Path(direntry.path)

            # We will visit our own __init__.py file, in which case we skip it.
            if direntry.is_file():
                if direntry.name == "__init__.py" and path.parent == this_path:
                    continue

            parts_ = parts(direntry.path)
            if any(
                str(pkg_prefix) in parts_ and pkg_prefix / "__init__.py" != path
                for pkg_prefix in pkg_prefixes
            ):
                continue

            if direntry.is_file():
                yield from self._collectfile(path)
            elif not direntry.is_dir():
                # Broken symlink or invalid/missing file.
                continue
            elif path.joinpath("__init__.py").is_file():
                pkg_prefixes.add(path)


def _call_with_optional_argument(func, arg) -> None:
    """Call the given function with the given argument if func accepts one argument, otherwise
    calls func without arguments."""
    arg_count = func.__code__.co_argcount
    if inspect.ismethod(func):
        arg_count -= 1
    if arg_count:
        func(arg)
    else:
        func()


def _get_first_non_fixture_func(obj: object, names: Iterable[str]):
    """Return the attribute from the given object to be used as a setup/teardown
    xunit-style function, but only if not marked as a fixture to avoid calling it twice."""
    for name in names:
        meth = getattr(obj, name, None)
        if meth is not None and fixtures.getfixturemarker(meth) is None:
            return meth


class Class(PyCollector):
    """Collector for test methods."""

    @classmethod
    def from_parent(cls, parent, *, name, obj=None, **kw):
        """The public constructor."""
        return super().from_parent(name=name, parent=parent, **kw)

    def collect(self) -> Iterable[Union[nodes.Item, nodes.Collector]]:
        if not safe_getattr(self.obj, "__test__", True):
            return []
        if hasinit(self.obj):
            assert self.parent is not None
            self.warn(
                PytestCollectionWarning(
                    "cannot collect test class %r because it has a "
                    "__init__ constructor (from: %s)"
                    % (self.obj.__name__, self.parent.nodeid)
                )
            )
            return []
        elif hasnew(self.obj):
            assert self.parent is not None
            self.warn(
                PytestCollectionWarning(
                    "cannot collect test class %r because it has a "
                    "__new__ constructor (from: %s)"
                    % (self.obj.__name__, self.parent.nodeid)
                )
            )
            return []

        self._inject_setup_class_fixture()
        self._inject_setup_method_fixture()

        return [Instance.from_parent(self, name="()")]

    def _inject_setup_class_fixture(self) -> None:
        """Inject a hidden autouse, class scoped fixture into the collected class object
        that invokes setup_class/teardown_class if either or both are available.

        Using a fixture to invoke this methods ensures we play nicely and unsurprisingly with
        other fixtures (#517).
        """
        setup_class = _get_first_non_fixture_func(self.obj, ("setup_class",))
        teardown_class = getattr(self.obj, "teardown_class", None)
        if setup_class is None and teardown_class is None:
            return

        @fixtures.fixture(
            autouse=True,
            scope="class",
            # Use a unique name to speed up lookup.
            name=f"_xunit_setup_class_fixture_{self.obj.__qualname__}",
        )
        def xunit_setup_class_fixture(cls) -> Generator[None, None, None]:
            if setup_class is not None:
                func = getimfunc(setup_class)
                _call_with_optional_argument(func, self.obj)
            yield
            if teardown_class is not None:
                func = getimfunc(teardown_class)
                _call_with_optional_argument(func, self.obj)

        self.obj.__pytest_setup_class = xunit_setup_class_fixture

    def _inject_setup_method_fixture(self) -> None:
        """Inject a hidden autouse, function scoped fixture into the collected class object
        that invokes setup_method/teardown_method if either or both are available.

        Using a fixture to invoke this methods ensures we play nicely and unsurprisingly with
        other fixtures (#517).
        """
        setup_method = _get_first_non_fixture_func(self.obj, ("setup_method",))
        teardown_method = getattr(self.obj, "teardown_method", None)
        if setup_method is None and teardown_method is None:
            return

        @fixtures.fixture(
            autouse=True,
            scope="function",
            # Use a unique name to speed up lookup.
            name=f"_xunit_setup_method_fixture_{self.obj.__qualname__}",
        )
        def xunit_setup_method_fixture(self, request) -> Generator[None, None, None]:
            method = request.function
            if setup_method is not None:
                func = getattr(self, "setup_method")
                _call_with_optional_argument(func, method)
            yield
            if teardown_method is not None:
                func = getattr(self, "teardown_method")
                _call_with_optional_argument(func, method)

        self.obj.__pytest_setup_method = xunit_setup_method_fixture


class Instance(PyCollector):
    _ALLOW_MARKERS = False  # hack, destroy later
    # Instances share the object with their parents in a way
    # that duplicates markers instances if not taken out
    # can be removed at node structure reorganization time.

    def _getobj(self):
        # TODO: Improve the type of `parent` such that assert/ignore aren't needed.
        assert self.parent is not None
        obj = self.parent.obj  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
        return obj()

    def collect(self) -> Iterable[Union[nodes.Item, nodes.Collector]]:
        self.session._fixturemanager.parsefactories(self)
        return super().collect()

    def newinstance(self):
        self.obj = self._getobj()
        return self.obj


def hasinit(obj: object) -> bool:
    init: object = getattr(obj, "__init__", None)
    if init:
        return init != object.__init__
    return False


def hasnew(obj: object) -> bool:
    new: object = getattr(obj, "__new__", None)
    if new:
        return new != object.__new__
    return False

def test_module_level_skip_error(pytester: Pytester) -> None:
    """Verify that using pytest.skip at module level causes a collection error."""
    pytester.makepyfile(
        """
        import pytest
        pytest.skip("skip_module_level")

        def test_func():
            assert True
    """
    )
    result = pytester.runpytest()
    result.stdout.fnmatch_lines(
        ["*Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed*"]
    )


def test_module_level_skip_with_allow_module_level(pytester: Pytester) -> None:
    """Verify that using pytest.skip(allow_module_level=True) is allowed."""
    pytester.makepyfile(
        """
        import pytest
        pytest.skip("skip_module_level", allow_module_level=True)

        def test_func():
            assert 0
    """
    )
    result = pytester.runpytest("-rxs")
    result.stdout.fnmatch_lines(["*SKIP*skip_module_level"])

Action Input: {"problem": "Problem: Improve handling of skip for module level
This is potentially about updating docs, updating error messages or introducing a new API.

Consider the following scenario:

`pos_only.py` is using Python 3,8 syntax:
```python
def foo(a, /, b):
    return a + b
```

It should not be tested under Python 3.6 and 3.7.
This is a proper way to skip the test in Python older than 3.8:
```python
from pytest import raises, skip
import sys
if sys.version_info < (3, 8):
    skip(msg="Requires Python >= 3.8", allow_module_level=True)

# import must be after the module level skip:
from pos_only import *

def test_foo():
    assert foo(10, 20) == 30
    assert foo(10, b=20) == 30
    with raises(TypeError):
        assert foo(a=10, b=20)
```

My actual test involves parameterize and a 3.8 only class, so skipping the test itself is not sufficient because the 3.8 class was used in the parameterization.

A naive user will try to initially skip the module like:

```python
if sys.version_info < (3, 8):
    skip(msg="Requires Python >= 3.8")
```
This issues this error:

>Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed. To decorate a test function, use the @pytest.mark.skip or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators instead, and to skip a module use `pytestmark = pytest.mark.{skip,skipif}.`

The proposed solution `pytestmark = pytest.mark.{skip,skipif}`, does not work  in my case: pytest continues to process the file and fail when it hits the 3.8 syntax (when running with an older version of Python).

The correct solution, to use skip as a function is actively discouraged by the error message.

This area feels a bit unpolished.
A few ideas to improve:

1. Explain skip with  `allow_module_level` in the error message. this seems in conflict with the spirit of the message.
2. Create an alternative API to skip a module to make things easier: `skip_module("reason")`, which can call `_skip(msg=msg, allow_module_level=True)`.
"}

@_with_exception(Skipped)

- Root problem: pytest's error message raised when pytest.skip() is called at module import-time (without allow_module_level=True) says "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed ... and to skip a module use pytestmark = ...", but it doesn't mention the existing allow_module_level option on pytest.skip nor suggest a convenience helper. This is confusing: the code actually supports module-level skipping when skip(..., allow_module_level=True) is used, but the error message discourages module-level skip without informing the user about allow_module_level. A user who wants to skip a module at import time needs allow_module_level=True (or importorskip), and the message should mention that. The message also actively discourages the very correct API the library already provides.

      - __init__, from_parent, _initrequest, function (property), _getobj, _pyfuncitem (property), runtest, setup, _prunetraceback, repr_failure

  - FunctionDefinition (parent: Function)
    - methods:
      - runtest (raises), setup aliasing

2c) For the primary class (Module): class-level attributes that point to another class
- There are no class-level attributes on Module that point to other classes. Module inherits from nodes.File and PyCollector; its behavior depends on Skipped exception from outcomes.py and import_path from _pytest.pathlib.

- Component classes referenced by Module (by name/behavior; we must inspect these in Checkpoint 3):
  - outcomes.Skipped / outcomes.skip (we must inspect Skipped class and skip function).
  - nodes.File / nodes.FSCollector / nodes.Node (parents) — we inspected nodes.py earlier but will analyze in detail next.

---

Checkpoint 3: Map the architecture

3a) For each component class identified above read its full definition and list hardcoded values.

- Component: src/_pytest/outcomes.py (full file read)
  - Skipped class:
    - __module__ = "builtins" (to make printing nicer)
    - __init__(..., allow_module_level: bool = False, *, _use_item_location: bool = False)
    - attributes:
      - allow_module_level (boolean flag)
      - _use_item_location (boolean flag)
    - Hardcoded values/constants:
      - default allow_module_level=False encodes an assumption that skip() is not allowed at module import-time unless caller explicitly sets allow_module_level=True.
  - skip() function:
    - signature skip(msg: str = "", *, allow_module_level: bool = False) -> NoReturn
    - raises Skipped(msg=msg, allow_module_level=allow_module_level)
    - Docstring text that already documents allow_module_level (but the error message in Module doesn't mention the flag).
    - No numeric constants.
  - importorskip() raises Skipped(..., allow_module_level=True) for import failure — demonstrates module-level skip is supported by code in this path and allowed.

- Component: nodes.Node, nodes.FSCollector, nodes.File (we read nodes.py)
  - No relevant hardcoded numeric constants for this bug. They contain logic for path/node IDs and tracebacks, not relevant to allow_module_level semantics.

3b) Parent class methods of primary class and which are overridden.

- Primary class Module inherits from nodes.File (which inherits FSCollector) and PyCollector.

- Methods defined in parent classes (nodes.File/FSCollector and PyCollector) include:
  - FSCollector.__init__, from_parent, gethookproxy, isinitpath, (also path and nodeid setup). FSCollector provides filesystem-specific collector functionality.
  - PyCollector.collect and helper methods (istestfunction, istestclass, _genfunctions, etc.)

- Which Module overrides:
  - Module overrides _getobj (to call _importtestmodule), collect (inject fixtures then call super().collect), and defines module-specific fixture injection methods and the critical _importtestmodule. Module does not override FSCollector.from_parent etc., nor PyCollector.funcnamefilter etc.

3c) Sibling classes (other classes inheriting from same parent)
- In src/_pytest/python.py, Package inherits from Module (so not a sibling, but a subclass).
- There are no other classes in this file that inherit from nodes.File directly, so no direct siblings in this module. The primary relevant sibling patterns are how other collectors handle exceptions during import. Importorskip raises Skipped(..., allow_module_level=True) and Module._importtestmodule specifically treats Skipped differently depending on allow_module_level.

3d) Call graph around Module (what calls into it and what it calls)
- Module._importtestmodule is invoked by Module._getobj which is called by PyobjMixin.obj (when collecting), and by Module._getobj being called when the Module node's obj property is needed during collection.
- _importtestmodule uses import_path (from _pytest.pathlib), which actually executes module import/compilation; import_path can raise SyntaxError, ImportError, or skip.Exception (the Skipped exception raised from at module import-time, e.g. pytest.skip(..., allow_module_level=True) or things like importorskip raising Skipped).
- When a Skipped is caught in _importtestmodule, Module checks e.allow_module_level:
  - if True: re-raise the exception (so collection machinery knows this is a module-level skip and will record skip)
  - if False: raise a CollectError with the discouraging error message.

This behaviour is correct but the error message is misleading because it doesn't mention the allow_module_level flag (or the fact that skip(..., allow_module_level=True) is a valid option), and the wording "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed" discourages a valid path (module-level skip using allow_module_level). The report asks to improve message and/or provide skip_module() helper to make it easier.

---

Checkpoint 4: Propose a fix, then immediately challenge it

Proposed fix: Two-part, minimal and complete:

1) Improve the error message in Module._importtestmodule so it no longer discourages module-level skips and instead explains the correct option (allow_module_level=True) and suggests the convenience API (skip_module) as an alternative. This clarifies the existing API.

2) Add a small convenience function skip_module() in src/_pytest/outcomes.py that raises Skipped(..., allow_module_level=True) — i.e., a convenience wrapper for module-level skipping. This is purely ergonomic and will make the documented usage obvious. Implement it in outcomes.py in parity with skip() (i.e. decorated with @_with_exception(Skipped)) so it exposes .Exception attribute like skip does.

Concrete code changes (full diffs to apply):

A) src/_pytest/outcomes.py — add skip_module:

Insert (near the skip(), xfail(), and importorskip functions; for example right after skip or before importorskip):


Additions in outcomes.py:

    @_with_exception(Skipped)
    def skip_module(msg: str = "") -> "NoReturn":
        """Skip the rest of the importing module (module-level skip).

        This is a convenience wrapper that is allowed at import/collection time.
        Equivalent to: pytest.skip(msg, allow_module_level=True).
        """
        __tracebackhide__ = True
        raise Skipped(msg=msg, allow_module_level=True)

Notes:
- Decorating with @_with_exception(Skipped) attaches a .Exception attribute to the function object (consistent with other helpers).
- It raises Skipped with allow_module_level=True, which Module._importtestmodule will accept and allow to bubble up to the collection machinery (so the module will be skipped without causing a collection error).

B) src/_pytest/python.py — improve CollectError message

Replace the existing except skip.Exception as e block (lines roughly 607-615 in the current file) which is currently:

    except skip.Exception as e:
        if e.allow_module_level:
            raise
        raise self.CollectError(
            "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed. "
            "To decorate a test function, use the @pytest.mark.skip "
            "or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators instead, and to skip a "
            "module use `pytestmark = pytest.mark.{skip,skipif}."
        ) from e

with an improved message like:

    except skip.Exception as e:
        if e.allow_module_level:
            raise
        # Better guidance for users who want a module-level skip
        raise self.CollectError(
            "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed. "
            "If you intended to skip the entire module during import/collection, "
            "call pytest.skip(<reason>, allow_module_level=True). "
            "For convenience, pytest provides pytest.skip_module(<reason>), "
            "which is equivalent. "
            "To decorate a test function, use the @pytest.mark.skip or "
            "@pytest.mark.skipif decorators instead, and to skip a module "
            "statically use: pytestmark = pytest.mark.{skip,skipif}."
        ) from e

(Formatting note: ensure this full string is kept as a single expression or use parentheses to span lines.)

Rationale: The message now explicitly mentions allow_module_level=True and the new convenience helper skip_module; it still keeps the previous advice about using markers where appropriate.

Important: This change preserves the initial clause "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed." — so existing tests that assert that phrase via fnmatch will keep working, while adding the helpful guidance.

Now: immediately challenge the fix using the subquestions.

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"

- One-sentence: The current code assumes that users should not use pytest.skip at module import time and recommends markers instead; my fix changes that assumption by explicitly acknowledging and documenting that module-level skipping is supported via the allow_module_level flag and providing a convenience helper (skip_module) to make the intention clearer.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"

- Hardcoded strings / values I examined:
  - In outcomes.py: skip default allow_module_level: False. This encodes the default assumption that skip() should not be called at module level unless explicitly allowed. My fix does not change this default — it preserves the safe default. It simply makes the supported escape hatch (allow_module_level=True) clearer.
    - Is this default still correct? Yes — it's a safety default that prevents accidental module-level skips; we should keep it.
  - In python.py: the CollectError message string was a hardcoded message discouraging skip at module level. I update that string to mention allow_module_level and skip_module. This does not change any numeric offsets or positions or other invariants. So the only hardcoded values that mattered were the text; they become more informative, not dynamic.

Conclusion: no numeric constants need to become dynamic. The only hardcoded text to update was the error message; that's handled.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"

- The "reverse" in this context means: if we add skip_module or change messaging, do we need to change any component that reads or acts on Skipped exceptions (i.e., any code that handles Skipped.allow_module_level)?
- We examined Module._importtestmodule which currently checks e.allow_module_level and if True re-raises. That behavior is correct and already supports module-level skip. Adding skip_module simply raises Skipped(..., allow_module_level=True), so Module will re-raise it and the collection machinery will interpret it as a skip for the module (not a CollectError). No change needed in the parent code.
- I also checked importorskip in outcomes.py — it already raises Skipped(..., allow_module_level=True) when the import fails, so it already uses the reverse operation correctly. No code path relying on the previous (discouraging) message needs modification for the new behavior; only the user-visible message needs improvement.
- For completeness I re-read Module._importtestmodule (parent methods are in nodes.FSCollector and Node), but none of those perform complementary operations to this change that would require code updates. The only complementary part was the check on allow_module_level which we leave untouched.
- Concretely: trace substitution for the parent method(s):
  - Module._importtestmodule catches skip.Exception as e -> if e.allow_module_level: raise (unchanged). skip_module raises Skipped(allow_module_level=True), so Module._importtestmodule will re-raise it: correct.
  - The rest of the collection pipeline interprets Skipped exceptions and reports them as SKIP — unchanged.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"

- Module.collect, Module._inject_setup_module_fixture, Module._inject_setup_function_fixture, and the rest operate as before. They don't rely on the exact text of CollectError, only that Skipped exceptions are allowed to bubble up if allow_module_level is True and otherwise a CollectError is raised. Since we only change message text and add a helper that raises Skipped with allow_module_level True, all logic remains consistent.
- skip() still exists and works exactly the same (default allow_module_level=False). skip_module will raise Skipped(allow_module_level=True) as intended.
- importorskip continues to raise Skipped(..., allow_module_level=True) and is unaffected.
- Tests which matched the earlier fragment "*Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed*" will continue to match because the updated message still begins with that phrase (so existing tests shouldn't fail), but tests that asserted the exact full message will need updating to accept the extra guidance text. I checked the usage in testing/test_skipping.py: the test matches "*Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed*" (fnmatch) and will still match.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"

- Yes: the new convenience function skip_module() added in outcomes.py.
- Do we need to add any parent overrides? No.
- For each inherited (non-overridden) parent method that touches related state: none require change. I re-read the parent's implementations around import and exception handling (Module._importtestmodule and underlying nodes.FSCollector/Node behavior) and their logic is compatible with Skipped.allow_module_level being True. They already have the intended complementary behavior: Module._importtestmodule re-raises Skipped when allow_module_level is True so the collection machinery treats it as a skip, not a collection error. There is no additional parent method that needs to be overridden.
- Exposing skip_module to users: adding skip_module to outcomes.py makes it available as _pytest.outcomes.skip_module. In typical usage "import pytest" maps to the top-level package which will make skip (and likely skip_module) available through pytest namespace — historically the top-level distribution makes these functions available. If the project style requires explicit re-export from a top-level stub module, we would need to add skip_module to that re-export. In this repository, tests import skip via pytest and that already uses the installed package behavior. If, in the packaging layout, top-level names are forwarded from internal modules, adding skip_module to outcomes.py is the correct place; otherwise, we would add a re-export at the public API entrypoint. I inspected src/_pytest/__init__.py and it only sets __version__ etc.; the public "pytest" package behaviour in the installed runtime usually binds names on import. If we needed to ensure that "from pytest import skip_module" works in every environment, we'd add an explicit public re-export in the top-level 'pytest' package entrypoint; but the usual runtime installation exposes pytest.skip already, so adding the function in outcomes.py keeps it logically located and discoverable.

Summary of changed files
- src/_pytest/outcomes.py
  - Add skip_module function (decorated with @_with_exception(Skipped); raises Skipped(..., allow_module_level=True)).
- src/_pytest/python.py
  - Update Module._importtestmodule's CollectError message to mention allow_module_level=True and skip_module, while preserving the previous guidance about using marks for skipping tests.

Optional further changes (not strictly required for correctness)
- If the public top-level 'pytest' module requires explicit re-export, add skip_module to the public API exposure layer so that "import pytest; pytest.skip_module(...)" will work. (In most deployment installs pytest already exposes skip via the public entrypoint; adding skip_module in outcomes.py should suffice in practice but if the project uses an explicit public API file that needs updating, that file must be updated as well.)
- Update docs to mention skip_module and show canonical usage and examples (e.g., for skipping test modules requiring Python >= 3.8).

---

Checkpoint 5: Final report — root cause, all locations to change, and the complete fix

Root cause
- The code already supports skipping a module at import/collection time by raising Skipped(..., allow_module_level=True) (skip(..., allow_module_level=True) or importorskip(...)), but the error message raised when skip() is used without allow_module_level is discouraging module-level skip and does not mention the allow_module_level option (or an ergonomic helper). This confuses users who might naturally try pytest.skip(...) at module level. The error message discourages the right API (skip with allow_module_level) and thus is incomplete and unhelpful.

Every location that needs to change (and exact changes)

1) src/_pytest/outcomes.py
- What it currently does:
  - Defines Skipped exception with attribute allow_module_level (default False).
  - Defines skip(msg, *, allow_module_level=False) that raises Skipped with the given flag.
- What assumption is encoded:
  - Default allow_module_level=False encodes that module-level skip is disallowed unless caller explicitly opts-in.
- What to change:
  - Add skip_module convenience function to help users. Implementation:

    @_with_exception(Skipped)
    def skip_module(msg: str = "") -> "NoReturn":
        """Skip the rest of the importing module (module-level skip).

        This is a convenience wrapper that is allowed at import/collection time.
        Equivalent to: pytest.skip(msg, allow_module_level=True).
        """
        __tracebackhide__ = True
        raise Skipped(msg=msg, allow_module_level=True)

  - Rationale: it does not change any runtime defaults; it only provides a clearer API for the supported behaviour. This function mirrors skip() but sets allow_module_level=True. It's decorated with @_with_exception to match the shape of other helpers (so skip_module.Exception exists and other code expecting that attribute isn't surprised).

2) src/_pytest/python.py
- What it currently does:
  - In Module._importtestmodule, catches skip.Exception and if e.allow_module_level is False, raises a CollectError with the (discouraging) message:

    "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed. To decorate a test function, use the @pytest.mark.skip or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators instead, and to skip a module use `pytestmark = pytest.mark.{skip,skipif}."

- What assumption the message encodes:
  - It suggests that using pytest.skip at module level is not allowed (discouraging the API), whereas the code actually allows module-level skip when allow_module_level=True.

- What to change:
  - Update the error message to both preserve the original warning and mention the supported API and the new helper. Replace the message with:

    "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed. "
    "If you intended to skip the entire module during import/collection, "
    "call pytest.skip(<reason>, allow_module_level=True). "
    "For convenience, pytest provides pytest.skip_module(<reason>), "
    "which is equivalent. "
    "To decorate a test function, use the @pytest.mark.skip or "
    "@pytest.mark.skipif decorators instead, and to skip a module "
    "statically use: pytestmark = pytest.mark.{skip,skipif}."

  - Rationale: clarifies the existing API, helps users find the correct flag, and points to the skip_module helper.

Hardcoded values to become dynamic
- There were no numeric or structural hardcoded constants requiring change. The only hardcoded item that changed is the phrasing of the CollectError message: it becomes more descriptive. The default flag allow_module_level=False remains appropriate and must not be changed.

Any methods that need to be added or overridden
- Added: skip_module in outcomes.py (new method).
- No overrides of parent class methods required. Module._importtestmodule already handled e.allow_module_level correctly, so no changes to behavior were required beyond the message wording.

Compatibility and tests
- Existing tests that match the previous message via substring fnmatch (e.g., "*Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed*") will still pass because the message still begins with that phrase.
- Add new tests if desired to assert skip_module works; for example, create a test that does:

    import pytest
    import sys
    if sys.version_info < (3,8):
        pytest.skip_module("requires >=3.8")

  and assert the module is skipped rather than causing a collection error. There are already tests for pytest.skip(..., allow_module_level=True) that will continue to pass.

Notes on public re-export
- If the packaging or top-level 'pytest' module requires explicit re-export of newly added skip_module to make "from pytest import skip_module" or "pytest.skip_module" available, ensure the public API exposure layer (top-level pytest module) is updated accordingly. In the codebase the outcomes functions are used via pytest.skip already, so adding skip_module in outcomes.py will make it straightforward to expose it in the public API if needed.

Edge cases and final checks
- The default allow_module_level remains False which prevents accidental module-level skips. The fix does not change any runtime behavior except for adding a convenience helper and improving a user-visible error message.
- The Module._importtestmodule logic that re-raises Skipped when allow_module_level is True will continue to allow module-level skips raised by skip_module and importorskip. There is no additional need to change other handlers in the collection pipeline.

Suggested patch summary (concise)
- src/_pytest/outcomes.py: add skip_module function (decorated and raising Skipped(..., allow_module_level=True)).
- src/_pytest/python.py: update CollectError message in Module._importtestmodule to explicitly mention pytest.skip(..., allow_module_level=True) and suggest pytest.skip_module(...).

If you want, I can produce the exact patch/diff text to apply to the two files, or open the relevant test file(s) and propose test changes/examples to demonstrate/exercise the new skip_module API.
compressed_analysis:
===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug summary: pytest discourages module-level use of pytest.skip by raising a CollectError with the message "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed..." even though a supported escape hatch exists: pytest.skip(..., allow_module_level=True). The message is misleading and hides the correct API.
- Root cause & location: incorrect / incomplete user-facing guidance lives in src/_pytest/python.py, Module._importtestmodule — it catches skip.Exception and, when e.allow_module_level is False, raises a CollectError with the discouraging message (Checkpoint 2 & 3).
- Supporting code (where behavior actually exists): src/_pytest/outcomes.py defines skip(msg, *, allow_module_level: bool = False) and Skipped(allow_module_level=...), and importorskip already uses allow_module_level=True (Checkpoint 1 & 3). So the capability exists; the message is the problem.
- Proposed fix: (1) Change the CollectError message in Module._importtestmodule to explicitly mention pytest.skip(..., allow_module_level=True) and optionally suggest a small ergonomic wrapper; (2) add a convenience function skip_module(msg) in src/_pytest/outcomes.py that raises Skipped(..., allow_module_level=True). These changes cover the actual problem location (python.py) and add an ergonomic API (outcomes.py).
- Hardcoded values to change: only the user-facing CollectError string in src/_pytest/python.py; do NOT change default allow_module_level=False (safety default).
- Methods to add/override: add skip_module in src/_pytest/outcomes.py; no parent method overrides are required. Gap: need to confirm if the public pytest package entrypoint re-exports the new skip_module name (public re-export was not checked; see gap).

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: Misleading error message in Module._importtestmodule (root cause)
What was discovered and why it matters:
- File: src/_pytest/python.py, class Module, method _importtestmodule.
- The except block for skip.Exception raises a CollectError with the exact message:
  "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed. To decorate a test function, use the @pytest.mark.skip or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators instead, and to skip a module use `pytestmark = pytest.mark.{skip,skipif}."
- Why it matters: this message discourages a valid API (skip(..., allow_module_level=True)); it hides the escape hatch and confuses users. (Found in Checkpoint 2 & 3)

### Finding 2: outcomes.skip already supports module-level skipping
What was discovered and why it matters:
- File: src/_pytest/outcomes.py, function skip(msg: str = "", *, allow_module_level: bool = False)
- The signature and implementation raise Skipped(msg=msg, allow_module_level=allow_module_level). importorskip already raises Skipped(..., allow_module_level=True).
- Why it matters: the feature works at the exception level; only the messaging/visibility is wrong. This proves we should change the message / add helper, not core behavior. (Found in Checkpoint 1 & 3)

### Finding 3: Module behavior already accepts allow_module_level=True
What was discovered and why it matters:
- File: src/_pytest/python.py, class Module, method _importtestmodule.
- The code checks e.allow_module_level and, if True, re-raises the Skipped exception (allowing module-level skip to be handled as SKIP). So code path is correct; only the UI is broken. (Found in Checkpoint 3)

### Finding 4: Tests already assert the old message (but use substring matching)
What was discovered and why it matters:
- File: testing/test_skipping.py, test_module_level_skip_error uses fnmatch_lines(["*Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed*"])
- Why it matters: updating the message must preserve that phrase or tests must be updated. The tests use a wildcard prefix, so keeping the original leading phrase preserves existing tests. (Found in Checkpoint 1)

### Finding 5: Ergonomics gap — missing skip_module helper
What was discovered and why it matters:
- Current UX: users naturally try pytest.skip("reason") at module top-level and get a discouraging message.
- Proposal: add skip_module(msg) as convenience wrapper calling skip(msg, allow_module_level=True) to reduce mistakes and make documentation / error guidance actionable.
- Risk/gap: I did NOT verify top-level public re-export of new name; ensure "pytest.skip_module" or "from pytest import skip_module" will be available in the public entrypoint if required. (Identified in Checkpoint 4)

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: src/_pytest/python.py:Module._importtestmodule
- **Wrong assumption**: The CollectError message assumes module-level pytest.skip should not be used and fails to mention the supported flag allow_module_level=True.
- **Evidence**: code path catches skip.Exception as e and does:
  raise self.CollectError(
    "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed. "
    "To decorate a test function, use the @pytest.mark.skip or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators instead, and to skip a module use `pytestmark = pytest.mark.{skip,skipif}."
  ) from e
  (exact string preserved from file). This hides the documented parameter allow_module_level and the fact that Module._importtestmodule will accept Skipped where e.allow_module_level is True.
- **Related locations**: src/_pytest/outcomes.py:skip (provides allow_module_level), src/_pytest/outcomes.py:importorskip (already uses allow_module_level=True), testing/test_skipping.py:test_module_level_skip_error (matches the original message by substring). (Finding produced in Checkpoint 2 & 3)

### Location: src/_pytest/outcomes.py:skip(msg: str = "", *, allow_module_level: bool = False)
- **Wrong assumption encoded**: The default allow_module_level=False encodes "module-level skip is disallowed by default", which is intentional for safety but the surrounding UX (error message) does not document the escape hatch.
- **Evidence**: function signature is exactly:
  def skip(msg: str = "", *, allow_module_level: bool = False) -> "NoReturn":
    raise Skipped(msg=msg, allow_module_level=allow_module_level)
  importorskip calls Skipped(..., allow_module_level=True) in its code path.
- **Related locations**: src/_pytest/python.py:Module._importtestmodule (checks e.allow_module_level), testing/test_skipping.py (module-level skip tests). (Finding produced in Checkpoint 1 & 3)

### Location: (new) src/_pytest/outcomes.py:skip_module(msg: str = "")
- **Wrong assumption / missing API**: There is no small ergonomic API to make module-level skipping obvious; users commonly try pytest.skip(...) and get the discouraging error.
- **Evidence**: bug report and test failures: users attempted
    if sys.version_info < (3, 8):
        skip(msg="Requires Python >= 3.8")
  and saw the error "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed..." even though the correct call is skip(..., allow_module_level=True). The file outcomes.py contains skip but not skip_module.
- **Fix to apply (exact code snippet)**:

    @_with_exception(Skipped)
    def skip_module(msg: str = "") -> "NoReturn":
        """Skip the rest of the importing module (module-level skip).
        Equivalent to: pytest.skip(msg, allow_module_level=True).
        """
        __tracebackhide__ = True
        raise Skipped(msg=msg, allow_module_level=True)

- **Related locations**: src/_pytest/python.py:Module._importtestmodule (will accept Skipped with allow_module_level=True), src/_pytest/outcomes.py:importorskip (already behaviorally compatible). Also consider updating public API re-export to include skip_module if needed. (Finding produced in Checkpoint 4)

### Location: testing/test_skipping.py:test_module_level_skip_error
- **Wrong assumption**: The test expects the old phrasing but only matches a substring. If a full-message assertion exists elsewhere, it would break.
- **Evidence**: test code uses:
    result.stdout.fnmatch_lines(["*Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed*"])
  so preserving the original leading phrase keeps the test passing.
- **Related locations**: src/_pytest/python.py:Module._importtestmodule (message text must retain at least the original leading fragment or tests must be updated). (Finding produced in Checkpoint 1)

---

src/_pytest/python.py (Module._importtestmodule) — code confirms the behavior and message: the method catches skip.Exception, checks e.allow_module_level, and if False raises the CollectError with the discouraging text. Relevant lines (approx):

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness analysis

I verified the draft report's claims against the source code. Below I document what I looked for, why, what I expected to find, and what the code actually contains. I quote relevant lines and note any unsupported claims or omissions.

1) Verify each method mentioned in the draft report

- Why I looked at src/_pytest/outcomes.py::skip
  - Expectation: confirm skip signature includes allow_module_level and that it raises Skipped with that flag.
  - What I inspected: src/_pytest/outcomes.py around the skip() implementation.
  - Evidence from code (exact lines):
    - The skip function signature and raise:
      - lines 123–145:
        - "def skip(msg: str = \"\", *, allow_module_level: bool = False) -> \"NoReturn\":"
        - "raise Skipped(msg=msg, allow_module_level=allow_module_level)"
    - The docstring explicitly documents allow_module_level (lines 127–134).
  - Conclusion: The draft's claim that outcomes.skip supports an allow_module_level flag is correct and supported by code.

- Why I looked at src/_pytest/python.py::Module._importtestmodule
  - Expectation: confirm Module._importtestmodule catches skip.Exception, checks e.allow_module_level, and raises a CollectError with the discouraging message when allow_module_level is False.
  - What I inspected: src/_pytest/python.py around Module._importtestmodule.
  - Evidence from code (exact lines):
    - The try/except and the allow_module_level check:
      - lines 572–616 show the method; in particular:
        - "except skip.Exception as e:"
        - "    if e.allow_module_level:"
        - "        raise"
        - "    raise self.CollectError(...message...) from e"
    - The exact message raised (lines 610–614):
      - "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed. To decorate a test function, use the @pytest.mark.skip or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators instead, and to skip a module use `pytestmark = pytest.mark.{skip,skipif}."
  - Conclusion: The draft's claim about the message text and the logic in Module._importtestmodule is accurate: the code supports module-level skip via the Skipped.allow_module_level attribute, and the code raises the discouraging CollectError only when that attribute is False.

- Why I looked at src/_pytest/outcomes.py::importorskip
  - Expectation: confirm importorskip uses allow_module_level=True when raising Skipped.
  - What I inspected: importorskip in outcomes.py (lines 180–232).
  - Evidence from code:
    - importorskip catches ImportError and raises Skipped(..., allow_module_level=True) (line 217).
    - Later version-check skips also raise Skipped(..., allow_module_level=True) (lines 226–231).
  - Conclusion: importorskip already uses the module-level allowance.

- Why I looked at testing/test_skipping.py
  - Expectation: confirm tests assert the discouraging message (this constrains safe message edits).
  - What I inspected: test_module_level_skip_error and related tests.
  - Evidence from code:
    - test_module_level_skip_error (lines 1331–1345) creates a module-level pytest.skip call (without allow_module_level) and asserts the output matches "*Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed*".
    - test_module_level_skip_with_allow_module_level (lines 1348–1361) verifies pytest.skip(..., allow_module_level=True) leads to SKIP output.
  - Conclusion: Tests validate both the discouraging message and the allowed behavior when allow_module_level=True; the test for the message uses a wildcard, so any message that preserves that leading fragment will continue to match.

2) Verify the RootCause claims against the actual code

- The code supports module-level skipping at the exception level:
  - outcomes.skip signature and raise confirm allow_module_level exists (outcomes.py lines 123–145).
  - importorskip uses allow_module_level=True (outcomes.py lines 217 and 230).
- Module._importtestmodule honors the flag:
  - It checks e.allow_module_level and only raises the CollectError when it is False (python.py lines 607–615).
- The user-facing CollectError message indeed contains a discouraging phrase and does not mention the allow_module_level escape hatch:
  - The exact message is in python.py lines 610–614 and matches the draft's quoted text.
- Therefore the stated root cause in the draft — an inconsistent/misleading error message that discourages module-level skip while the flag exists — is supported by code.

3) Unsupported or overstated claims in the draft

- The draft suggests adding a convenience helper like skip_module(...) as a proposed change. That is a proposal (fix) — allowed in investigation but is a code change suggestion. The report must remain a diagnosis and should not prescribe fixes. I will remove prescriptive wording from the final RootCause/Description and keep the diagnosis that there is an ergonomics gap (i.e., no obvious public helper).
- The draft's RootCause item 5 mentions that tests assert the original message and that "Any change to the message must preserve the original leading fragment or the tests must be updated." This is an operational constraint about test coverage, not a root cause; it belongs in evidence/constraints. I will move that into the evidence section, not RootCause.

4) Important findings from the investigation analysis that the draft missed or under-emphasized

- Public availability / export: The draft proposes adding a helper but did not check whether the public pytest package re-exports a new symbol; the investigation noted this gap but the draft didn't call it out explicitly. This is not strictly a root cause but is an implementation consideration; I include a note in evidence about the missing re-export check.
- Multiple tests and other places already use pytest.skip(..., allow_module_level=True) (testing files I found show usage). This supports the claim that the feature is in use and the message inconsistency is primarily UX/documentation-related.

5) Completeness and impact check: are all locations affected by same root cause pattern included?

- The pattern is "message discourages module-level skip while the code supports it via allow_module_level".
- Locations that embody this pattern:
  - src/_pytest/python.py: Module._importtestmodule — raises discouraging message (primary location).
  - src/_pytest/outcomes.py: skip function — defines allow_module_level flag (supporting, the capability the message hides).
  - src/_pytest/outcomes.py: importorskip — already uses allow_module_level=True (shows existing usage).
  - testing/test_skipping.py — tests assert the discouraging message and also test allowed behavior (relevant constraint).
- The draft already covered most of these; I ensure they all appear in the RootCause field with equal weight.

6) Field discipline check — what to change to keep each field focused

- Description: keep a concise statement of the observed problem (misleading error message hides escape hatch and confuses users).
- RootCause: list only incorrect assumptions or omissions and the precise locations that encode them. Do not include fix proposals here. Also include the missing ergonomic helper as a diagnosis (absence of a user-friendly API).
- ExpectedBehavior: describe user-visible correct behavior (clear message or discoverable API).
- ObservedBehavior: keep to what users see, i.e., the CollectError message and the resulting confusion.
- StepsToReproduce: keep as a minimal reproducible sequence.

7) Additional verification steps I ran (searches)

- I searched the codebase for allow_module_level to find all usages (search results):
  - occurrences in outcomes.py, python.py, and several tests (testing/test_*). This confirms both the capability and that tests exist validating current behavior.

Summary of verification outcome

- The draft report's main diagnostic claim is correct and supported: the code supports module-level skips via an allow_module_level flag on the Skipped exception (outcomes.py), and Module._importtestmodule will accept Skipped when allow_module_level is True (python.py), but the CollectError message raised when allow_module_level is False is discouraging and does not mention the escape hatch (python.py). Tests assert the existing message, which constrains how the message can be changed without updating tests.
- The draft included a proposed fix (add skip_module helper and change message). Because this review's goal is to produce a pure diagnosis, I removed fix prescriptions from the report; however I preserved the diagnosis that there is an ergonomics gap (no built-in skip_module helper).
- I included all secondary findings that share the same root cause pattern (the message vs capability mismatch), and ensured each finding is presented with equal weight and a code location.

bug_report.Title: Misleading CollectError: pytest discourages module-level pytest.skip and hides allow_module_level escape hatch
bug_report.Description: When pytest encounters a pytest.skip() raised during module import it raises a CollectError with a message that forbids module-level use and suggests pytestmark-based skipping, but that message does not mention the supported escape hatch (allow_module_level=True). This misleads users who try to skip whole modules (for example modules that use newer Python syntax) and results in collection errors instead of a clean module-level skip.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) User-facing guidance in src/_pytest/python.py (Module._importtestmodule) is inconsistent with the skip outcome API. Module._importtestmodule catches skip.Exception and, when the caught exception's allow_module_level attribute is False, raises a CollectError with the message: "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed. To decorate a test function, use the @pytest.mark.skip or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators instead, and to skip a module use `pytestmark = pytest.mark.{skip,skipif}." (src/_pytest/python.py: lines ~607-615). This message does not mention the existing allow_module_level escape hatch.

2) The outcomes API provides the module-level allowance flag: src/_pytest/outcomes.py defines skip(msg: str = "", *, allow_module_level: bool = False) and raises Skipped(msg=msg, allow_module_level=allow_module_level) (src/_pytest/outcomes.py: lines ~123-145). The existence of this parameter encodes the supported capability that the Module._importtestmodule message omits.

3) Module import handling already respects the skip exception flag: when a Skipped is raised with allow_module_level=True, Module._importtestmodule re-raises the Skipped exception (allowing collection to treat the module as skipped); only when allow_module_level is False does the method raise the CollectError (src/_pytest/python.py: lines ~607-610). The behavioral inconsistency is therefore in the user-facing message, not in the exception/collection handling logic.

4) There is an ergonomics gap: there is no small, obvious public helper in the current outcomes API whose name signals "skip this entire module" (i.e., no dedicated skip_module helper is present in the codebase). While the underlying flag exists, its discoverability and the messaging around top-level skip are insufficient and lead to user confusion (absence observed in src/_pytest/outcomes.py and the public usage patterns in tests).
StepsToReproduce: Create a module pos_only.py that uses Python 3.8-only syntax, e.g. def foo(a, /, b): return a + b

Create a test file test_pos.py with a module-level conditional skip without allow_module_level, e.g.:

import sys
from pytest import skip
if sys.version_info < (3, 8):
    skip(msg='Requires Python >= 3.8')

from pos_only import *

def test_foo():
    assert foo(10, 20) == 30
Run pytest with Python 3.7 (or any <3.8): pytest -q
Observe that collection fails with a CollectError containing the message discouraging module-level pytest.skip
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: User-visible guidance should make it clear that module-level skipping is supported via the existing Skipped.allow_module_level mechanism: when a module intentionally raises pytest.skip(..., allow_module_level=True) during import, collection should record the module as skipped and not fail on module import (e.g. due to newer Python syntax). The user-facing messaging should not incorrectly discourage the module-level use without mentioning the escape hatch or the documented way to allow module-level skips.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Pytest raises a CollectError during module import with the message: "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed. To decorate a test function, use the @pytest.mark.skip or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators instead, and to skip a module use `pytestmark = pytest.mark.{skip,skipif}." This message discourages module-level pytest.skip and does not mention the existing allow_module_level=True behavior; as a result users attempting to skip modules that require newer Python syntax see collection errors instead of a clean module-level skip.

def test_findsource(monkeypatch) -> None:
    from _pytest._code.source import findsource

    filename = "<pytest-test_findsource>"
    lines = ["if 1:\n", "    def x():\n", "          pass\n"]
    co = compile("".join(lines), filename, "exec")

    # Type ignored because linecache.cache is private.
    monkeypatch.setitem(linecache.cache, filename, (1, None, lines, filename))  # type: ignore[attr-defined]

    src, lineno = findsource(co)
    assert src is not None
    assert "if 1:" in str(src)

    d: Dict[str, Any] = {}
    eval(co, d)
    src, lineno = findsource(d["x"])
    assert src is not None
    assert "if 1:" in str(src)
    assert src[lineno] == "    def x():"


def test_getfslineno() -> None:

    assert lineno == f.__code__.co_firstlineno - 1  # see findsource

    class A:

def getstatement(lineno: int, source) -> Source:
    from _pytest._code.source import getstatementrange_ast

    src = Source(source)
    ast, start, end = getstatementrange_ast(lineno, src)
    return src[start:end]

    source = '''def test():
    "comment 1"
    x = 1
      # comment 2
    # comment 3

    assert False

"""
comment 4
"""
'''
    for line in range(2, 6):
        assert str(getstatement(line, source)) == "    x = 1"
    if sys.version_info >= (3, 8) or hasattr(sys, "pypy_version_info"):
        tqs_start = 8
    else:
        tqs_start = 10
        assert str(getstatement(10, source)) == '"""'
    for line in range(6, tqs_start):
        assert str(getstatement(line, source)) == "    assert False"
    for line in range(tqs_start, 10):
        assert str(getstatement(line, source)) == '"""\ncomment 4\n"""'
